doc_description: A USDA Rural Development HB-2-3550 handbook table of contents and regulatory excerpts covering Section 502/504 single-family housing loan origination and servicing, payment subsidies, income verification, recapture, foreclosure, debt settlement, and REO property disposition.

structure.title: CHAPTER 1: OVERVIEW
structure.summary: This partial document is a table of contents for the HB-2-3550 Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It introduces the handbook’s purpose and organization, explains how to use it, and defines handbook symbols, citations, text boxes, attachments, appendices, and terminology. It also provides an overview of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program, including the goals of the program, Section 502 program and nonprogram loans, Section 504 grants and loans, and the role of field offices. In addition, it covers automation systems used for loan origination and servicing, specifically the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, UniFi, and LoanServ. The section on general program requirements begins with civil rights topics, including nondiscrimination and reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK

It also summarizes the program itself, covering the goals of the Direct Single Family Housing Program, Section 502 loans and nonprogram loans, Section 504 grants and loans, and the role of field offices. In addition, it includes a section on automation, specifically the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system and its components UniFi and LoanServ. The final listed topic addresses general program requirements, focusing on civil rights, nondiscrimination, and reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities.

nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be a table of contents from USDA HB-2-3550, outlining key topics in loan servicing and borrower review procedures. It covers administrative review and appeal rights, including adverse decisions, responsibilities of agency staff, mediation, NAD hearing preparation, conflict of interest, and other federal requirements. It also introduces Chapter 2 on regular servicing, with sections on onboarding new borrowers, setting up loans in the servicing system, borrower communication, payment frequency and methods, acceptable forms of payment, lockbox processing, and handling irregular payments such as partial, excess, or payments on closed accounts.

nodes.title: Appeals and Hearings
nodes.summary: This partial document is a table of contents for 7 CFR Part 11, which sets out the National Appeals Division Rules of Procedure. It lists the main procedural topics covered in the part, including definitions, general statements, applicability, exceptions from other laws and regulations, informal review of adverse decisions, Director review of appealability and hearing rights, rules on ex parte communications, Division hearings, review of hearing officer determinations, bases for determinations, reconsideration of Director decisions, the effective date and implementation of final determinations, and judicial review.

nodes.title: Table of Contents
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a table of contents and opening section of 7 CFR Part 11, which sets out the National Appeals Division Rules of Procedure. It lists the main regulatory topics covered, including definitions, applicability, informal review of adverse decisions, appealability and hearing rights, ex parte communications, division hearings, review of hearing officer determinations, standards for determinations, reconsideration, implementation of final decisions, judicial review, and filing deadlines.

It then begins § 11.1 by defining key terms. In particular, it explains what counts as an “adverse decision,” including denials of relief or agency inaction within required time limits, and notes exclusions such as matters under the Board of Contract Appeals. It also defines “agency” by listing the USDA agencies covered under these procedures, such as ASCS, CCC, FSA, FmHA, FCIC, NRCS, RBS, RDA, RHS, and RUS, with an exception for certain rural electrification and telephone bank programs.

nodes.title: Definitions
nodes.summary: The partial document defines key terms used in USDA National Appeals Division proceedings and begins the general statement of the appeals part. It explains what counts as an adverse decision, identifies the agencies covered, and defines core process terms such as appeal, appellant, authorized representative, agency record, case record, hearing, hearing officer, record review, and equitable relief. It also clarifies which matters are excluded from this appeals process, such as contract disputes, FOIA requests, personnel issues, tort claims, and discrimination complaints. The document then states that the National Appeals Division is an independent organization within the Department of Agriculture, under the Secretary’s general supervision, and governs procedures for appeals before that Division.

nodes.title: General statement
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines key exclusions and definitions related to USDA National Appeals Division procedures, then explains the scope and rules for appeals. It lists several types of disputes that fall under this framework, including suspension and debarment issues, export program disputes, crop insurance disputes, tenant grievances, personnel and EEO matters, certain claims under the Federal Tort Claims Act and Military Personnel and Civilian Employees Claims Act, and discrimination complaints. It defines “record review” as an appeal decided based on the agency record plus submissions from the appellant and agency, and identifies the Secretary as the Secretary of Agriculture.

The document then states that these rules govern proceedings before the National Appeals Division, an independent organization within USDA that is supervised by the Secretary. It explains that program participants generally must use this administrative appeal process before seeking judicial review. Finally, it describes the kinds of agency adverse decisions covered by the rules, such as denials of program participation or benefits, compliance determinations, payment disputes, and wetland or highly erodible land findings, while noting that the process cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations. It also specifies that certain general legal procedures and rules, including the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence, do not apply to these proceedings.

nodes.title: Applicability
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains the administrative appeal framework for USDA-related programs. It states that program participants must first seek review of an adverse decision before a Hearing Officer, and may then appeal to the Director before going to court. It defines what types of decisions can be appealed, including denial of program participation or benefits, compliance determinations, payment disputes, and wetland or highly erodible land classifications. It also makes clear that the procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations themselves. Finally, it specifies that certain general legal rules do not apply to these proceedings, including the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: Inapplicability of other laws and regulations
nodes.summary: This partial document describes the USDA administrative appeal process for adverse decisions. It explains that program participants must first seek review before a Hearing Officer and may then seek further review by the Director before going to court. It outlines the types of agency decisions covered, such as denial of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land classifications. It also states that the procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations themselves. Finally, it notes that certain general legal frameworks do not apply to these proceedings, including the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: Informal review of adverse decisions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for informal review of adverse agency decisions before appealing to NAD. It states that for FSA field office decisions, a participant must first seek mandatory informal review through the responsible county or area committee, with possible further review by the State FSA committee or direct appeal to NAD. For adverse decisions at the FSA State office level and for other agencies, informal review is optional before appealing. The section also describes the right to use mediation or other alternative dispute resolution programs, and explains how requesting mediation affects appeal deadlines and hearing timing.

nodes.title: Director review of agency determination of appealability and right of participants to Division hearing
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for appeal review within the Division. It covers how a participant can request Director review of an agency’s determination that a decision is not appealable, the Director’s authority to decide whether a matter is appealable or of general applicability, and the finality of that decision. It also describes the process and deadlines for appealing adverse decisions, including required contents of a written hearing request and rules for requests by authorized representatives. Finally, it sets out restrictions on ex parte communications, prohibiting private merit-based communications with Division officers or employees during an appeal, with limited exceptions for procedural matters or discussions involving all parties.

nodes.title: Ex parte communications
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains the procedure for requesting a hearing or record review, including the requirement that the request be written and personally signed, and that it include the adverse decision and a brief explanation of why it is believed to be wrong. It also describes how an authorized representative must prove written authorization to act for the participant. 

The section then outlines rules against ex parte communications during the appeal process, prohibiting off-the-record merit discussions with interested persons and requiring that any improper communications be placed in the hearing record. It also gives the Hearing Officer or Director authority to respond to violations, including possibly dismissing or adversely affecting a party’s claim.

Finally, it covers general hearing procedures, stating that the appellant, Director, and Hearing Officer may access the agency record, that the agency must provide a copy upon request, and that subpoenas may be issued for witnesses or evidence under specified timing and approval rules.

nodes.title: Division hearings
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for Division hearings and review of adverse decisions. It covers rules on ex parte communications and how prohibited communications must be placed in the record, along with possible sanctions for violations. It then explains hearing procedures, including access to agency records, subpoena authority, service and enforcement of subpoenas, and the handling of witness costs. The document details the process for both record reviews and formal hearings: assignment of a Hearing Officer, required pre-hearing submissions from the appellant and agency, notice requirements, pre-hearing conferences, conduct of hearings, recording of proceedings, handling party absences, post-hearing submissions, burden of proof, and timing for issuing determinations. Finally, it begins the section on Director review, describing how appellants and agencies may request review of a Hearing Officer’s determination, deadlines for requests, notice to other parties, and the opportunity for responses.

nodes.title: Director review of determinations of Hearing Officers
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedure for appealing a Hearing Officer’s determination to the Director and the Director’s review powers. It sets deadlines for when an appellant or agency may request review, requires written requests with specific reasons, and provides that copies must be sent to all parties. It also describes how other parties may respond, how the Director reviews the record and may uphold, reverse, modify, or remand the case, and the time limits for completing that review. Finally, it notes that the Director may delegate review authority and may grant equitable relief, with the Director’s decision being final and not appealable.

nodes.title: Basis for determinations
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules governing how Hearing Officers and the Director make appeal determinations and how those determinations can be reconsidered. It states that decision-makers are not bound by prior factual findings from the agency’s adverse decision, but must ensure their decisions align with applicable laws, regulations, and official interpretations. Determinations must be based on the case record and the laws and regulations in effect at the relevant time. The document also outlines the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s determinations, including a 10-day filing deadline, the need to show a material factual error or legal inconsistency, notice and response procedures for other parties, and a 5-day timeline for the Director to issue a reconsideration decision. If the reconsideration changes the original determination, the reconsidered decision becomes the final determination.

nodes.title: Reconsideration of Director determinations
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how appeal determinations are made and how they can be reconsidered. It states that Hearing Officers and the Director are not required to follow prior factual findings, but their decisions must align with agency laws, regulations, and general interpretations of those rules. Determinations must be based on the case record and on the laws and regulations applicable at the relevant time.

It also describes the process for requesting reconsideration of a Director’s determination. Either the appellant or the agency may request reconsideration within 10 days, but only if they identify a material factual error or show that the decision conflicts with law or regulation. The Director must notify all parties whether the request meets the requirements, allow non-requesting parties 5 days to respond, and then issue a reconsideration decision within 5 days after receiving responses. If the reconsideration changes the result, that new decision becomes the final Director determination.

nodes.title: Effective date and implementation of final determinations of the Division
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures and rules for final determinations, appeals, and filing deadlines within a Division’s review process. It explains when a final determination becomes effective and how quickly an agency must implement it, describes the availability of judicial review in federal district court and the requirement to obtain a final Division determination before seeking review, and sets out filing rules including what counts as a filed document, how deadlines are extended when they fall on non-business days, and when filing time expires on the last day.

nodes.title: Judicial review
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers three procedural rules for handling appeals and final determinations by the Division:

- **Effective date and implementation of final determinations (§ 11.12):** It explains when a final determination becomes effective and requires the agency head to implement it within 30 days after the effective date.
- **Judicial review (§ 11.13):** It states that Division final determinations can be reviewed and enforced by a U.S. District Court, but only after the appellant has received a final determination from the Division.
- **Filing of appeals and time computation (§ 11.14):** It defines when a document is considered filed, how deadlines are extended if they fall on weekends or federal holidays, and that filings generally must be completed by 5:00 p.m. local time on the last day.

nodes.title: Filing of appeals and computation of time
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers procedures and rules for agency appeal determinations and appraisal guidelines. It explains when and how final determinations must be implemented by an agency, how the effective date of a final determination is established, and the right to judicial review in U.S. District Court. It also describes rules for filing appeals and calculating filing deadlines, including what counts as “filed” and how weekends, holidays, and office closing times affect timing.

In the appraisal guidelines section, the document explains when the Servicing Office may require an appraisal for special servicing actions, such as determining subsidy recapture, releasing part of collateral, evaluating a deed-in-lieu, deciding whether a lien has value, setting a foreclosure bid price, or supporting a protective advance. It also outlines basic appraisal concepts, including the factors appraisers consider and the main valuation methods used, especially the sales comparison approach.

structure.title: APPENDIX 5
structure.summary: This partial document is an appraisal guideline section from HB-2-3550, Appendix 5, focused on when and why appraisals are required in servicing actions and the basic methods used to determine property value. It explains that the Servicing Office may request Field Staff assistance to obtain appraisals for situations such as determining subsidy recapture, evaluating partial release of security property, deciding whether to accept a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, assessing whether a lien has value, setting a foreclosure bid price, or judging whether a protective advance is supported by the property’s value. It also introduces appraisal fundamentals, noting that appraisers consider factors like location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities, and typically use the sales comparison approach and cost approach, with a broker’s price opinion sometimes permitted under certain servicing actions.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL GUIDELINES [7 CFR 3550.62]
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction to directly return a description of the document’s main points, without including any extra text.

nodes.title: SERVICING ACTIONS REQUIRING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: This partial document provides appraisal guidelines for servicing actions under HB-2-3550. It explains when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal to determine a property’s market value, including for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien valuation, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. It also outlines basic appraisal concepts, noting that appraisers consider factors such as location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities. The document introduces the main valuation methods used: the sales comparison approach, the cost approach, and in some cases a broker’s price opinion, and briefly explains that the sales comparison approach estimates value by comparing the property to similar recent sales and adjusting for differences.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL BASICS
nodes.summary: The partial document is an appraisal guideline appendix for servicing actions that require determining a property’s market value. It explains when the Servicing Office must request an appraisal, such as for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien value, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. It outlines basic appraisal methods, including the sales comparison approach, cost approach, and, in limited cases, a broker’s price opinion (BPO). The document also distinguishes between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value, noting that “as is” value is used for most servicing actions. It describes how appraisals may be ordered either through Field Staff or contract appraisers, with timing requirements for completion. In addition, it introduces due diligence requirements related to environmental review and provides special rules for appraisals in remote rural areas or Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be unavailable and a different appraisal form and staff qualifications are required.

nodes.title: APPRAISAL ORDERS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers appraisal methods and procedures used to determine property value for servicing actions. It explains the cost approach and BPO (broker price opinion), and notes that appraisers may provide either an “as is” value or an “as developed/improved” value depending on the loan purpose. It also describes how appraisals are ordered, including use of Field Staff or contracted local appraisers, along with timeframes for completion. The document then introduces due diligence requirements related to environmental condition checks when market value is being determined. Finally, it discusses special appraisal rules for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where comparable sales are often unavailable, requiring use of Form 1007 and Agency staff appraisers, and it defines what qualifies as a remote rural area.

nodes.title: DUE DILIGENCE
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains appraisal-related procedures for loan servicing, including how property value is estimated and reviewed. It covers the cost approach and broker price opinions (BPOs), distinguishing between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value. It describes how appraisals are ordered by the Servicing Office, including timelines for Field Staff and contract appraisers. The document also outlines due diligence requirements, especially environmental inquiry using the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and referral to an Environmental Coordinator if concerns arise.

In addition, it discusses special appraisal rules for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be unavailable; in those cases, the sales comparison approach is not required and a Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal form must be used by Agency staff. Finally, it details appraisal review procedures, including administrative and technical reviews, what each review checks, required timeframes, and the need to redo an appraisal if it is found unacceptable.

nodes.title: REVIEWING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It states that contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products must be checked through the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528), and any concerns should be referred to an Environmental Coordinator. It then describes how the Servicing Office reviews appraisals to determine property value, including administrative and technical reviews. Administrative reviews check completeness, math, comparable sales, and proper valuation methods and must be completed within 7 days. Technical reviews assess whether the appraisal is well-supported and are required for the first appraisal by a contract or fee appraiser, with additional random or problem-based reviews possible. If an appraisal is unacceptable, a new one must be obtained before servicing can continue.

nodes.title: Administrative Review
nodes.summary:
The document section discusses environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It explains that due diligence is used to check for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products and to assess how contamination could affect property value, beginning with the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528). If concerns are identified, the matter is referred to an Environmental Coordinator.

It then outlines appraisal review requirements for the Servicing Office. Appraisals must be reviewed for accuracy through administrative reviews and random spot-checks, and an unacceptable appraisal requires a new one before the servicing action continues. Administrative reviews verify completeness, math accuracy, current comparables, and use of both cost and comparable sales approaches, and must be completed within 7 days. Technical reviews examine whether the appraisal is complete and well supported, are required for the first appraisal by any contract or fee appraiser, and may also be used for spot checks or when administrative reviews reveal serious problems.

nodes.title: Technical Review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the due diligence process for checking properties for hazardous contamination, including use of the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and referral to an Environmental Coordinator if concerns arise. It then outlines how appraisals are reviewed during servicing actions: an administrative review for completeness and accuracy, a technical review for appraisal quality and support, and entering acceptable appraisal results into MortgageServ. Finally, it states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, which is billed to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: Entering the Appraisal Results
nodes.summary: The document explains that after an appraisal review is completed and the appraisal is found acceptable, the Servicing Office should enter the appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value to decide on the proposed servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, and that this fee is added to the borrower’s account.

It covers two main areas: first, how appraisal results are handled in loan servicing, including entering an acceptable appraisal value into MortgageServ and using that value to decide on the servicing action; and second, the policy for paying appraisal fees, stating that borrowers are charged $280 for each servicing action requiring an appraisal and that the fee is added to the borrower’s account. The document then begins Chapter 1, which introduces the handbook’s purpose, audience, and use. It explains that the handbook provides Agency staff with tools for effective loan servicing, focuses on Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, and is intended to comply with applicable laws and regulations. It also describes how the handbook is organized for easy reference and introduces handbook symbols, such as the reference symbol that points readers to additional sources of information.

nodes.title: GOALS OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and basic structure of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, especially the Section 502 direct loan program. It explains that the program helps low- and very low-income people obtain adequate, modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas. The document emphasizes three main agency goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining processing to reduce administrative burden and improve borrower benefits.

It also gives an overview of Section 502 program loans, describing their purpose to help eligible individuals acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. The section notes typical loan terms, possible extensions, how loan interest rates are set, and the availability of payment subsidies. Finally, it begins listing eligibility requirements, focusing on income limits for applicants and distinguishing between standard loans, assumed loans, and loans for REO properties.

nodes.title: SECTION 502 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and overview of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program, focusing on Section 502 direct loans. It explains that the program is meant to help low- and very low-income people obtain decent, safe, and sanitary housing in rural areas. It also emphasizes three main agency goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining loan processing.

The document then introduces Section 502 loans, describing their purpose as helping eligible applicants acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. It notes standard loan terms, possible exceptions, note rates set by the Agency, and the availability of payment subsidies for some borrowers. Finally, it begins listing basic eligibility requirements, including income limits for applicants depending on the type of loan or property involved.

nodes.title: Program Loans
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, focusing on its goals and two major loan/grant programs.

Main points covered:
- The overall purpose of the program is to help low- and very low-income people obtain adequate, modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas.
- The Agency’s goals include:
  - providing customer-friendly service,
  - using public funds efficiently,
  - and streamlining loan processing to reduce administrative burden and improve benefits to borrowers.
- Section 502 direct loans are described as a program for very low- and low-income applicants to buy, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas.
  - It explains loan terms, interest rate setting, and the possible availability of payment subsidies.
  - It lists basic eligibility requirements such as income limits, inability to get credit elsewhere, occupancy, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity, and willingness and ability to repay.
- Nonprogram loans are also discussed as special credit arrangements used in limited situations, such as loan assumptions or REO property purchases by ineligible borrowers, or other government-interest cases.
- Section 504 is introduced as a direct loan and grant program for homeowners who need help repairing homes or correcting health and safety hazards.
  - Eligibility is limited to very low-income applicants and includes requirements similar to Section 502.
  - Section 504 grants are specifically for older homeowners age 62 and over who cannot obtain loans.
  - Grant funds may be used to remove health and safety hazards or make homes accessible for people with disabilities, with a lifetime grant limit of $7,500.

nodes.title: Nonprogram Loans
nodes.summary: The passage outlines key eligibility and purpose details for USDA housing loan programs. It explains Section 502 loan requirements, including inability to obtain other credit, intent to occupy the home, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to take on the loan, and willingness and ability to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as assuming an existing loan, buying REO property, handling unauthorized assistance, or continuing a loan after partial release of security, and notes these loans are only made when in the government’s best interest. The document then summarizes Section 504, a direct loan and grant program for very low-income homeowners who cannot get other credit and need help repairing homes or addressing health and safety hazards. Finally, it details Section 504 grants, which are limited to eligible applicants age 62 or older and can be used for repairs, accessibility improvements, or hazard removal, with a lifetime maximum grant amount of $7,500.

nodes.title: SECTION 504 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The document section provides an overview of USDA housing loan programs. It summarizes Section 502 loans, including borrower eligibility requirements such as inability to obtain credit elsewhere, intent to occupy the home, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to incur debt, and willingness and ability to repay. It also explains nonprogram loans, which may be used in special situations like assuming an existing loan, purchasing an REO property, or continuing a loan after a partial release of security, and are limited to cases in the Government’s best interest. The section then introduces Section 504, a direct loan and grant program for very low-income homeowners who need help repairing homes or correcting health and safety hazards. It highlights that Section 504 grants are specifically for eligible homeowners age 62 or older and can be used for hazard removal or accessibility-related repairs, with a lifetime maximum grant amount of $7,500.

nodes.title: Section 504 Grants
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of USDA housing loan programs and field office responsibilities. It explains eligibility and basic requirements for Section 502 loans, including inability to obtain other credit, intent to occupy the home, citizenship/eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity, and willingness and ability to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as loan assumptions or REO purchases when not eligible for Section 502, and notes that these are only made when in the government’s best interest.

The document then summarizes Section 504 direct loans and grants, which help very low-income homeowners repair or rehabilitate homes and address health and safety hazards. It distinguishes between Section 504 grants for eligible homeowners age 62 or older, with a lifetime maximum of $7,500, and Section 504 loans, which can fund general repairs and hazards removal, have a 20-year term, a 1 percent interest rate, and a $20,000 maximum outstanding balance.

Finally, it outlines the role of Field Offices and Field Staff: loans are originated and closed in Field Offices, serviced afterward by the Servicing Office, and Field Staff assist when local presence is needed. Field Staff are also responsible for reporting issues affecting property security or borrower circumstances, such as hazardous conditions, abandonment, non-occupancy, environmental hazards, or income changes.

nodes.title: Section 504 Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Section 504 loans and the role of field offices. It states that Section 504 loan funds can be used for general property repairs, improvements, and removal of health and safety hazards. It also notes that borrowers may have multiple Section 504 loans, but the total outstanding balance cannot exceed $20,000, with a maximum term of 20 years and a 1 percent interest rate. The document then describes how loans are originated and closed in Field Offices, while servicing is handled by the Servicing Office after final disbursement. It further explains that Field Staff may assist the Servicing Office when local presence is needed and are responsible for reporting risks to the security property or changes in borrower circumstances, such as hazardous conditions, environmental hazards, non-occupancy, abandonment, or income changes.

nodes.title: THE ROLE OF FIELD OFFICES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Section 504 loans, including their permitted uses for repairs and hazard removal, loan limits, term, and interest rate. It also describes the role of Field Offices in originating, closing, and assisting with servicing actions, along with their duty to report borrower or property risks. In addition, it introduces the Agency’s DLOS system and its two parts: UniFi for loan origination and LoanServ for loan servicing, recordkeeping, communication, and work queue coordination between Field Offices and the Servicing Office.

structure.title: SECTION 3: AUTOMATION
structure.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to speed up loan processing, standardize records, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two linked components of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant data and handles application activities through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to track loan servicing actions and loan performance. The document also notes how LoanServ supports coordination between field and servicing staff through shared access and work queues, helping them exchange information and assist with servicing tasks.

nodes.title: THE DEDICATED LOAN ORIGINATION AND SERVICING (DLOS) SYSTEM
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to streamline loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two main components of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant data and tracks applications through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage loans, record servicing actions, and monitor loan performance. The document also highlights how LoanServ supports coordination between staff through shared access and work queues, and notes that consistent use of LoanServ allows multiple servicing operators to work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: UniFi
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to streamline loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected parts of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant data and tracks the application from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage and monitor loan performance. The text also highlights how LoanServ supports coordination between field and servicing staff through work queues and shared access, allowing information and servicing actions to be exchanged efficiently.

nodes.title: LoanServ
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the Agency’s automation system for direct single-family loans, focusing on the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system and its two components: UniFi and LoanServ. It explains that UniFi is a web-based loan origination tool used to collect applicant information, perform calculations, and maintain application records through loan closing, while LoanServ is a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage loan performance and record servicing office actions. The document also notes how the two systems work together to share information between Field Offices and the Servicing Office, including use of work queues for coordination. In addition, the excerpt introduces general program requirements related to civil rights, stating that the Agency administers its programs in accordance with equal opportunity and fair housing laws, and lists major federal civil rights statutes and executive orders that apply to the program.

structure.title: SECTION 4: GENERAL PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the civil rights requirements for the Agency’s programs, emphasizing that they must be administered fairly and in compliance with equal opportunity and fair housing laws, as well as relevant Executive Orders. It references RD Instruction 1901-E for the detailed compliance rules and provides a list of major federal civil rights laws affecting the Direct Single Family Loan Program. These include protections against discrimination based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, disability, familial status, income from public assistance, and exercise of consumer protection rights. The exhibit highlights key laws such as the Equal Credit Opportunity Act, Title VI, the Fair Housing Act, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, the Age Discrimination Act, and several Executive Orders addressing housing discrimination, equal employment opportunity, and access for individuals with limited English proficiency.

nodes.title: CIVIL RIGHTS [7 CFR 3550.3]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the civil rights requirements for the USDA direct single-family loan program. It says the Agency must administer programs fairly and in compliance with equal opportunity, fair housing laws, and related Executive Orders. It lists the major civil rights laws and protections involved, including bans on discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin, disability, age, familial status, marital status, source of income, and exercise of consumer credit rights, as well as requirements for accessible services and equal employment opportunity.

The document also outlines nondiscrimination expectations for loan and servicing activities under Sections 502 and 504. It emphasizes that all borrowers must be treated consistently and fairly in outreach, eligibility determinations, and exceptions. It notes that even unintentional poor program administration can create civil rights problems. Finally, it highlights key issues for loan servicers such as access, fairness, disparate impacts, and record keeping.

nodes.title: Nondiscrimination
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers civil rights requirements and appeals procedures for USDA Section 502 and 504 loan programs. It explains nondiscrimination rules, emphasizing that loans, grants, services, and benefits cannot be denied based on protected characteristics such as race, color, national origin, sex, religion, marital status, familial status, age, disability, source of income, or exercising rights under consumer credit laws. It also highlights the importance of consistent servicing practices in outreach, eligibility verification, exceptions, subsidies, and hearings or appeals to avoid unfair treatment or disparate impacts.

The document then discusses reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, stating that the Agency must provide equal access through physical accessibility, effective communication, or process modifications when needed.

Finally, it introduces review and appeals procedures, defining adverse decisions, requiring them to be based on applicable regulations rather than handbook guidance, and noting that the National Appeals Division rules apply when decisions are appealed. It also mentions consultation between servicing and field offices and the use of standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: Reasonable Accommodations for Persons with Disabilities [7 CFR 15e.130]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers civil rights requirements and appeals procedures for the HB-2-3550 handbook. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing remedies, hearings, and appeals must be made available consistently. It also outlines the Agency’s duty to provide reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, including accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and process modifications to ensure equal access to programs. In addition, it describes review and appeals rules for adverse decisions, defining what counts as an adverse decision, emphasizing that such decisions must be based on published regulations, and noting the role of the National Appeals Division and the use of standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: REVIEW AND APPEALS [7 CFR 3550.4]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers civil rights requirements and program review procedures for USDA housing assistance. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing, hearings, and appeals must be provided consistently. It also describes reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, including accessible facilities, effective communication, and process modifications to ensure equal access to Agency programs. Finally, it outlines adverse decisions under the review and appeals process, noting that such decisions must be based on published regulations, follow National Appeals Division procedures, and use standardized adverse decision letters when applicable.

nodes.title: Adverse Decisions
nodes.summary:
The document section outlines civil rights requirements and the agency’s obligations to provide equal access and fair treatment in its programs. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing remedies, hearings, and appeals must be available consistently. It then details reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, including physical access, effective communication, outreach tools, and process modifications to ensure equal participation.

The section also covers review and appeals procedures for adverse decisions in the Rural Development single-family housing program. It defines adverse decisions, states they must be based on federal regulations rather than handbook guidance, and describes the role of the National Appeals Division in appeals. A sample adverse decision letter is included, showing how denials should explain the reasons and cite regulations.

Finally, the document describes required contents of adverse decision notices, including information on informal administrative reviews, mediation or alternative dispute resolution, appeal rights, and civil rights language. It assigns responsibilities to State Directors and ADR Coordinators for issuing state guidance, maintaining mediator lists, verifying mediation requests, and managing the mediation selection process.

nodes.title: State Director Responsibilities
nodes.summary: The excerpt describes procedures for handling adverse decisions, including required notice contents about informal administrative meetings, mediation/ADR, appeal rights to NAD, and civil rights information. It explains the use of specific attachments for participant notices, and when those attachments are not needed if another USDA document already includes the correct rights language. It also outlines State Director responsibilities to issue state supplements with mediation and appeal guidance, including identifying state-specific mediation providers and an ADR Coordinator. Finally, it details ADR Coordinator duties such as maintaining an approved list of mediation providers, ensuring referrals are made equitably, distinguishing mediators from arbitrators, and verifying mediation requests are timely before issuing the appropriate attachments to participants.

nodes.title: ADR Coordinator Responsibilities
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the procedures and responsibilities related to adverse decisions, reviews, appeals, mediation, and civil rights notices in the USDA Rural Development housing program context. It covers:

- What information must be included in adverse decision letters, including informal review, mediation/ADR, NAD appeal rights, and civil rights language.
- The role of State Directors in issuing state-specific guidance for appeals and mediation, including identifying mediation providers and an ADR Coordinator.
- Responsibilities of the ADR Coordinator, such as maintaining approved mediator lists, verifying timely mediation requests, issuing the correct attachments, ensuring mediation occurs within required timeframes, and communicating documentation to all parties.
- Responsibilities of the Agency Representative (Appeal Coordinator), including preparing supporting documentation, representing the Agency in mediations and hearings, and implementing final mediation or appeal decisions.
- The beginning of the “Agency Decisions” section, introducing the right to an informal administrative review when a participant is adversely affected, and the requirement to notify the participant of that right within 10 days.

nodes.title: Agency Representative (Appeal Coordinator) Responsibilities
nodes.summary: The document section outlines procedures for reviews, appeals, mediation, and agency decision processes under HB-2-3550. It describes the responsibilities of the ADR Coordinator, including initiating mediation, ensuring it occurs within 45 days, notifying participants if mediation fails, and distributing mediation documentation. It also explains the role of the Agency Representative (Appeal Coordinator), who must provide supporting documentation, represent the agency in mediations and hearings, and implement final decisions within 30 days. The section then begins discussing agency decisions, specifically the right of a program participant to request an informal administrative review after receiving an adverse decision, and the requirement that notice of this right be provided within 10 days.

nodes.title: AGENCY DECISIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Rural Development/Farm Service Agency procedures for agency decisions and participant appeal rights. It explains the difference between appealable and non-appealable decisions, the timelines for sending notice letters, and the forms used to inform participants of their rights. It also describes participant rights to request informal administrative review, mediation/ADR, or a hearing with the National Appeals Division, as well as the deadlines and order in which appeal options must be pursued.

nodes.title: Informal Administrative Review
nodes.summary: This partial document explains the USDA/Rural Housing program’s procedures for agency decisions, appeal rights, and participant review options. It distinguishes between appealable and non-appealable decisions, describes the notices provided to participants after adverse decisions, and outlines when and how participants can request mediation/ADR, a National Appeals Division hearing, or an informal administrative review. It also details participant rights, including the 30-day timeframe for requesting appeals, the order in which options should be used, the possibility of waiving earlier options by skipping them, and requirements for submitting written requests and conducting reviews or hearings.

nodes.title: Appealable Decisions
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains the Agency’s decision and appeal process for program participants. It distinguishes between appealable and non-appealable decisions, describes the required notices and forms sent after adverse decisions, and outlines the participant’s rights to request review options. It also notes timelines for requests, including the 30-day period to appeal or seek review, and explains that participants may pursue informal administrative review, mediation/ADR, a hearing with the National Appeals Division, or a record review, depending on the type of decision.

nodes.title: Non-Appealable Decisions
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA/Rural Housing Service procedures for agency decisions and participant appeal rights under HB-2-3550. It covers:  
- The difference between informal reviews, appealable decisions, and non-appealable decisions.  
- Required notice timelines and forms/attachments used to inform participants of adverse decisions.  
- Participant rights to request mediation/ADR, a National Appeals Division hearing, or an informal administrative review.  
- The 30-day deadline for participants to request appeal options after receiving an adverse action.  
- Rules for how appeal options may be requested and the order in which they are handled, including how waiving earlier options affects rights.  
- Details on informal administrative review, including that it must be requested in writing and kept in the case file, and how receipt dates are calculated if the letter date is unclear.

nodes.title: Participant Rights
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA/Rural Housing Service procedures for agency decisions and participant appeal rights. It explains the difference between appealable and non-appealable decisions, the notices that must be sent to participants, and the timing for those notices. It describes available review and appeal options, including informal administrative review, mediation/ADR, and hearings through the National Appeals Division. It also states that participants generally have 30 days to request appeal options, that requests may be made simultaneously but are handled in order, and that choosing later options can waive earlier ones. Finally, it details how to request an informal administrative review, including the requirement for a written request and the deadline calculation if the letter receipt date is unknown.

nodes.title: Informal Administrative Review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s process for handling adverse decisions and participant rights under the program. It distinguishes between appealable and non-appealable decisions, outlines the notices and attachments used to inform participants, and describes how informal administrative review works. It also explains participant appeal rights, including deadlines for requesting review, the option for face-to-face, teleconference, or record review hearings, and the order in which appeal options must be used. Finally, it covers mediation for appealable cases, including the purpose of mediation, time limits, possible costs and cost-sharing, how mediators are selected, and what happens if the participant does not timely choose a mediator.

nodes.title: Mediation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains participant rights under the USDA Rural Development program, focusing on how participants can challenge adverse decisions. It covers the informal administrative review process, including its purpose, timing, and notification of outcomes. It then describes mediation as an option for appealable decisions, outlining the mediation process, time limits, possible costs, funding arrangements, and the roles of the State Director and available mediation providers such as USDA-funded programs, community-based mediation centers, and alternative providers. Finally, it explains the formal appeal process to the National Appeals Division (NAD), including the 30-day filing deadline, required contents of an appeal request, and the Agency’s responsibility to provide case records and supporting information to NAD and the participant.

nodes.title: Appeal
nodes.summary: The partial document explains participant rights under USDA housing and loan program procedures, focusing on mediation and appeal options. It describes three mediation pathways: USDA-funded state mediation programs, community-based mediation centers, and alternative mediation service providers, including when each option is used. It then outlines the appeal process for adverse decisions, including the 30-day deadline to file with NAD, required contents of the appeal request, and the Agency’s obligation to submit the record and supporting information. The section also describes what happens after NAD’s final determination. Finally, it begins a section on NAD hearing preparation, listing what documents both the appellant and the Agency must submit and explaining that hearing notice must be provided at least 14 days in advance.

nodes.title: NAD Hearing Preparation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains participant rights and the National Appeals Division (NAD) hearing process. It covers how NAD notifies participants and the Agency of final determinations, what happens if the Agency decision is reversed, and where to find additional appeal guidance. It then outlines hearing preparation requirements, including what materials both the appellant and the Agency must submit, deadlines for those submissions, and the required hearing notice. The document also describes pre-hearing conferences, post-hearing procedures for adding new information to the record, timelines for issuing determinations, and the process for requesting Director review of reversed Agency decisions.

nodes.title: Pre-Hearing Conference
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for NAD hearing preparation and appeal processing. It covers where hearings may be held and the right to an official hearing record, the use of pre-hearing conferences to resolve or narrow disputes, post-hearing procedures for keeping the record open and submitting additional evidence, deadlines for issuing determinations after hearings or record reviews, and the process and timing for requesting Director review of adverse Agency decisions.

nodes.title: Post-Hearing Procedure
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for NAD hearing preparation and post-hearing actions. It explains where hearings may be held and the right to obtain an official record, then describes pre-hearing conferences, which are used to resolve or narrow issues and are usually held by telephone unless all parties agree otherwise. It also outlines post-hearing procedures, including the judge’s ability to keep the record open for additional submissions and the agency’s responsibility to respond to new evidence. The document specifies deadlines for issuing appeal determinations after hearings or record reviews. Finally, it details the process and timing for requesting a Director’s review of reversed agency decisions, including who may request it, the 15-business-day deadline, and the need to state reasons and legal citations.

nodes.title: Timing of Issuance of Determination
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for NAD hearing preparation and follow-up. It covers where hearings may be held and the right to an official hearing record, the use of pre-hearing conferences to resolve or narrow disputes, and post-hearing procedures for keeping the record open and handling new evidence. It also explains the timeline for issuing the Administrative Judge’s determination after a hearing or record review, and the process for requesting a Director review of unfavorable Agency decisions, including who may request it, the deadline, and the need to provide specific legal reasons.

nodes.title: Requests for a Director Review
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for NAD hearing preparation and appeals, including pre-hearing conferences, post-hearing submission of additional information, timelines for issuing determinations, and how both the Agency and appellant may request a Director review and reconsideration. It also introduces conflict-of-interest rules for Agency employees, emphasizing honesty, impartiality, and restrictions on employees handling cases involving relatives, associates, or close working relationships.

nodes.title: Conflict of Interest [7 CFR 3550.9]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers three main areas:

1. **NAD Hearing Preparation and Appeals Process**
   - Explains how a participant can request a Director review after an Administrative Judge’s decision.
   - Describes deadlines for submitting review requests and reconsideration requests.
   - States that the Director reviews whether the decision is supported by substantial evidence and can uphold, reverse, or modify it.
   - Notes that the Agency must implement the final determination within 30 days.

2. **Conflict of Interest Requirements**
   - Sets ethical standards for Agency employees, emphasizing honesty, integrity, and impartiality.
   - Describes restrictions on who may process, approve, service, or review cases to avoid conflicts of interest.
   - Requires disclosure of relationships by both borrowers and Agency employees.
   - Prohibits employees, their families, and certain related parties from purchasing REO properties or related foreclosure/sale properties.

3. **Other Federal Requirements: Environmental Requirements**
   - States that environmental considerations must be integrated into loan and grant decisions.
   - Assigns responsibility to both the Loan Originator and Loan Approval Official for applying environmental policies.
   - References related guidance on environmental, historic preservation, and lead-based paint requirements.
   - Says conflicts between Agency and State/local environmental rules must be resolved through consultation with environmental coordinators and, if needed, National Office staff.

nodes.title: Borrower Disclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics: conflict of interest rules and other federal requirements for Agency-assisted housing programs. It explains that borrowers and Agency employees must disclose any known relationships or associations, and it restricts certain people—such as Agency employees, their family members, and closing agents—from purchasing REO properties, foreclosure properties, or properties they were professionally involved with. It then outlines environmental requirements, stating that the Agency must consider environmental quality in decision-making, that staff must follow environmental and historic preservation procedures, that assisted properties must meet lead-based paint requirements, and that any conflicts with state or local environmental laws must be reviewed with the appropriate environmental officials.

nodes.title: Agency Employee Disclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key USDA Rural Development housing program requirements related to conflict of interest and environmental compliance. It explains that borrowers and Agency employees must disclose any relationships or associations that could create a conflict, and restricts certain purchases of REO properties, foreclosure sale properties, and properties involving professional involvement by employees or their families. It also summarizes broader federal environmental requirements, stating that environmental considerations must be integrated into loan and grant activities, and that agency-assisted properties must comply with historic preservation and lead-based paint rules, with consultation required when federal and state/local environmental rules conflict.

nodes.title: Disposition of REO Properties
nodes.summary: The partial document covers conflict of interest rules and other federal requirements for the Agency’s loan and property programs. It explains that borrowers must disclose any relationship with Agency employees, and Agency employees must disclose any relationship with borrowers. It also restricts Agency employees, their family members, and certain closing agents from purchasing REO properties, assumptions, or foreclosure-sale properties in which they are involved. In addition, the document outlines environmental requirements, stating that environmental and historic preservation considerations must be integrated into program decisions, that lead-based paint rules apply to Agency-assisted properties, and that conflicts between federal and state or local environmental laws must be resolved through consultation with the appropriate environmental coordinators.

nodes.title: Other Federal Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Development loan program requirements related to conflict of interest and other federal compliance rules. It explains that borrowers and Agency employees must disclose any known relationships or associations, and it prohibits certain purchases by Agency employees, family members, and involved closing agents involving REO properties, assumptions, or foreclosure sales. It also outlines environmental requirements, stating that environmental considerations must be integrated into loan and grant activities, that historic preservation and lead-based paint rules apply, and that conflicts between Agency and state or local environmental laws must be resolved through consultation with environmental staff.

nodes.title: Environmental Requirements [7 CFR 3550.5]
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key administrative and compliance requirements for the USDA Rural Development Single Family Housing program. It covers conflict of interest rules, including borrower and employee disclosure obligations and restrictions on purchasing REO or foreclosure properties. It then summarizes other federal requirements such as environmental protections, historic preservation, lead-based paint compliance, and construction standards for assisted properties. The document also notes administrative responsibilities related to procurement, file management, and handling funds. Finally, it explains exception authority, stating that certain handbook requirements may be waived by authorized officials when necessary and consistent with law and government interests.

nodes.title: Administrative Requirements
nodes.summary: The document section outlines additional federal and administrative requirements for USDA Section 502 housing loans. It explains that construction or rehabilitation must meet specified construction standards and that existing homes must be decent, safe, sanitary, and compliant with state and local codes. It also summarizes administrative rules for agency staff, including procurement procedures for goods and services, proper file and records management, and how to handle funds received in field offices. Finally, it describes the exception authority process, stating that exceptions to handbook or regulation requirements may be approved case-by-case by the Administrator or designee when doing so protects the Government’s interest and remains consistent with law.

nodes.title: Procurement
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key administrative and compliance requirements for the Section 502 housing program. It explains that construction and rehabilitation projects must follow specific construction standards and that existing homes must be decent, safe, and meet state and local codes. It also covers agency administrative responsibilities, including procurement rules, file management requirements, and procedures for handling funds received in field offices. In addition, it describes the authority for granting exceptions to handbook or regulatory requirements, who may request them, and the conditions under which exceptions can be approved.

nodes.title: File Management
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several key administrative and regulatory requirements for Section 502 housing programs. It explains that construction standards for new or rehabilitated dwellings must follow specific Rural Development instructions, and existing homes must meet safety, sanitation, and code requirements. It also covers administrative responsibilities for agency staff, including procurement rules for goods and services, proper file and records management, and procedures for handling funds received in field offices. In addition, the document describes exception authority, stating that deviations from handbook or regulatory requirements may be approved case by case by the Administrator or designee when necessary to protect the Government’s interest, and it identifies who may initiate such requests.

nodes.title: Handling Funds
nodes.summary: The document section outlines other federal requirements for Section 502 housing funds, including construction standards for developed or rehabilitated dwellings, which must comply with RD Instructions 1924-A and 1924-C and meet state and local codes. It also covers administrative requirements for agency employees, such as procurement rules, file management standards, and procedures for handling funds received in Field Offices. Finally, it explains exception authority, stating that exceptions to handbook or regulatory requirements may be approved by the Administrator or designee when necessary to protect the Government’s interest, and identifies who may initiate such requests and how they are submitted.

nodes.title: Exception Authority [7 CFR 3550.8]
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines several key compliance and administrative requirements for USDA Rural Development Single Family Housing programs. It explains that construction and rehabilitation projects funded under Section 502 must meet specified construction standards and that existing homes must be decent, safe, sanitary, and compliant with state and local codes. It also covers agency administrative responsibilities, including procurement rules for services, proper file and records management, and procedures for handling funds received in field offices.

In addition, the document describes exception authority under 7 CFR 3550.8. It states that exceptions to handbook or regulation requirements may be approved in individual cases when necessary to protect the Government’s interest, provided they are legally consistent. The text also explains who may initiate exception requests, how they must be routed, and what documentation is required, including a clear justification, alternatives considered, and mitigation of any adverse effects. For certain technical issues such as architectural, environmental, or civil rights matters, additional review and comments from technical staff are required.

nodes.title: Exception Authority
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics:

1. **Exception Authority Procedures**: It explains the requirements for submitting an exception request under USDA rules, including the need for clear and convincing evidence, a full explanation of the circumstances and adverse government impact, consideration of alternatives, and how the adverse effect will be reduced if the exception is approved. It also notes that requests involving architectural/engineering, environmental, or civil rights issues must include State Office Technical Staff review and comments, and that the Deputy Administrator coordinates these requests before they go to the Administrator.

2. **Informal Administrative Review Letter Template**: It provides a sample letter used to notify applicants or borrowers of an adverse decision after an informal review. The letter explains that if the facts are believed to be wrong, the person may use other appeal options within 30 days. It also includes required legal notices about the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and the Fair Housing Act, and identifies the agencies responsible for enforcing these laws.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-A INFORMAL ADMINISTRATIVE REVIEW
nodes.summary: The document provides USDA Rural Development template notices for adverse decisions and appeals related to housing assistance or foreclosure cases. It includes an informal administrative review letter that informs the recipient of an unfavorable decision, explains the 30-day window to challenge factual errors, and cites anti-discrimination protections under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and Fair Housing Act. It also includes an “appealable adverse decision” notice describing the recipient’s appeal rights and outlining available options, including requesting an informal administrative review and mediation (alternative dispute resolution), with instructions and deadlines for submitting written requests.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-B AN APPEALABLE ADVERSE DECISION
nodes.summary: The document is a USDA Rural Development notice template explaining how applicants can respond to an adverse decision. It outlines two main letter types: one for an appealable adverse decision and one for an adverse decision that cannot be appealed. For appealable decisions, it describes three response options within 30 days: request an informal administrative review, request mediation, or file an appeal hearing with the National Appeals Division (NAD). It explains how these options affect appeal rights, including waivers and time limits, and provides guidance on where to send requests. The document also includes civil rights and anti-discrimination notices under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and the Fair Housing Act, plus references to related offices and regulatory procedures.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-C AN ADVERSE DECISION THAT CANNOT BE APPEALED
nodes.summary:
The document provides two USDA Rural Development notice templates related to adverse decisions and mediation. Attachment 1-C explains that an adverse decision cannot be appealed in the usual way, and gives the applicant/borrower two options: request an informal administrative review within 30 days, or request a review by the National Appeals Division to determine whether the decision is truly non-appealable. It also includes required nondiscrimination notices under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and Fair Housing Act, with contact information for the Federal Trade Commission and HUD.

Attachment 1-D is a notification to applicants or borrowers that their mediation request has been assigned to a mediation service provider. It explains that mediation may involve a cost, that Rural Development will pay 50% of reasonable mediation costs, and that the participant must select or concur on a mediator within 10 days or waive mediation rights. It also states that mediation should be completed within 45 days and that, after mediation, the participant may appeal the original adverse decision to the National Appeals Division.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-D NOTIFICATION TO APPLICANTS OR BORROWERS OF THE ASSIGNMENT OF THEIR CASE TO A MEDIATION SERVICE PROVIDER
nodes.summary: The partial document contains USDA Rural Development mediation-related forms and instructions. It includes a notice to applicants or borrowers that their request for mediation of an adverse decision has been referred to a mediation service provider, explains possible mediation costs and the agency’s policy to pay 50% of reasonable costs, and instructs the recipient to select or concur in a mediator within 10 days or waive the right to mediation. It also describes the mediation timeline, the option to appeal to the National Appeals Division after mediation, the need to notify the office if mediation is cancelled, and that cancellation does not affect appeal rights. The notice also includes Equal Credit Opportunity Act and Fair Housing Act nondiscrimination statements. The second form is a request sent from Rural Development to a mediation service provider, identifying the customer, noting that an adverse decision and mediation request have been received, indicating whether an informal administrative review occurred, and providing the agency office responsible for the case.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-E REQUEST TO A MEDIATION SERVICE PROVIDER
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two USDA Rural Development mediation-related form letters.

The first letter, “Request to a Mediation Service Provider,” is a formal request from the Rural Development State Director asking a mediation service provider to take a case after a customer has received an adverse decision and requested mediation. It includes the customer’s information, whether an informal administrative review was offered or conducted, the agency office responsible for the decision, payment terms for mediation costs (split 50/50 between the agency and customer), a requirement to complete mediation within 45 days unless extended, and legal nondiscrimination notices under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and Fair Housing Act. It also lists required attachments and copies to be sent.

The second letter, “Attachment for Notifying Customers of Expiration of the 10 Days to Select a Mediation Service Provider,” informs the customer that the 10-day period to accept or name a mediation provider has expired. It states that mediation request processing will end and the agency will proceed with the original adverse decision. It also explains that the customer still may request an appeal hearing through the National Appeals Division if time remains, provides instructions for filing the appeal, and notes that there is no cost for an appeal hearing.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-F ATTACHMENT FOR NOTIFYING CUSTOMERS OF EXPIRATION OF THE 10 DAYS TO SELECT A MEDIATION SERVICE PROVIDER
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two USDA Rural Development notification templates related to mediation of adverse decisions and appeal rights.

Main points covered:
- **Attachment 1-F**: A notice to customers that the 10-day period to select or agree to a mediation service provider has expired. It explains that the mediation request has expired and Rural Development will proceed with the original adverse decision.
- It also reminds customers that mediation does **not** replace the right to request an **appeal hearing with the National Appeals Division (NAD)**, and gives instructions for contacting NAD, filing an appeal, and copying the State Director.
- It includes statements about the customer’s right to review or copy the agency record and requires written authorization for a representative or counsel.
- The notice also provides **Equal Credit Opportunity Act** and **Fair Housing Act** nondiscrimination information and complaint contact details.

- **Attachment 1-G**: A notice informing the customer that mediation has been completed but did **not resolve** the issue.
- It states that the requested assistance cannot be granted, will be terminated, or will be reduced, depending on the case.
- It again explains that the customer may still pursue an **NAD appeal**, with different instructions depending on whether an appeal was already requested before mediation.
- The document emphasizes that appeal requests must be made within the remaining allowable time and provides NAD contact guidance.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 1-G ATTACHMENT FOR CUSTOMER NOTIFICATION OF UNRESOLVED RESULT OF THE MEDIATION OF THE ADVERSE DECISION
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics:

1. **Notice of Unresolved Mediation Result and Appeal Rights**
   - It is a form letter informing a customer that mediation of an adverse decision was unsuccessful.
   - It states that the requested assistance cannot be granted, or may be terminated or reduced.
   - It explains that the customer may still appeal to the National Appeals Division (NAD) at no cost.
   - It provides instructions depending on whether the customer already requested an appeal hearing before mediation.
   - It also explains rights to review the case record, request a teleconference hearing, or ask for a decision without a hearing.
   - The notice includes nondiscrimination statements referencing the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and the Fair Housing Act, along with contact information for the relevant federal agencies.

2. **Introduction to Chapter 2: Regular Servicing**
   - The document introduces the servicing phase of a loan after funds are fully disbursed and transferred to the Servicing and Asset Management Office.
   - It explains that servicing includes routine, day-to-day loan administration rather than only delinquency-related actions.
   - Main servicing responsibilities described include:
     - handling borrower payments and fees,
     - approving certain borrower requests that affect the security property,
     - reviewing and managing escrow accounts, taxes, and insurance obligations.
   - It notes that later sections and chapters will provide more detailed policies for these servicing functions.

nodes.title: CHAPTER 2: REGULAR SERVICING
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction telling the reader to directly return a description of the document’s main points without including any other text.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a section from USDA HB-2-3550 about regular servicing of housing loans after disbursement. It explains that once a loan is fully funded, the Servicing and Asset Management Office takes over day-to-day servicing responsibilities.

Main points covered include:
- Handling borrower payments, due dates, late fees, returned payments, and electronic funds transfer processing.
- Approving borrower requests for actions that could affect the property.
- Managing escrow accounts, property taxes, and insurance requirements.
- Reviewing borrowers for eligibility for payment subsidies and conducting annual income reviews.
- Periodic review of whether borrowers can refinance with private credit.
