- Calculating subsidy recapture amounts when loans are paid off and handling deferred recapture.
- Procedures for deceased borrowers, including estate documentation and possible account transfer or foreclosure referral.
- Final payment and payoff procedures, including release of security instruments.
- Special servicing rules for borrowers with both RHS and FSA Farm Credit Program loans.
- Rights and servicing provisions under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act, including reduced interest rates for active-duty military members.

The document then begins Section 1, which introduces how new borrowers are brought into the servicing system, distinguishing between permanent loans and construction loans and explaining how each is disbursed and converted into permanent status.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: GETTING STARTED WITH NEW BORROWERS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how new borrowers are entered into the servicing office system and focuses on the two main loan types: permanent loans and construction loans. It describes when each type is used, how funds are disbursed, and how interest and payments are handled. Permanent loans are funded in a single advance at closing for borrowers who can occupy the property within 30 days, with any remaining repair funds held in escrow or a supervised account. Construction loans are used for new construction or repairs that delay occupancy beyond 30 days, are disbursed in multiple advances, accrue interest during construction, and have deferred payments until completion, after which they are converted to permanent loans.

nodes.title: GETTING A BORROWER INTO THE SERVICING OFFICE SYSTEM
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how new borrowers are entered into the servicing office system and focuses on the two main loan types used by the program: permanent loans and construction loans. It states that all loans originate at the Field Office and that loan disbursement depends on whether the borrower can occupy the property within 30 days.

It describes permanent loans as loans for borrowers who can occupy the property quickly; these are funded in a single advance and disbursed in full at closing, with any remaining repair funds held in escrow or a supervised account. It also explains construction loans as loans for building a new dwelling or making repairs that delay occupancy beyond 30 days; these are disbursed in multiple advances, accrue interest during construction, and have deferred payments until completion. After construction, the loan is adjusted to include accrued interest, amortized, and converted into a permanent loan.

nodes.title: Types of Loans
nodes.summary:
The document explains how new borrowers are entered into the servicing office system and focuses on the two main types of loans: permanent loans and construction loans. It describes when each type is used, how funds are disbursed, and how repayment is handled.

Key points covered:
- Permanent loans are used when the borrower can occupy the property within 30 days; funds are disbursed in a single advance at closing.
- If repair funds are not fully used at closing, the remaining proceeds are placed in an escrow or supervised bank account and disbursed later according to policy.
- Construction loans are used when the borrower needs funds to build a dwelling or make repairs that delay occupancy for more than 30 days.
- Construction loan funds are disbursed in multiple advances, and interest begins accruing daily at the borrower’s Equivalent Interest Rate when funds are disbursed.
- During construction, payments are deferred because the borrower may also be paying for other housing.
- After construction is completed, the loan originator adjusts the principal to include accrued interest, amortizes the loan, and converts it into a permanent loan.

nodes.title: Loan Activation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a borrower’s loan is moved into the servicing office system after closing, focusing on loan activation and the handling of the loan docket. It describes when loans are activated in LoanServ, the difference between permanent and construction loan servicing, and how basic borrower information is automatically transferred from origination systems. It also details the contents of the Loan Docket, the required transmittal forms and supporting documents, how escrow checks are handled, and the servicing office’s responsibilities for verifying, scanning, and completing loan records. The section emphasizes resolving missing or inconsistent information with the Field Office and notes that only copies of documents are sent in the docket while originals remain in the Field Office.

nodes.title: Loan Docket
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a loan is transferred into the Servicing Office system after full disbursement, including loan activation in LoanServ, the handling and verification of the Loan Docket, and how missing or inconsistent documents are resolved. It also describes how borrowers are introduced to the Servicing Office after closing and how the borrower’s transition from the Field Office is communicated. Finally, it covers the Servicing Office’s long-term role in borrower counseling, follow-up, and maintaining thorough case notes to support consistent, effective servicing and help borrowers stay current on their mortgage payments.

nodes.title: INTRODUCING THE BORROWER TO THE SERVICING OFFICE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how borrowers are introduced to and transitioned from the Field Office to the Servicing Office after loan disbursement, including when and how the Servicing Office must provide written contact information and servicing guidance. It also covers the Agency’s long-term borrower support approach, emphasizing follow-up, counseling, timely and effective communication with borrowers who are behind on payments, and the importance of accurate, thorough case documentation so different servicing staff can continue helping the borrower without duplicating efforts.

nodes.title: WORKING WITH BORROWERS OVER THE LONG TERM
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how borrowers should be transitioned from the Field Office to the Servicing Office after loan funds are fully disbursed, including notifying borrowers that servicing-related communication must go to the Servicing Office. It also requires the Servicing Office to send a written explanation before the first billing statement describing its role, servicing policies, and important contact numbers. In addition, the document emphasizes the Agency’s long-term responsibility to support borrowers through counseling, reminders, and follow-up for late payments, stressing the importance of both the quality and frequency of contact. Finally, it highlights the need for thorough and accurate documentation so any servicing staff member can continue helping the borrower effectively without duplicating efforts.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: PAYMENTS AND FEES [7 CFR 3550.152 AND 3550.153]
nodes.summary:
This section explains how loan payments must be made and how they are processed. It covers the required payment frequency, generally monthly but sometimes annual depending on the loan documents. It describes what makes up a scheduled payment, including principal, interest, taxes, and insurance for borrowers with escrow accounts, or just principal and interest for those without escrow. It also explains that payments are not credited until the full scheduled payment is received.

The section then outlines acceptable payment methods, emphasizing preauthorized debit as the preferred option because it automatically withdraws funds from a checking or savings account and helps ensure timely payment. It notes that borrowers with multiple loans on one property may use a single billing statement or debit, and that annual-pay borrowers cannot use preauthorized debit.

Finally, it details how preauthorized debit works, including how new borrowers authorize it at closing and how existing borrowers can set up, change, or cancel it by contacting the Servicing Office. It also states that borrowers remain responsible for making payments until they receive confirmation that the change has been processed.

nodes.title: FREQUENCY OF PAYMENTS
nodes.summary:
This section explains how loan payments must be made and what payment methods are available. It states that borrowers generally make monthly payments, unless the loan documents set different terms, and that scheduled payments may include principal, interest, taxes, and insurance if there is an escrow account, or principal and interest only if there is no escrow account. It also notes that payments are not credited until the full scheduled payment is received, and that some loans require a single annual payment with partial payments allowed during the year.

The section then describes payment methods, emphasizing preauthorized debit as the preferred option because it automatically withdraws funds from a checking or savings account and helps ensure timely payment. It explains that borrowers with multiple loans secured by one property can use one billing statement or debit, and that annual-pay borrowers are not eligible for preauthorized debit.

Finally, it details how preauthorized debit works, including how new borrowers can authorize it at closing, how existing borrowers can set it up or change it by contacting the Servicing Office, and that borrowers must keep making payments until they receive confirmation that the change has been processed.

nodes.title: PAYMENT METHOD
nodes.summary: This section explains how loan payments and fees are handled, focusing on payment frequency and payment methods. It states that borrowers generally must make monthly payments, which include principal and interest, and escrowed taxes and insurance when applicable, while some loans use annual payment terms. It also describes how payments are processed and credited only when the scheduled payment is received. The section then outlines payment methods, emphasizing preauthorized debit as the preferred option because it automatically withdraws funds from a checking or savings account and improves on-time payment likelihood. It notes that borrowers with multiple loans secured by one property may use a single billing statement or debit, and that annual-pay borrowers cannot use preauthorized debit. Finally, it details how preauthorized debit works, including enrollment at closing, later changes or termination by existing borrowers through the Servicing Office, and the requirement to continue making payments until written confirmation is received.

nodes.title: Preauthorized Debit
nodes.summary: This section explains how loan payments and fees are handled, focusing on payment frequency and payment methods. It states that borrowers generally make monthly payments, with payment amounts depending on whether they have an escrow account, and notes that some loans are set up for annual payments with partial payments allowed during the year. It describes two main payment methods: preauthorized debit, which is preferred and automatically withdraws funds from a borrower’s account, and billing statements, which are mailed to borrowers who pay manually. The document also outlines how preauthorized debit is authorized or changed, what happens if funds are unavailable, and when late fees apply. Finally, it explains when annual-pay borrowers may or must convert to monthly payments, including situations involving subsequent loans, reamortization, new subsidy, or the need to establish an escrow account.

nodes.title: Billing Statement
nodes.summary: The document section explains payment processing methods for borrowers, including how preauthorized debit works and what happens if funds are unavailable, as well as late fee consequences. It describes billing statement procedures for borrowers who are not on preauthorized debit, including monthly and annual billing timelines and handling partial annual payments. It also covers when and how annual-payment borrowers should convert to monthly payments, including required agreement to amortized interest and escrow accounts, and identifies situations where conversion is mandatory. Finally, it notes that when a borrower takes a subsequent loan, the Field Office must coordinate with the Servicing Office to update the repayment schedule from annual to monthly.

nodes.title: Conversion from Annual to Monthly Payments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how borrower payments are handled, including preauthorized debit rules, billing statement timing, and what happens if a payment is missing or short. It describes when annual-payment borrowers should or must convert to monthly payments, including cases involving new loans, reamortization, subsidy changes, or escrow requirements. It also outlines the process for converting payment schedules and updating loan records between the Field Office and Servicing Office. In addition, it lists acceptable payment methods, and details how the lockbox service receives, processes, and forwards payment information, including how late fees are assessed for unpaid balances.

nodes.title: ACCEPTABLE FORMS OF PAYMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how borrower payments are handled under HB-2-3550. It covers the reamortization agreement process for borrowers who are converted, including notification, signing Form RD 3550-18, and filing procedures. It then outlines acceptable payment methods, stating that checks, money orders, and bank drafts are allowed, foreign currency is not accepted, and cash payments must be accepted but are discouraged and may incur a conversion fee. Finally, it describes lockbox payment processing, including how payments are received, date-stamped, recorded, and transmitted to the Servicing Office, and how the Servicing Office credits accounts in LoanServ and assesses late fees when payments are incomplete or late.

nodes.title: LOCKBOX SERVICES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains payment-related procedures for borrower loans. It covers how borrowers who are converted are notified to sign a reamortization agreement, how the Servicing Office and field office handle and file that agreement, and how existing subsidy agreements may continue unless circumstances change. It also lists acceptable payment methods, stating that checks, money orders, and bank drafts are allowed, foreign currency is not, and cash payments are accepted but discouraged with a conversion fee. Finally, it describes lockbox services for receiving and processing payments, including date-stamping, recording, and forwarding payment data to the Servicing Office, and explains how the Servicing Office credits payments in LoanServ and applies late fees when full payment is not received by the deadline.

nodes.title: Crediting Accounts at The Servicing Office
nodes.summary: The partial document explains payment-related procedures for USDA Rural Housing borrowers. It covers how converted borrowers are notified and processed for reamortization agreements, what payment methods are acceptable, and how cash payments are handled. It also describes the lockbox payment system, including how payments are received, recorded, and forwarded to the Servicing Office, and how the Servicing Office credits accounts in LoanServ and automatically applies late fees when payments are not fully received by the due date.

nodes.title: Payments Received at The Servicing Office
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how borrower payments are handled under the lockbox services system, especially when payments are received at the Servicing Office instead of the lockbox. It states that such payments are processed and forwarded to the lockbox, and if received more than 15 days after the due date, the borrower is charged a late fee. It also emphasizes employee responsibility for any losses while handling funds and requires that all cash, checks, and money orders be recorded and secured immediately.

The document then covers irregular payments, such as partial, excess, or payments on closed accounts, which are processed by the lockbox and reported to the Servicing Office. It focuses on partial payments, explaining that payments less than the scheduled amount are held in suspense until enough money is received to cover a full scheduled payment. Special rules are given for annual payment loans, where partial payments remain suspended until the full amount is received and the borrower is notified, and for multiple loans, where payments are applied first to the oldest loan and then to other loans in order of age.

nodes.title: IRREGULAR PAYMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling borrower payments at the servicing office and through the lockbox, including what happens when payments arrive late or are received outside the normal process. It also covers employee responsibility for safeguarding funds and recording any cash, checks, or money orders immediately. In addition, it describes how irregular payments are handled, especially partial payments, including suspense treatment, how annual-payment loans are processed, and how partial payments are allocated when a borrower has multiple loans.

nodes.title: Partial Payments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains payment handling procedures for a servicing office and lockbox system. It covers what happens when borrower payments are received at the servicing office instead of the lockbox, including forwarding the payment, late fee responsibility if received after 15 days past due, and employee accountability for funds in their custody. It also describes irregular payments, such as partial payments, excess payments, and payments on closed accounts, and how they are processed and reported. The section explains that partial payments are held in suspense until enough funds are received to match a scheduled payment, with special rules for annual payment loans and for borrowers with multiple loans, where payments are applied in order starting with the oldest loan.

nodes.title: Loans on an Annual Payment Schedule
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how borrower payments are handled through the lockbox and servicing office, including procedures for payments received outside the normal lockbox process. It covers responsibility for late fees when a payment reaches the servicing office more than 15 days after the due date, and notes that employees are accountable for losses of funds while in their custody.

It also describes irregular payments, such as partial payments, excess payments, and payments on closed accounts, and states that the lockbox processes and reports these to the servicing office. A major focus is on partial payments: they are held in suspense until enough money is received to cover a scheduled payment, at which point they are credited to the borrower’s account. Special rules are included for annual payment loans, where partial payments remain in suspense until the full annual amount is received, and for borrowers with multiple loans, where payments are applied first to the oldest loan and then to others in descending order of age.

nodes.title: Multiple Loans
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for handling borrower payments in the servicing office and through the lockbox system. It states that if a payment is received at the servicing office instead of the lockbox, it must be processed and forwarded, and late fees still apply if it arrives more than 15 days after the due date. It also emphasizes employee responsibility for funds in their custody and requires that all cash, checks, and money orders be recorded and secured immediately.

The document then covers irregular payments, including partial payments, excess payments, and payments on closed accounts. It explains that the lockbox processes these payments and reports them to the servicing office. For partial payments, the funds are held in suspense until a full scheduled payment is received. Special rules are described for annual payment loans, where partial payments remain in suspense until the full amount is paid and the borrower is notified, and for borrowers with multiple loans, where payments are applied to the oldest loan first and then to other loans in order of declining age.

nodes.title: Excess Payments
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how irregular mortgage payments are handled and what fees may be charged. It covers:

- How excess payments are applied first to fees or charges, then to principal
- How year-end statements must show the treatment of excess payments
- Rules for borrowers who want to prepay an installment
- When loan accounts may be reamortized if excess payments reach certain thresholds
- How payments made on closed or charged-off accounts are processed
- Agency fee policies, including late fees and insufficient funds fees
- Conditions under which fees may be waived and the need to document waiver decisions

nodes.title: Payments on Closed Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how irregular mortgage payments are handled, including excess payments, prepayments, and payments made on closed accounts. It states that extra funds are first applied to outstanding fees or charges, then to principal, and that borrower statements must show how excess payments were used. It also notes that if excess payments reach certain thresholds, the account may be reamortized to lower monthly payments. The section then covers payments received on closed or charged-off accounts, which are still accepted and credited appropriately. Finally, it outlines fee rules, including late fees and insufficient funds fees, when they are charged, and under what limited circumstances fees may be waived, emphasizing consistent standards and documentation.

nodes.title: FEES [7 CFR 3550.153]
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers how irregular loan payments are handled, including excess payments, payments on closed accounts, and fee assessments. It explains that overpayments are first applied to outstanding fees or charges and then to principal, and that year-end statements must show how excess funds were used. It also states that scheduled installments intended as regular payments must be paid in the exact scheduled amount with borrower indication, while certain accumulated excess payments or construction loan surplus may trigger reamortization to lower monthly payments. For closed or charged-off accounts, payments are still accepted and credited appropriately. The section also outlines late fees and insufficient funds fees, including a 4% late fee after the 15th day past due and a $15 returned-check fee, plus limited circumstances under which fees may be waived with proper documentation.

nodes.title: SECTION 3: BORROWER ACTIONS REQUIRING APPROVAL [7 CFR 3550.159]
nodes.summary: This partial document describes USDA Rural Development loan servicing procedures for borrower actions that require Agency approval because they may affect the value of the security property. It explains that such requests are handled by Field Offices, while Servicing Offices refer borrowers and provide supporting information. The section outlines the general process for submitting requests using Form RD 465-1, the need for additional information if the request is incomplete, and when an appraisal is required—specifically for subordination or partial release of security when consideration exceeds $5,000. It also notes that the borrower must pay for the appraisal, though the cost may be charged to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Development servicing procedures for borrower actions that require Agency approval because they may affect the security value of the property. It identifies key actions needing approval, including subordination, mineral rights leases, partial release of security, lease of security property, and assumption of indebtedness. It also describes the roles of the Servicing Office and Field Office: the Servicing Office refers borrowers and provides supporting information, while the Field Office reviews and makes the approval decision.

The section then outlines general guidelines for processing requests. Borrowers must submit Form RD 465-1 to the local Field Office for subordination, mineral leases, partial release, or lease of security property. If needed, the Field Office may request additional information, with the Servicing Office helping supplement the file. It also states that an appraisal and environmental review may be required, and that an appraisal is required when the Agency subordinates its interest or approves a partial release if the consideration exceeds $5,000, with the borrower responsible for the appraisal cost.

nodes.title: GENERAL GUIDELINES
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines Section 3 on borrower actions requiring Agency approval under HB-2-3550. It explains that certain actions affecting property security must be approved by the Agency and are handled by Field Offices, with Servicing Offices referring requests and providing supporting information. The main actions mentioned are subordination, lease of mineral rights, partial release of security, lease of security property, and assumption of indebtedness.

It then presents general guidelines for processing requests, including that borrowers must submit Form RD 465-1 for subordination, mineral leases, partial releases, or leases of security property. If needed, the Field Office may request additional information, and the Servicing Office may help supplement the record.

Finally, it notes that an appraisal and environmental review may be required, and that an appraisal is required when the Agency subordinating its interest or approving a partial release involves consideration over $5,000. The borrower pays for the appraisal, though the cost may be charged to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: Reviewing Requests
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for borrower actions that require Agency approval because they can affect the security value of the property. It explains that these matters are handled by Field Offices, while Servicing Offices refer borrowers and provide supporting information. The section covers general guidelines for reviewing requests such as subordination, mineral leases, partial release of security, and lease of security property, including the required Form RD 465-1. It also notes that additional information may be requested if needed. Finally, it discusses the need for an appraisal and environmental review, stating that an appraisal is required when the Agency subordinates its interest or approves a partial release if the consideration exceeds $5,000, with the borrower responsible for the appraisal cost.

nodes.title: Obtaining an Appraisal and an Environmental Review
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines USDA Rural Housing Service procedures for borrower actions that require Agency approval because they may affect the security value of the property. It explains that these cases are handled by Field Offices, while Servicing Offices refer borrowers and provide supporting information.

Main points covered include:
- Overview of actions requiring approval, such as subordination, mineral rights leases, partial release of security, lease of security property, and assumption of indebtedness.
- General application procedures, including use of Form RD 465-1 to request approval.
- Requirements for additional information if the borrower’s submission is incomplete.
- Appraisal rules, including when an appraisal is required, when an existing appraisal may be used, and when lender-provided appraisals are acceptable.
- Environmental review requirements for mineral leases.
- How the Field Office evaluates requests by assessing impacts on property value, physical effects, and proceeds distribution.
- Approval documentation and file handling, including distribution and retention of signed forms.
- Initial guidance on how proceeds from transactions must be used, starting with paying reasonable transaction-related costs.

nodes.title: Evaluating and Approving the Request
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines guidelines for handling appraisal and mineral lease requests, including when an existing appraisal can be used, when an Agency appraisal is unnecessary, and the need to follow environmental requirements before approving mineral leases. It then explains the process for evaluating and approving a request: the Field Office must assess how the proposed action affects the security property, consider market value, physical impacts, and payment proceeds, and document findings on Form RD 465-1. If the Agency’s security is not endangered, the approval official may sign the form and distribute copies appropriately. Finally, it describes how proceeds from transactions involving part of the security property should be applied first to customary and reasonable transaction-related costs such as taxes, title work, surveys, attorney’s fees, and appraisal expenses.

nodes.title: Using Proceeds
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Development loan servicing guidance, focusing on how the Agency handles transactions affecting security property. It explains when an existing appraisal may be accepted, when an Agency appraisal is not required, and that mineral lease approvals must meet environmental requirements. It then describes the process for evaluating requests: the Field Office must assess the impact on the security property, document findings on Form RD 465-1, consider market value, physical effects, and how sale proceeds will be applied, and distribute the approved form appropriately. The document also outlines how proceeds from sales, easements, damage compensation, and similar transactions must be used, including payment of transaction costs, prior liens, and either Agency debt or property improvements. Finally, it introduces the subordination policy, explaining that the Agency may subordinate its lien to help borrowers access private credit, which office handles different types of requests, the criteria for approval, and that subordination is not allowed for nonprogram loans.

nodes.title: SUBORDINATION [7 CFR 3550.159(b)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains general guidelines for using loan proceeds and the Agency’s subordination policy for liens. It covers how proceeds may be applied either to prior lien debt or to Agency indebtedness/improvements on the security property, including required planning, supervision, documentation, and reporting procedures. It also describes when the Agency may subordinate its lien to help a borrower obtain private credit, who handles different subordination requests, the typical refinancing scenarios involved, and the main approval criteria such as verifying eligible use of funds and ensuring the borrower can still reasonably repay the debt. It notes that subordination is not allowed for nonprogram loans.

nodes.title: Criteria for Subordination
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Agency rules for using proceeds from transactions on security property, including how proceeds must be applied to prior lien debt, Agency indebtedness, or approved improvements, and how such use is documented and transmitted. It then covers subordination of the Agency’s lien, describing when it may be allowed, who handles different types of requests, the criteria that must be met, and the limits on the amount of debt that can be ahead of the Agency’s interest. Finally, it discusses mineral leases on security property, stating that borrowers must get Agency authorization, that legal and environmental review may be required, and that approved mineral lease income affects subsidy and does not give the leaseholder rights to property sale proceeds.

nodes.title: Amount of Subordination
nodes.summary: This portion of the document explains Agency rules for subordinating its lien and approving mineral leases on security property. It covers the conditions required for subordination, including construction standards compliance, written notice before foreclosure, and documentation from the other lender. It also explains how the maximum allowable debt is calculated depending on whether the borrower repays the Agency loan or retains it. Finally, it outlines the requirements for leasing mineral rights, including prior Agency authorization, involvement of OGC, environmental review, and how any rental income or lease proceeds are handled.

nodes.title: MINERAL LEASES [7 CFR 3550.159(a)]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains Agency rules for handling changes to secured property under USDA Rural Development loan programs. It covers:

- **Subordination of the Agency’s lien**, including required conditions such as compliance with Agency construction standards, advance notice before foreclosure by a prior lien holder, and how to document the subordination request.
- **Limits on the amount of debt allowed** when the Agency subordinates its interest, distinguishing between cases where the borrower repays the Agency loan but defers recapture, and cases where the borrower keeps the Agency loan while obtaining another loan.
- **Mineral leases**, requiring Agency authorization before leasing mineral rights, involving OGC and an environmental review, and explaining when lease income or compensation must be assigned to the Agency.
- **Criteria for approving mineral lease subordination**, including ensuring the property remains a suitable residence and that the Agency’s security is not harmed, with different rules depending on whether the lease is expected to reduce property value.
- **Partial release of security**, describing when a borrower may request release of part of the secured property for sale, exchange, or right-of-way purposes, and indicating that approval depends on meeting Agency conditions.

nodes.title: No decrease in value
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for mineral leases and partial releases of security in USDA/Rural Housing loan security property. It explains when the Field Office may consent to leasing mineral rights and subordinating liens, focusing on whether the property will remain a suitable residence and whether the Government’s security interest is protected. It distinguishes between cases where the lease is not likely to reduce property value and cases where it likely will, including requirements for damage compensation or assigning lease income to reduce the loan principal. It also describes the need for an income assignment format approved by OGC when required. Finally, it introduces the process for partial release of security, which borrowers may request for property sales, exchanges, or rights-of-way, and notes that the Agency may approve such transactions if certain conditions are met.

nodes.title: Likely decrease in value
nodes.summary: The document explains rules for mineral leases and partial release of security on a borrower’s property. It states that the Field Office may approve leasing mineral rights and subordinate liens only when the property remains suitable as a residence and the government’s security interest is protected. Approval depends on whether the lease is likely to affect property value: if there is no likely decrease, the borrower must use damage compensation to repair the property or assign it to reduce the loan principal; if there is likely to be a decrease, the borrower must assign all lease income to the Agency and ensure rent at least equals the estimated loss in market value. It also notes that any required income assignment must comply with state law and be approved by OGC. The section then introduces partial release of security, which may be requested to sell, exchange, or grant rights-of-way over part of the property, and says the Agency may consent if certain conditions are met.

nodes.title: PARTIAL RELEASE OF SECURITY [7 CFR 3550.159(c)]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for two property-related situations under HB-2-3550: mineral leases and partial release of security. It explains when the Field Office may consent to leasing mineral rights and subordinating liens, focusing on whether the property will remain a safe, decent residence and whether the government’s security interest is protected. It distinguishes between leases unlikely to reduce property value, where damage compensation must be used for repairs or assigned to the Agency, and leases likely to reduce value, where all lease income must be assigned to reduce principal and rent must cover the estimated loss in value. It also notes that income assignments must comply with state law and be approved by OGC. The second section describes when a borrower may request a partial release of security for selling, exchanging, or granting a right-of-way over part of the property, giving examples such as boundary swaps or highway widening, and states that Agency consent depends on meeting required conditions.

nodes.title: The borrower will receive adequate compensation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and procedures for a partial release of security in a loan context. It states that the borrower must receive adequate compensation when part of the security property is sold, exchanged, or used for an easement or right-of-way. It also requires that the remaining security property still be adequate, decent, safe, sanitary, and, for program loans, remain modest, and that repayment of the Agency debt not be put at risk. The document then outlines the Field Office’s responsibilities in processing a partial release, including completing required release forms, handling document recording and imaging, obtaining title clearance and new security instruments for exchanges, delivering the release upon full payment for sales, and updating legal descriptions as needed.

nodes.title: The security property, after the transaction is completed, must be adequate, decent, safe, and sanitary
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the rules and procedures for a partial release of security on a loan. It says the borrower must receive adequate compensation through a sale, exchange, or easement/right-of-way, with value equal to or greater than what is released. After the transaction, the remaining security property must still be adequate, decent, safe, sanitary, and for program loans, modest. The release must not jeopardize repayment of the Agency debt.

It also outlines the Field Office steps for processing a partial release: completing required state release forms, uploading unrecorded documents to the imaging repository, ensuring the requester pays recording costs, obtaining title clearance and new security instruments for exchanges, delivering the release after full payment for sales, and updating legal descriptions as needed.

nodes.title: Repayment of the Agency debt must not be jeopardized
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development loan servicing rules for the partial release and lease of security property. It covers the conditions that must be met for a partial release, including adequate compensation to the borrower, maintaining adequate/decent/safe/sanitary and modest security, and ensuring the Agency’s debt is not jeopardized. It also outlines Field Office processing steps such as completing release forms, handling title clearance for exchanges, releasing security after full payment in sales, and updating legal descriptions. In addition, it describes situations where the Field Office may approve releases, including release of additional security, mutual mistake, no evidence of indebtedness, and valueless liens. Finally, it addresses lease of security property, requiring borrower notification, describing impacts on subsidy or servicing benefits, and noting that leases over three years or with purchase options may lead to liquidation, while nonprogram borrowers may lease without restriction.

nodes.title: Additional security
nodes.summary: This document section explains the rules for partial release of security and leasing of security property under USDA Rural Housing loan regulations. It outlines when the Field Office may approve releasing property from the Agency’s lien, including releases for additional security, mutual mistake, no evidence of indebtedness, and valueless liens in liquidation cases. It also describes the process for notifying the Servicing Office and consulting legal or borrower assistance staff when needed. The second part covers lease restrictions: borrowers must notify the Agency if they lease the property, leasing can affect eligibility for subsidy or special servicing benefits, and long-term leases or leases with purchase options may allow the Agency to liquidate the loan. Nonprogram borrowers are not subject to these leasing restrictions.

nodes.title: Mutual Mistake
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Agency rules on partial release of security and leasing of security property for Section 502 loans. It covers when the Field Office may approve release of real estate from the Agency’s lien, including cases of additional security, mutual mistake, no evidence of indebtedness, and valueless liens in liquidation. It also describes borrower obligations and Agency actions when a program borrower leases the property, including loss of subsidy or special servicing benefits during the lease, notice requirements, possible liquidation for certain lease terms, and the distinction that nonprogram borrowers may lease without restriction.

nodes.title: No evidence of indebtedness
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for releasing part of the security tied to a USDA Section 502 loan and for leasing security property. It explains when the Agency may approve partial lien releases, including additional security that is no longer needed, releases caused by mutual mistake, cases where there is no evidence of indebtedness, and valueless liens in liquidation situations. It also describes the process for notifying the Servicing Office and consulting the Borrower Assistance Branch or OGC when needed. The second main topic is leasing of security property: borrowers must notify the Agency if they lease the property, and leasing can affect eligibility for payment subsidy or special servicing benefits. The document also notes that the Agency may liquidate the loan if the lease is longer than three years or includes an option to purchase, while nonprogram borrowers may lease without restriction.

nodes.title: Valueless Lien
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Agency rules for releasing part of a borrower’s security and for leasing security property. It covers several types of partial lien release situations: additional security that is no longer needed, releases caused by mutual mistake, cases where there is no evidence of secured indebtedness, and valueless liens in liquidation cases. It also describes the process for notifying the Servicing Office and consulting legal or borrower assistance staff when appropriate. The second section states that program borrowers must notify the Agency if they lease the property, and that leasing can affect subsidy or special servicing eligibility, may trigger loan liquidation if the lease is long-term or includes an option to purchase, while nonprogram borrowers may lease without restriction.

nodes.title: LEASE OF SECURITY PROPERTY [7 CFR 3550.159(d)]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Section 502 loan servicing rules on several related topics:

- **Partial release of security**: When and how the Agency may release portions of its lien, including cases involving additional security, mutual mistake, no evidence of indebtedness, and valueless liens in liquidation cases.
- **Lease of security property**: Borrower notification requirements if the property is leased, loss of subsidy/special servicing benefits during the lease, possible loan liquidation for longer leases or leases with purchase options, and the distinction for nonprogram borrowers.
- **Assumption of indebtedness**: Procedures for transferring an existing loan obligation to a new party, including:
  - **New rates and terms assumptions**, usually handled during a sale and allowing release of the former borrower from liability.
  - **Same rates and terms assumptions**, typically for family transfers, where the original loan terms stay unchanged and special rules apply for subsidy, delinquency, and refinancing review.
- **Eligible transfers for same rates and terms assumptions**: The section begins listing the types of transfers allowed, starting with transfers from a borrower to a spouse or children.

nodes.title: ASSUMPTION OF INDEBTEDNESS [7 CFR 3550.163]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for assuming Agency indebtedness under Section 502 loans. It distinguishes between two main types of assumptions: new rates and terms assumptions, which are usually used in sales and handled by the Field Office, and same rates and terms assumptions, which are limited cases such as transfers between family members and keep the existing loan terms unchanged. It also covers how liability is released for the former borrower, when the Servicing Office becomes involved if debt remains or the account is delinquent, how subsidy may continue for an eligible new owner, and when refinancing or a new rates and terms assumption may be more beneficial.

nodes.title: A. New Rates and Terms Assumptions
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how Agency loan indebtedness can be assumed and distinguishes between two types of assumptions: new rates and terms assumptions and same rates and terms assumptions. It notes that most assumptions occur as part of a sale and are handled by the Field Office, which may release the former borrower from liability, while any remaining debt is handled by the Servicing Office. It also describes limited cases, such as family transfers, where a same rates and terms assumption is allowed without changing the existing loan terms or interest rate. The document discusses when subsidy may continue, when a new rates and terms assumption may be more beneficial, and mentions that assumptions can apply to certain transfers such as from a borrower to a spouse or children.

nodes.title: B. Same Rates and Terms Assumptions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for assuming Agency debt under USDA Section 502 loans. It distinguishes between two main types of assumptions: new rates and terms assumptions, which are usually used in property sales and handled by the Field Office or Servicing Office depending on whether the debt is fully assumed, and same rates and terms assumptions, which are limited cases often involving family transfers and keep the original loan terms unchanged. It also discusses when payment subsidy may continue, when refinancing or servicing review may apply, and which types of title transfers qualify for same rates and terms treatment. Finally, it covers the Agency’s approval requirements under the due-on-sale clause, emphasizing that title transfers with assumption require Agency approval, that approval depends on the Government’s interest, and that unauthorized transfers may lead to loan liquidation or continued liability for the original borrower.

nodes.title: C. Agency Approval
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains rules and procedures for assumption of indebtedness on Agency mortgages, including when property transfers are allowed, when Agency approval is required, and how due-on-sale clauses affect loan transfers. It outlines specific transfer situations that may be exempt, such as transfers due to death, divorce, transfers into certain trusts, and some dependent-related assumptions. It also describes when the Agency will approve an assumption, what happens if a property is transferred without prior approval, and the limitations on a new owner who does not assume the loan.

The document further covers procedural requirements for completing an assumption, including signing Form RD 3550-22 and handling original loan notes. Finally, it explains how a departing borrower can be released from liability, the conditions that must be met for that release, the use of Form RD 3550-16, and the documentation required to request removal of a borrower from the loan obligation.

nodes.title: D. Procedural Requirements
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains how assumption of indebtedness works when a property is transferred to a new owner, including when Agency approval is not required, the limits on assistance for a new owner who does not assume the loan, and the need to sign Form RD 3550-22. It also covers the procedure for releasing a departing borrower from liability, especially in cases like divorce, including the conditions that must be met, the use of Form RD 3550-16, and the required supporting documents and financial information to request the release.

nodes.title: E. Releasing a Departing Borrower From Liability
nodes.summary:
This excerpt explains the rules for assumption of indebtedness and releasing a departing borrower from liability under USDA Rural Housing loan servicing. It states that a new owner who acquires property through a qualifying same-rates-and-terms assumption does not need Agency approval or to formally assume the loan, as long as payments continue and other obligations are met, but such an owner cannot receive payment assistance or a moratorium unless the loan is assumed. It then outlines procedural steps, including signing Form RD 3550-22 and how the original note is handled.

The section also covers how a borrower can be released from liability after changes in household composition, such as divorce. It lists the conditions under which the Agency will allow a release, including that the divorce or settlement did not assign payment responsibility to the departing borrower, ownership has been conveyed to the remaining borrower, and the remaining borrower meets debt ratio and credit requirements or the account has been current for six months. Finally, it identifies Form RD 3550-16 as the release document and details the supporting documentation required, such as a written request, contact information, divorce documents, deed, financial statement, income verification, and tax return copies.

nodes.title: SECTION 4: REFINANCING WITH PRIVATE CREDIT [7 CFR 3550.160]
nodes.summary: The partial document only provides an instruction to directly return a description of the main points covered in the partial document, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Agency’s process for refinancing Section 502 loans with private credit. It states that the loan program is meant to supplement, not replace, private lending, and borrowers may be reviewed periodically to see whether they can refinance 100% of their Agency loan through private credit. It describes the role of the Servicing Office in identifying borrowers, sending notices, answering questions, and providing payoff and recapture information, and notes that failure to cooperate or refinance when required can lead to default and liquidation. The document also outlines how borrowers are selected for review, generally every two years, based on payment history, outstanding balance, and any known changes in circumstances such as inheritance or increased income, with the initial criteria including that the loan be active and the borrower be in the program.

nodes.title: SELECTING BORROWERS FOR REVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the Agency’s policy on refinancing Section 502 loans with private credit. It states that these loan programs are not meant to replace private credit sources and that borrowers may be periodically reviewed to see if they can refinance their entire Agency loan privately. It describes the consequences for borrowers who do not cooperate with information requests or who refuse to refinance when required, including default and possible liquidation.

The document also outlines the Servicing Office’s responsibilities in managing the refinance review process, such as identifying borrowers, sending notices, responding to questions, and providing payoff and recapture information. It then explains how borrowers are selected for review, noting that all Section 502 borrowers are reviewed every two years, with additional reviews triggered by changes in circumstances like inheritance or a new job. The criteria for selection begin with the loan being active and the borrower being in the program.

nodes.title: Criteria
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the USDA Section 502 refinancing process with private credit. It outlines the purpose of periodic reviews to determine whether borrowers can refinance their Agency loans through private lenders, and notes that failure to cooperate or refinance when requested can lead to default or liquidation. It describes how the Servicing Office manages borrower review lists, notifications, payoff information, and enforcement actions.

The document then details the criteria used to select borrowers for refinancing review, including loan status, credit score, delinquency history, balance requirements, foreclosure or bankruptcy status, disaster area restrictions, and other conditions. It also explains how pending subsequent loan requests are handled during a refinancing review, generally requiring the subsequent loan to be processed first. Finally, it covers the subordination of recapture when refinancing, allowing the Agency to subordinate its interest in deferred recapture under certain conditions to help borrowers obtain private credit.

nodes.title: Subsequent Credit
nodes.summary: The partial document explains criteria and procedures for selecting borrowers for refinancing review under HB-2-3550. It lists the conditions borrowers and accounts must meet to be considered, such as credit score, loan type, delinquency status, foreclosure or bankruptcy status, balance thresholds, disaster-area restrictions, and timing requirements. It also describes how to handle subsequent credit requests during a refinancing review, requiring the subsequent loan to be processed first and denied if private credit is available. In addition, it covers subordination of recapture when a Section 502 loan is refinanced, allowing the Agency to subordinate deferred recapture in some cases to help a borrower obtain private refinancing, and notes that borrowers asked to refinance generally cannot receive additional credit until the refinancing issue is resolved unless needed to protect the Government’s interest.

nodes.title: Subordination of Recapture
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the agency selects borrowers for refinancing review and the conditions that make a borrower eligible for consideration. It lists many screening criteria, such as no foreclosure, bankruptcy, delinquency workout, moratorium, or recent delinquencies, plus minimum loan balance, credit score, loan age, and disaster-area restrictions.

It then covers how subsequent credit requests are handled if a borrower is already being reviewed for refinancing, stating that the subsequent loan must be processed first and may be denied if private credit is available.

Next, the document describes subordination of recapture for Section 502 loan refinances, including when the agency may subordinate its interest and what costs or amounts can be included.

Finally, it outlines the process for requesting a borrower to refinance with private credit, including the letters sent, the 90-day response period, the required lender rejection information if credit is denied, and follow-up actions for uncooperative borrowers. It also notes a Servicemembers Civil Relief Act provision affecting recapture calculations.

nodes.title: PROCESSING REQUEST TO REFINANCE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA loan servicing procedures for reviewing borrowers who may be able to refinance with private credit. It covers when the Agency loan can be subordinated, what costs can be included in the subordinated amount, and the steps the Servicing Office takes when refinancing appears possible, including sending letters to the borrower and requesting lender rejection letters if private credit cannot be obtained. It also describes follow-up actions for borrowers who do not respond, such as reminder letters and possible calling campaigns, and notes a Servicemembers Civil Relief Act provision stating that the interest rate reduction to 6% is not subject to recapture.

nodes.title: UNCOOPERATIVE BORROWERS AND ACCELERATION
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Servicing guidance on borrower refinancing review, handling uncooperative borrowers, and subsidy recapture. It explains when a borrower may refinance with private credit, how the Servicing Office notifies borrowers and requests lender rejection letters, and how follow-up letters are used if borrowers do not respond. It also outlines actions for borrowers who may or may not qualify for refinancing, including continued review timelines and reminder/follow-up notices. The latter section introduces recapture policy, describing when subsidy recapture is triggered, how borrowers are notified, the 60-day payment window before acceleration and foreclosure, and when recapture is collected if the borrower stops occupying the property or transfers title.

nodes.title: SECTION 5: RECAPTURE [7 CFR 3550.162]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA loan subsidy recapture rules and when recapture is triggered. It covers how borrowers who received payment subsidies may owe repayment of some or all of that subsidy when they repay the loan, stop occupying the property, or transfer title. It describes the notification process, the 60-day deadline before referral for acceleration and foreclosure, and how the Servicing Office determines whether borrowers have ceased to occupy the property. It also notes exceptions for temporary unoccupancy approved by the Agency, ways the office may learn a property has been vacated or rented, and the impact of the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act on recapture calculations.

nodes.title: TRIGGERING RECAPTURE
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing subsidy recapture policy under HB-2-3550, including when recapture applies and how it is enforced. It covers:

- The purpose of subsidy recapture: borrowers who received payment subsidy may have to repay some or all of it when the loan is repaid and certain conditions change, while still benefiting from property equity.
- Eligibility and exceptions: loans approved before October 1, 1979 are generally exempt unless later assumed on new terms.
- Triggering events for recapture: recapture becomes due when the loan principal and interest are paid in full and the borrower stops occupying the property or transfers title.
- Notification and collection process: the Servicing Office sends a notice of the amount due, and if payment is not made within 60 days, the account may be referred for acceleration and foreclosure.
- Definition of ceasing occupancy: the borrower no longer uses the dwelling as a permanent residence; the office investigates if there is evidence the property was vacated.
- Temporary absence and acceptable reasons: recapture is not triggered if the property is only temporarily unoccupied for agency-acceptable reasons.
- Ways the Servicing Office may learn of vacancy or rental: borrower notice, field office referral, change-of-address forms, returned mail, insurance policies, and tax notices.
- Special rule for servicemembers: under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act, the difference between the full note rate and 6% is not subject to recapture.

nodes.title: A. Ceasing to Occupy the Property
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Rural Development subsidy recapture policy for Section 5 of HB-2-3550, including when recapture applies and how it is collected. It covers the main triggering events: when a borrower stops occupying the property, when a borrower dies, and when title to the property is transferred. It describes the servicing office’s responsibilities to notify borrowers, investigate occupancy status, and refer delinquent cases for acceleration and foreclosure if recapture is not paid within 60 days. It also outlines special rules for temporary vacancy, deceased borrowers, joint borrowers, and different transfer scenarios such as cash sales, assumptions under new rates and terms, and assumptions under same rates and terms. The excerpt also notes an exception related to the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act, which limits the amount of subsidy subject to recapture.

nodes.title: B. Deceased Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture is triggered and handled in specific situations. It covers procedures for deceased borrowers, including how the Servicing Office verifies death, notifies responsible parties, updates account records, and refers recapture receivable accounts for recovery when all borrowers are deceased. It also describes how title transfers affect recapture, outlining rules for cash sales, assumptions under new rates and terms, deferred subsidy amounts, and assumptions under the same rates and terms.

nodes.title: C. Transferring Title
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains when subsidy recapture is triggered and how it is handled in special situations, especially when a borrower dies or transfers title. It describes servicing procedures for deceased borrowers, including contacting the estate, documenting death, and referring recapture receivables for collection when all borrowers are deceased. It also outlines how recapture applies under different title transfer scenarios, such as cash sales, assumed loans with new rates and terms, deferred subsidy amounts, and assumptions at the same rates and terms.

The document then introduces the rules for calculating recapture owed. It explains that the calculation is generally automated but important for servicers to understand, and that the recapture amount is based on the borrower’s equity, subsidy received, and any principal reduction attributable to subsidy (PRAS). It distinguishes normal recapture calculations from foreclosure and deed-in-lieu cases, where the full subsidy amount is recoverable from the property. Finally, it defines PRAS and explains its historical basis for certain loans that received interest credit between 1979 and 1989.

nodes.title: CALCULATING RECAPTURE OWED
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how subsidy recapture is calculated for certain USDA Rural Housing loans. It states that the recapture amount is generally automated but servicers should understand it well enough to answer borrower questions. The section describes the formulas used to determine recapture, including how the amount depends on the borrower’s equity at payoff and includes Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) for certain older loans, plus the lesser of total subsidy received or the property’s appreciation subject to recapture. It also explains that if there is no equity, PRAS is not collected. For foreclosure or deed-in-lieu cases, the full subsidy amount is recaptured except PRAS, and the recapture is limited to the security property rather than being a personal liability. The document also outlines the order in which foreclosure sale proceeds are applied to debt. Finally, it defines PRAS, explains how it was created by interest credit reducing the effective interest rate on loans made between 1979 and 1989, and notes that PRAS continued to grow over time because the borrower was repaying at a lower effective interest rate.

nodes.title: A. Recapture Formulas
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture owed on certain loans is calculated and what servicers need to know to explain it to borrowers. It covers the general recapture formula, including how the amount is based on the borrower’s equity at loan payoff and may include Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS), subsidy received, or the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture. It also distinguishes normal payoff situations from foreclosure or deed in lieu of foreclosure, where the full subsidy received is recaptured except PRAS, and describes the order in which sale proceeds are applied to the debt. Finally, it defines PRAS and explains that it arose from interest credit subsidies on certain Section 502 loans made between October 1, 1979 and December 31, 1989, causing faster principal reduction and remaining subject to recapture.

nodes.title: B. Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how recapture owed is calculated for subsidized housing loans, with emphasis on how servicers should understand and explain the process to borrowers. It covers:

- The general recapture formula, which combines Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) with the lesser of the subsidy received or the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture.
- Special rules for foreclosure or deed-in-lieu cases, where the full subsidy amount is recaptured except PRAS, and the recaptured funds are applied in a specific order to costs, interest, principal, and subsidy.
- A detailed explanation of PRAS, including which loans are affected, how PRAS accumulated under the old subsidy method, why it can exceed subsidy received, and how the system later reduces it over time.
- The concept of value appreciation for non-foreclosure cases, including the need for an appraisal or sales contract and the formula used to determine the portion of appreciation subject to recapture.
- An exhibit/formula showing how current market value is adjusted for liens, settlement costs, principal reductions, original equity, and capital improvements to calculate recapture amount.

nodes.title: C. Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: This section explains how subsidy recapture is calculated for certain Rural Housing Service loans, focusing on Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) and value appreciation. It describes how PRAS accumulates, when it stops or decreases, and notes that loans made since January 1, 1990 do not have PRAS because the subsidy method changed. The document then outlines the formula for determining the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture, including current market value, prior liens, other affordable housing products, Agency loan payoff amounts, FLP equity recapture, settlement costs, principal reduction, original equity, and capital improvements. It also explains how market value may be established using appraisals, sales contracts, or other reliable evidence, and details the individual components used in the recapture calculation.

nodes.title: 1. Current Market Value
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a property, focusing on the factors used in determining the amount due. It covers: current market value and how it must be established through acceptable evidence such as appraisals, sales contracts, assessed value, or tax records; the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing assistance used to determine original equity; the balance owed on Agency loans including fees and other charges; any recapture related to prior Farm Loan Program debt if equity was applied to that debt; and reasonable settlement costs that may be deducted if they are actual, customary, and directly paid by the borrower.

nodes.title: 2. Original Amount of Prior Liens and Subordinate Affordable Housing Products
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate the recapture amount owed on a property, focusing on the components used in the calculation. It covers determining the current market value of the property and the acceptable evidence for that value, including appraisals, sales contracts, assessed values, and tax records. It also discusses including the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products, the balance owed on Agency loans, any recapture due from prior Farm Loan Program debt applied to the home, and reasonable settlement costs that may reduce the recapture amount.

nodes.title: 3. Balance to be Paid Off on Agency Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on the factors used in the calculation. It covers determining current market value through appraisals, contracts, tax records, or other reliable evidence; identifying the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products; totaling all amounts owed on Agency loans being paid off; including any applicable equity recapture from an accelerated Farm Loan Program loan; and accounting for reasonable, actual settlement costs directly paid by the borrower. It also notes what evidence is acceptable and when the Agency or borrower is responsible for providing market value information.

nodes.title: 4. Equity Recapture Due from Farm Loan Program Loan (if applicable)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate equity recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on the components used in the recapture formula. It covers determining current market value through appraisals, sales contracts, assessed value, or other reliable evidence; the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing assistance used in the original equity calculation; all balances owed on Agency loans including fees and advances; any recapture due from prior Farm Loan Program debt if applicable; and reasonable settlement costs that may be deducted in a sale or refinance.

nodes.title: 5. Reasonable Settlement Costs
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture owed is calculated under HB-2-3550, with a focus on determining current market value and the components included in the recapture formula. It covers acceptable methods for valuing the property, including borrower-provided appraisals or sales contracts, and Agency-determined evidence such as appraisals, assessed value, or tax records. It then lists other required calculation inputs: original amounts of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products, amounts owed on Agency loans, any equity recapture from a prior Farm Loan Program loan, reasonable settlement costs, principal reductions, and original equity. It also explains how original equity is determined and defines market value for different transaction types. Finally, it describes how borrowers can receive credit for capital improvements, noting that an appraiser must determine the value added by the improvements rather than the cost of the improvements.

nodes.title: 6. Principal Reductions
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under USDA Rural Housing Service rules, focusing on factors that affect the amount due. It covers:

- **Principal reductions**: how much of the RHS loan principal has actually been paid by the borrower, excluding amounts covered by payment assistance subsidy (PRAS).
- **Original equity**: how to determine the borrower’s original equity using the property’s market value at loan approval, minus prior liens and certain subordinate financing; it also distinguishes how market value is defined for self-help loans versus other transaction types.
- **Capital improvements**: how borrowers can receive credit for improvements that increased the property’s value, with the value determined by an appraiser based on the added market value rather than the cost of the improvement.

nodes.title: 7. Original Equity
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on three key inputs: principal reductions, original equity, and capital improvements. It defines principal reduction at the note rate and notes that payment assistance subsidy principal is excluded. It then describes how original equity is determined using the property’s market value at loan approval, minus liens and certain subordinate debts, with different valuation rules for self-help loans versus other transaction types. Finally, it states that borrowers must identify capital improvements to receive credit, and that an appraiser—not the improvement cost—determines their value based on the increase in property value.

nodes.title: 8. Capital Improvements
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on the inputs used in the calculation. It covers: principal reductions at the note rate, excluding payments tied to payment assistance subsidy; original equity, including how it is determined from market value and adjusted for liens and certain subordinate loans; and capital improvements, which must be identified and appraised based on the increase in property value rather than the cost of the improvement.

nodes.title: 9. Amount Due if There is No Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: This section explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers cases where there is no value appreciation, how to determine the share of appreciation subject to recapture based on the proportion of recapture-eligible debt, how the recapture percentage is set by loan term and subsidized interest, how borrower equity affects the calculation, and how much of the property’s appreciation is ultimately subject to recapture.

nodes.title: 10. Percentage of Outstanding Balance of Loans (Percentage of Total Debt Subject to Recapture to be Paid-Off)
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff, focusing on the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers: when no value appreciation exists, how to determine the amount due; how to calculate the share of appreciation subject to recapture based on the proportion of debt being paid off; how the recapture percentage is determined by loan duration and subsidized interest rate; how borrower equity return is computed; and how the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture is calculated, including the role of original equity and recapture factors.

nodes.title: 11. Recapture Percentage
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers cases where there is no value appreciation, how to determine the share of appreciation subject to recapture based on the proportion of recapture-eligible debt, how recapture percentage is calculated from loan term and subsidized interest, how borrower equity return is determined, and how the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture is computed using a recapture factor and original equity credit.

nodes.title: 12. Return on Borrower’s Equity
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture owed is calculated in a loan payoff situation. It covers: determining the amount due when there is no value appreciation, calculating the percentage of outstanding loan balance subject to recapture, defining the recapture percentage based on loan term and subsidized interest, computing the borrower’s return on equity, and determining the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture. It also references the Final Payoff Worksheet attachments and notes that recapture on value appreciation can range from 9 to 50 percent depending on loan conditions.

nodes.title: 13. Portion of Value Appreciation Subject to Recapture
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate and collect subsidy recapture owed on a loan payoff. It covers the main worksheet components used to determine the amount due in different situations, including when there is no value appreciation and when there is appreciation. It explains how the payoff amount is based on the balance of loans subject to recapture, the recapture percentage, the borrower’s original equity, and the portion of value appreciation that must be repaid. It also describes the process for collecting recapture information, including when a final payoff statement is issued, what documentation is required, and how the Servicing Office handles payoff requests.

nodes.title: 14. Amount Due if There is Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how subsidy recapture is calculated and collected when a borrower pays off an Agency loan. It describes how the final payoff amount is assembled, including the loan balance, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and the lesser of the subsidy received or the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture. It then outlines the process for obtaining recapture information: the borrower requests a payoff statement from the Servicing Office, provides required documentation such as an appraisal or arm’s length sales contract, and the Servicing Office calculates the recapture due and issues a final payoff statement. It also notes that field offices usually are not involved. Finally, it specifies the information needed for a final payoff statement, including the payoff date and documented market value of the property, and states that a Broker’s Price Opinion is not acceptable.

nodes.title: COLLECTING RECAPTURE INFORMATION
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture owed is calculated and collected when a borrower pays off an Agency loan. It describes how the final payoff amount is assembled, including the loan balance, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and either the full subsidy received or the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture, whichever is less. It then outlines the process for collecting subsidy recapture information, noting that a payoff request triggers a payoff statement and that the borrower must provide documentation such as an appraisal or arm’s-length sales contract. Finally, it states that the Servicing Office usually handles the calculation and issuance of the final payoff statement, and it lists the key requirements for a payoff with subsidy recapture, including the payoff date and documented market value of the property.

nodes.title: A. Payoff With Final Payoff Statement
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains how subsidy recapture is calculated and collected for a Rural Development loan payoff. It covers:

- How the recapture amount is determined when there is value appreciation, including combining the loan payoff, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and the lesser of subsidy received or the portion of appreciation subject to recapture.
- The process for collecting recapture information when a borrower requests a final payoff statement, including required documentation such as payoff date, market value evidence, capital improvement documentation, and settlement statements.
- Rules for payoffs made without first contacting the Servicing Office, where the Agency determines market value and later informs the borrower of the subsidy received and how to calculate recapture due.
- Acceptable methods for determining market value, such as appraisals, arm’s length sales contracts, assessed values, tax records, and other reliable evidence, along with borrower rights to submit their own appraisal if they disagree.

nodes.title: B. Payoff Without Contacting the Servicing Office for a Final Payoff Statement, or Final Installment Payment
nodes.summary:
The excerpt from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 2.24, explains how recapture information is collected and documented when a borrower has to repay subsidy. It covers:
