- How borrowers can receive credit for the added value of capital improvements, requiring the appraiser to document the value in a separate addendum, with receipts only serving as supporting evidence of what was improved.
- How settlement costs may be credited for refinancing or sale transactions, provided they are reasonable, customary, and properly documented by a good faith estimate or estimated settlement statement.
- The process when a borrower pays off a loan without first obtaining a final payoff statement, including the Servicing Office sending a “Congratulations” letter and explaining subsidy recapture calculations.
- How market value is determined for recapture purposes using appraisal evidence, sales contracts, assessed value, tax records, or other reliable sources, and how appraisal costs are handled.
- The borrower’s right to submit their own appraisal if they disagree with the Agency’s valuation.
- General guidance on acceptable methods for determining market value, including abbreviated appraisals and assessed values when they accurately reflect property value.

nodes.title: 1. Determining Market Value
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how subsidy recapture information is collected and documented when a borrower pays off a loan, especially in cases involving capital improvements and settlement costs. It states that capital improvements must be supported by an appraisal addendum itemizing each improvement’s added value, and receipts alone are not enough. It also explains when settlement costs may be credited using a good faith estimate or estimated settlement statement, depending on whether the borrower is refinancing or selling, and notes that real estate agent estimates are not acceptable.

The document then covers procedures for payoff situations where the borrower does not first request a final payoff statement. In those cases, the Servicing Office sends a “Congratulations” letter, and the Agency is responsible for determining market value using evidence such as an appraisal, sales contract, assessed value, tax records, or other reliable sources. It describes how field offices and servicing offices coordinate to establish market value, including the use of appraisals, addendums for capital improvements, and reimbursement of appraisal costs by the Agency when needed.

Finally, it outlines the notification and workflow process between the Servicing Office and field offices, including task assignments, deadlines, and the instruction not to release security instruments until recapture is calculated. It explains the timeframes for borrower response, field office valuation, and submitting the recapture calculation request to the Payoff Unit with supporting documentation.

nodes.title: 2. Borrower Notification – Servicing Office and Field Office Interface
nodes.summary:
This document section explains how recapture information is collected and processed when an Agency loan is paid off, including how market value is established and how capital improvements are handled. It describes the coordination between the Servicing Office and Field Office, required tasks and timeframes, borrower notification steps, and the documents that must be sent to calculate recapture.

It also covers what happens after recapture is calculated: the borrower has 120 days to pay or defer the amount, with a possible 25% discount if payment is made while the borrower keeps title and occupies the property. Finally, it outlines deferring recapture payment, establishing an interest-free recapture receivable account, annual reporting requirements, and special rules for refinancing with private credit, including Agency subordination limits and incentives to encourage borrowers to pay recapture rather than defer it.

nodes.title: DEFERRING PAYMENT OF RECAPTURE
nodes.summary:
The document explains how recapture payments are collected, deferred, and tracked after a borrower repays an Agency loan. It describes the Servicing Office’s responsibilities to calculate recapture due, notify the borrower, allow 120 days for payment or deferral, and apply a 25% discount if paid within that period while the borrower keeps title and occupies the property. It also covers how cases are marked and how lien release processing occurs when recapture is paid in full.

The section on deferring payment of recapture explains that borrowers may postpone payment if they continue to occupy the property and title does not transfer. In that case, an interest-free recapture receivable account must be set up, annual statements must be sent, and the debt becomes due only if the borrower moves or transfers title.

The document also discusses refinancing with private credit, including when the Agency may subordinate its interest during deferral based on property value and debt limits. Finally, it outlines the incentive to pay recapture, emphasizing the 25% discount as a way to reduce long-term receivable accounts, especially in refinancing situations.

nodes.title: A. Refinancing with Private Credit
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how recapture amounts are handled when a borrower repays an Agency loan, including calculating the amount due, notifying the borrower, and allowing 120 days for payment or deferral. It describes the conditions for receiving a 25% discount if the borrower pays within the time limit while keeping title and occupying the property. It also covers deferring recapture payment, which creates an interest-free recapture receivable account, with payment due only if the borrower moves or transfers title, and requires annual statements from the Servicing Office. In addition, it outlines refinancing with private credit, including when the Agency may subordinate its interest, and notes the incentive structure for encouraging borrowers to pay recapture rather than defer it.

nodes.title: B. Incentive to Pay Recapture
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing recapture procedures, including how the Servicing Office calculates and notifies borrowers of recapture amounts, the 120-day period to pay or defer, and the 25% discount if paid promptly while retaining and occupying the property. It also covers deferring recapture payment by establishing an interest-free receivable account, conditions for subordination when refinancing with private credit, annual statements for unpaid recapture, and incentives for borrowers to pay rather than defer. Finally, it describes how recapture is handled for certain Trust-held former Agency loans, including reassignment to the Agency, creation of receivable accounts, and recording instructions to the Field Office.

nodes.title: TRUST ACCOUNTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers several servicing topics for Rural Housing loans, focusing on how borrowers may defer recapture payment, how trust-held loans handle recapture, and how final loan payoffs are processed. It explains that recapture discounts may be available within 120 days in certain cases, and that Trust loans needing deferred recapture must be reassigned to the Agency so a receivable account can be established. The document then outlines what makes up a borrower’s final payment amount, including unpaid principal, interest, fees, protective advances, unauthorized assistance, and recapture, and notes exceptions for older loans and debt settlements. It also describes the payoff statement process, requiring borrowers to obtain written payoff statements from the Servicing Office rather than receiving amounts by phone, and explains how Field Offices can request payoff information.

nodes.title: SECTION 6: FINAL PAYMENTS [7 CFR 3550.161]
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be an instruction rather than substantive content. It states that the response should directly return a description of the main points covered in the partial document, without adding any extra text.

nodes.title: FINAL PAYMENT AMOUNT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for final loan payments and payoff statements under Section 6 of HB-2-3550. It states that a borrower’s account cannot be closed until the Agency receives payment in full, and that the final amount includes the outstanding balance plus any fees, charges, protective advances, FLP equity recapture, unauthorized assistance, and any required subsidy recapture. It notes that loans approved before October 1, 1979, are not subject to subsidy recapture, and that escrow or supervised bank account funds are returned separately. The document also describes how borrowers or authorized third parties can request payoff statements from the Servicing Office, emphasizing that final payoff amounts are provided only in writing and not by telephone, and that Field Offices may request payoff information through the specified handbook form or by phone to the payoff section.

nodes.title: PAYOFF STATEMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA loan final payment procedures. It covers how the final amount due is calculated, including outstanding loan balance, fees, protective advances, equity recapture, unauthorized assistance, and subsidy recapture, and notes that loans approved before October 1, 1979 are exempt from subsidy recapture. It states that an account is not closed until all required amounts are paid, though debt settlements may satisfy the account under certain conditions, and any remaining escrow or supervised bank account funds are returned separately. The document also describes payoff statements: borrowers should request one from the Servicing Office before making the final payment, written authorization rules for third-party requests, and that final payoff amounts are never given by phone. Finally, it distinguishes between two payoff statement types—Statement of Loan Balance and Final Payoff Statement—and explains how each is used to determine the amount due and recapture based on borrower-provided information.

nodes.title: Statement of Loan Balance
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payoff statements are handled for loans, especially when the final payoff amount depends on the payment date and any recapture due. It describes two types of statements: a Statement of Loan Balance, which shows the current loan balance and subsidy information and tells the borrower what documents are needed to determine recapture; and a Final Payoff Statement, which gives the actual amount due based on the proposed payoff date and borrower-provided information. It also notes that the Servicing Office issues the final payoff statement after receiving written confirmation of needed information, and will generate an updated statement if the borrower later provides revised details.

nodes.title: Final Payoff Statement
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how payoff statements are handled for loans, especially when final repayment depends on the payment date and subsidy recapture amounts. It distinguishes between two statement types: a Statement of Loan Balance, which shows the current balance and gives instructions for calculating recapture, and a Final Payoff Statement, which gives the actual amount due based on the borrower’s provided information and proposed payoff date. It also covers the process for updating payoff statements if new information is received.

The document then describes how final payments are processed: payments are date-stamped when received, the payoff amount is recalculated based on the actual receipt date, and any underpayment is held in suspense until the borrower provides the remaining funds. If the borrower overpays or if the payoff date changes, the Servicing Office must resolve the difference and issue an updated final payoff statement. Finally, it outlines account closing actions, including releasing or subordinating security documents depending on whether the loan balance and subsidy recapture have been fully paid.

nodes.title: PROCESSING FINAL PAYMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Servicing Office should process final loan payments. It states that payments are date-stamped when received and the payoff amount is recalculated based on that date. If the payment is too low, it is held in suspense and the borrower is contacted to resolve the difference and receive a revised final payoff statement. If the payment is more than the payoff amount, the office handles the excess accordingly. Once the correct payoff amount is received, the office closes the account by releasing security documents. It also notes that because release procedures vary by state, the Servicing Office may need to ask Field Offices to prepare the necessary documents and provide clear instructions, especially when subsidy recapture is deferred and security instruments are not fully released.

nodes.title: A. Release Security Documents
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how final loan payments are processed. It says payments are date-stamped when received, and the payoff amount is recalculated based on that date. If the payment is short, it is held in suspense until the borrower provides the remaining funds and a new payoff statement is issued; if the borrower overpays, the Servicing Office handles the excess. Once the correct payoff is received, the account is closed by taking several actions: releasing security documents, notifying the property insurance company to end the Agency’s interest, stopping billing and preauthorized debits, and terminating any assignments of income by returning checks to the borrower and informing the sender to pay the borrower directly.

nodes.title: B. Release Insurance
nodes.summary: This section explains the actions the Servicing Office must take after a loan is paid in full. It covers notifying the property insurance company that the Agency’s interest in the property has ended, stopping billing statements and preauthorized debit requests, and terminating any assignments of income by forwarding any received checks to the borrower and telling the sender to send future payments directly to the borrower.

nodes.title: C. Terminate Billing and Preauthorized Debits
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Servicing Office’s responsibilities when processing final loan payments. It covers notifying the property insurance company that the Agency’s interest has ended, stopping billing statements and preauthorized debit requests after payoff, and ending income assignment arrangements by forwarding any received checks to the borrower and informing the sender that future payments should go directly to the borrower.

nodes.title: D. Terminate Assignments of Income
nodes.summary: The document section explains procedures for processing final loan payoffs, including notifying the property insurance company to end the Agency’s interest, stopping billing and preauthorized debits when a loan is paid off, and forwarding any assigned income payments to the borrower while informing senders to pay the borrower directly. It also includes a Final Payoff Worksheet used to calculate value appreciation and determine any amount due at payoff, with steps for accounting for current market value, prior liens, RHS loans, equity recapture, settlement costs, principal reduction, subsidies, original equity, and capital improvements.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-A FINAL PAYOFF WORKSHEET
nodes.summary:
The document is a “Final Payoff Worksheet” used to calculate the amount owed when a borrower pays off certain RHS loans and related affordable housing obligations. It is structured into five parts:

- **Part I: Value Appreciation** — calculates whether the property has appreciated in value after subtracting prior liens, loans being paid off, equity recapture, settlement costs, principal reduction, subsidy-related reductions, original equity, and capital improvements.
- **Part II: Amount Due if There Is No Value Appreciation** — determines the payoff amount when there is no appreciation, including RHS loans, FLP equity recapture, and PRAS amounts.
- **Part III: Percentage of Total Debt Subject to Recapture to Be Paid Off** — computes what portion of total mortgage debt is subject to recapture, especially for leveraged loans or when some loans are not being repaid.
- **Part IV: Value Appreciation Subject to Recapture** — applies the recapture percentage and original equity percentage to determine the portion of appreciation subject to recapture.
- **Part V: Amount Due if There Is Value Appreciation** — calculates the final recapture amount and total final payoff, including possible discounted recapture if applicable.

Overall, the worksheet provides a step-by-step method for determining final payoff and recapture obligations based on property value, loan balances, subsidies, and equity.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-B SUBSIDY RECAPTURE CASE STUDY
nodes.summary:
The partial document contains two main topics:

1. A subsidy recapture case study for the Potter family, showing how to calculate the final payoff amount on a subsidized rural housing loan. It walks through the family’s mortgage history, home improvements, sale details, and the step-by-step worksheet for determining value appreciation, recapture, and payoff.

2. Guidance for servicing borrowers who have both Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) Farm Credit Program loans. This section explains the background for separating servicing responsibilities after reorganization and outlines the purpose of the attachment, including case file separation, account flagging, handling RH loans tied to FSA buildings, and general servicing actions such as refinancing, partial releases, and lien position determination.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-C UNITED STATES DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE SERVICING BORROWERS WITH BOTH RURAL HOUSING SERVICE (RHS) AND FARM SERVICE AGENCY (FSA) FARM CREDIT PROGRAM (FCP) LOANS
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction stating that the response should directly return the description without any additional text.

nodes.title: 1 Overview
nodes.summary: The partial document is an USDA Rural Development servicing attachment that explains how to handle borrowers who have both Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans and Farm Service Agency (FSA) Farm Credit Program (FCP) loans after agency reorganization. It gives background on why these jointly held or cross-collateralized loans must now be split between agencies, with FSA servicing FCP loans and RHS servicing Rural Housing and Labor Housing loans. The document’s purpose is to guide field offices on key servicing actions, including separating borrower case files and flagging accounts, identifying and servicing RH loans tied to FSA buildings, and applying general servicing procedures such as graduation, refinancing, partial releases, releases, and determining lien position.

nodes.title: A Background
nodes.summary: This partial document is an USDA Rural Development servicing attachment for borrowers who have both Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) Farm Credit Program (FCP) loans. It explains the background for why these jointly held loans must now be split into separate case files and serviced by separate agencies after reorganization. The document states that FSA will service all FCP loans, while RHS will service Rural Housing and Labor Housing loans. It also outlines the purpose of the attachment: to provide field office guidance on key servicing actions, including separating borrower case files and flagging accounts, identifying and servicing RH loans for FSA buildings, and handling general servicing procedures such as graduation, refinancing, partial releases, releases, and lien position determination.

nodes.title: B Purpose
nodes.summary:
This document is a USDA servicing attachment that explains how to manage borrowers who have both Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans and Farm Service Agency (FSA) Farm Credit Program (FCP) loans. It provides background on why these shared loans must now be split into separate agency case files and serviced separately after reorganization, with FSA handling FCP loans and RHS handling housing-related loans.

The main points covered include:
- The purpose of the attachment and the servicing actions it addresses.
- How to separate borrower case files and identify which documents belong in the RHS file versus the FSA file.
- General servicing procedures for these dual-loan borrowers, including graduation, refinancing, partial releases, lien position, and releases.
- Servicing of delinquent or financially distressed accounts.
- Procedures for foreclosures and voluntary conveyances.
- Contact information for field and state offices needing guidance.

The document also begins the detailed instructions for initial action, including splitting borrower files and moving original documents related only to the RHS loan into the RHS case file.

nodes.title: C Contact
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines servicing procedures for delinquent and financially distressed accounts, including foreclosures and voluntary conveyances, while an MOU is being developed to govern future handling. It provides contact guidance for different offices and explains the initial action required to separate borrower case files into RHS and FSA loan files, with FSA retaining the original file and moving RHS-specific original documents to the RHS file.

nodes.title: 2 Initial Action
nodes.summary: The partial document is an attachment to HB-2-3550 that explains how delinquent and financially distressed borrower accounts will be serviced until a future Memorandum of Understanding is issued. It covers the purpose and contact points for questions, then begins the initial action procedures. A key topic is separating borrower case files into two files: one for the RHS loan and one for the FSA FCP loan. It also notes that RHF loans are treated as FCP loans and kept in the FSA case file, while original documents related only to the RHS or LH loan are moved to the RHS file, with examples such as the promissory note, mortgage/deed of trust, and payment assistance agreement.

nodes.title: A Separating Borrower Case Files
nodes.summary:
This partial document outlines procedures for servicing accounts involving both Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) loans, including delinquent and financially distressed accounts, foreclosures, and voluntary conveyances. It explains that a memorandum of understanding will eventually define future handling, but until then the attachment governs. The document also provides contact directions for field and state offices.

A major focus is the initial handling of borrower case files: files must be separated into distinct RHS and FSA case files, with FSA keeping the original file and transferring RHS-only original documents, while joint documents remain as copies in both files. It identifies RHF loans as FCP loans to be included in the FSA file. The document also describes how joint-security borrowers will be flagged in case files and systems, including red labeling, Management Record System flags, and related status report indicators.

nodes.title: B How Accounts Will Be Flagged
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains procedures for handling borrower case files and accounts that involve both RHS and FSA FCP loans secured by the same instruments. It covers how to separate and copy joint documents, how both agencies should flag case files and systems to identify joint security instruments, and how certain reports indicate the presence of both loans. It also discusses when additional security may be released to simplify future servicing, including guidance for RHS and FSA on reviewing cross-collateralized property and notes that a mortgage or deed of trust cannot be released if it covers both agencies’ loans.

nodes.title: C Release of Additional Security
nodes.summary: The partial document explains servicing procedures for joint FSA and RHS loans, especially how accounts are flagged and how agencies should handle collateral, releases, and insurance. It notes that reports and inquiry screens may not show cross-collateralization, so security documents must be reviewed carefully before any subordination, release, or liquidation to determine whether both agencies are involved. It also describes when FSA or RHS may consider releasing additional security, such as nonfarm dwellings or farm property, and references the appropriate regulations and instructions. A note emphasizes that a mortgage or deed of trust cannot be released of record if it covers both agencies’ loans, and that a release letter should instead be issued to the non-releasing agency. The document then covers insurance policy requirements, instructing borrowers to update the mortgagee clause so both RHS and FSA are listed and to file proof of the change. Finally, it begins a section on RHF loans, explaining that these loans are now serviced by FSA as FCP loans, how they were transferred and re-coded, where they appear on reports, and what state offices should do if any have not been transferred.

nodes.title: D Insurance Policies
nodes.summary: This section covers two main topics related to loan servicing. First, it explains insurance policy requirements: agencies must ensure borrowers update their insurance records so that both RHS and FSA are listed as mortgagees, with proper USDA address information, and that proof of the change is kept in the borrower’s case file with follow-up to confirm completion. Second, it describes RHF loans, noting that these older loans for farm building construction were transferred to FSA, should now be serviced as FCP loans, appear on the 540 Status Report instead of older reports, and if any were not transferred, State Offices must send specific identifying information to KCMO.

nodes.title: 3 RHF Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics related to loan servicing procedures. First, it explains how agencies must handle insurance policies for borrowers by ensuring the lienholder or mortgagee name is updated to list the United States Department of Agriculture, with both RHS and FSA named so both agencies receive insurance notices; it also notes that the change must be documented and followed up in the borrower’s case file. Second, it describes RHF loans, including how they are identified and serviced by FSA as FCP loans, their historical background, the transfer of these loans to FSA in 1995, how they appear in reporting systems, and what information State Offices must send if any loans were not transferred.

nodes.title: A Identifying RHF Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document describes servicing procedures for USDA farm loan programs. It covers how borrowers’ insurance policies must be updated so both RHS and FSA are listed as mortgagees, with follow-up to ensure the change is made and recorded. It then explains how to identify and service RHF loans, including their transfer from RH funds to FSA, how they appear in loan reports, and what state offices should do if a loan was not transferred. Finally, it outlines a general servicing procedure for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, requiring the contacted agency to provide payoff information and notify the requester that the other agency also has a loan.

nodes.title: B Servicing RHF Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to identify and service Rural Housing Finance (RHF) loans, including specific coding changes needed to transfer the loan to FSA. It also outlines how RHF loans are treated in future Farm Ownership lending and servicing, such as not counting toward FO loan limits, being included in DALR$ under fund code 46 for restructuring, using the lower of the original or current RH loan rate, extending amortization up to 33 years from the original note date, and requiring Form RD 1940-17. In addition, it covers general servicing procedures for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, instructing the contacted agency to provide payoff information and direct the requester to the other agency for its loan payoff details.

nodes.title: 4 General Servicing Procedures
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for handling and servicing RHF loans, including how to identify them and what coding changes may be requested, such as transferring the loan to FSA and changing fund and kind codes. It also explains how RHF loans are treated in future Farm Ownership lending and servicing, including that they do not count toward FO loan limits, may be considered for restructuring under specific funding rules, use the lower of the original or current RH loan rate, can be amortized up to 33 years from the original note date, and require a promissory note form. Finally, it covers general servicing procedures for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, instructing agencies to provide payoff information and direct requestors to the other agency for its loan payoff details.

nodes.title: A Payoff Requests
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes servicing procedures for RHF loans and joint FSA/RHS loan actions. It explains how to identify RHF loans by requesting transfer to FSA and changes to fund and kind codes. It then outlines how RHF loans are treated during future Farm Ownership lending and servicing, including that they are not counted toward FO loan limits, may be considered for restructuring in DALR$ under fund code 46, use the lower of the original note rate or current RH loan rate, can be amortized up to 33 years, and require Form RD 1940-17.

The document also covers general servicing procedures for loans held jointly by FSA and RHS. This includes handling payoff requests by informing borrowers about both agencies’ loans, treating refinancing and classification separately by each agency, and allowing shared financial documents upon request. For partial releases, subordinations, and consents involving joint security instruments, both agencies must approve, one Form RD 465-1 is used, the agency receiving the form leads processing and obtains any needed appraisals, and if both agencies cannot approve the request, it is denied.

nodes.title: B Refinancing and Classification
nodes.summary: The document section outlines general servicing procedures for joint RHS and FSA loans, focusing on how refinancing and account classification are handled separately by each agency using their own loan records, while allowing relevant financial documents to be shared on request. It also explains procedures for partial releases, subordinations, and consents on joint security instruments: both agencies must approve, only one application form is needed, the agency receiving the request leads the process, obtains needed appraisals, and follows its own authority and appraisal requirements. If both agencies cannot approve, the request is denied, and the borrower may receive the response in one combined letter or separate letters from each agency.

nodes.title: C Partial Releases, Subordinations, and Consents for Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines joint servicing procedures for loans shared by RHS and FSA. It explains how refinancing and account classification are handled separately by each agency, while financial documents may be shared upon request. It also describes the process for partial releases, subordinations, and consents on jointly secured instruments, requiring approval from both agencies and coordination by a lead agency, including appraisal requirements and denial if both cannot approve. In addition, it covers the release of joint security instruments when one agency’s debt is satisfied before the other, including document transfer, handling of promissory notes, and case file documentation. Finally, it explains how lien position is determined when RHS and FSA loans are made at the same time, noting that the lien is shared proportionally based on debt amount and referencing a guide for calculating the shared security value or proceeds.

nodes.title: D Release of Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary:
This partial document describes servicing procedures for joint RHS and FSA loans, focusing on how security instruments are released when both agencies have interests in the same loan. It explains the approval process needed from FSA and Rural Development State Offices, the steps to follow depending on which agency’s debt is satisfied first, and how promissory notes and related letters are handled and filed.

It also covers how lien position is determined when RHS and FSA loans were made at the same time and secured by the same mortgage or deed of trust. In such cases, the lien position is shared proportionately based on the amount of debt owed, with reference to a guide for calculating each agency’s share of security value or proceeds in situations such as sales, foreclosures, voluntary conveyances, or insured losses.

nodes.title: E Determining Lien Position When Both RHS and FSA Loan Were Made at the Same Time
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines general servicing procedures for cases involving both FSA and Rural Development (RHS) loans. It explains how joint security instruments are released when one agency’s debt is satisfied before the other’s, and requires proper coordination, letters of satisfaction, and file documentation. It describes how lien position is determined when RHS and FSA loans were made at the same time, with security shared proportionately based on the debt owed. It also covers how offsets are handled, including distribution by lien priority or on a prorata basis when priorities are the same, and how IRS offsets are allocated based on delinquency date or debt percentage. Finally, it addresses losses covered by insurance, including agency responsibilities for overseeing repairs, sharing inspection and development information, applying proceeds according to lien priority, and requiring both agencies to approve release of insurance proceeds.

nodes.title: F Offsets
nodes.summary: The partial document covers general servicing procedures for joint loans involving two agencies, focusing on how offsets and insurance losses are handled. It explains that offset payments are applied according to lien priority or delinquency date, with prorated distribution when priorities or dates are equal, and that IRS offset payments require review and processing by agency representatives. It also describes how losses covered by insurance are managed, including deciding which agency oversees repairs, sharing inspection and development plan information, applying insurance proceeds based on lien priority, and requiring approval from both agencies before releasing insurance proceeds for jointly secured property.

nodes.title: G Losses Covered by Insurance
nodes.summary: The document section describes servicing procedures for joint or cross-collateralized loans handled by two agencies, focusing on how offsets, insurance losses, ADPS transactions, and labor housing loans are coordinated. It explains how payments from administrative and IRS offsets are distributed based on lien priority, delinquency date, or borrower debt share, and how both agencies share responsibilities for insurance-related repairs, inspections, and approval of proceeds. It also notes that both agencies have access to inquiry screens, but certain transactions must be coordinated to avoid duplication. Finally, it outlines that each agency services its own loans, but when loans are cross-collateralized, both agencies must work together to protect the government's security interest.

nodes.title: H ADPS Inquiry Screens and Processing of Transactions
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing procedures for USDA loan programs, focusing on how the RHS and FSA Agencies handle inquiries and transaction processing under ADPS, including the need to coordinate certain transactions that affect both accounts. It also explains servicing for Labor Housing (LH) loans, noting that RHS generally manages them, but shared collateral situations require special handling of releases and liquidations. The document then begins a section on servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts, emphasizing that each Agency is normally responsible for its own loans, but both must cooperate when loans are cross-collateralized to protect the government’s security interest.

nodes.title: I Labor Housing Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document describes servicing procedures for loans managed by RHS and FSA agencies. It explains that both agencies can access inquiry screens for RHS and FCP loans, but ADPS transactions are generally handled separately unless they affect both accounts, in which case coordination is required to avoid duplicate actions and future issues. It also covers Labor Housing (LH) loans, noting they are usually serviced by the RHS official responsible for multi-family housing, except when the same security covers both FCP and LH loans, in which case releases and liquidations follow the attachment’s rules. Finally, it outlines debt servicing responsibilities for delinquent or financially distressed accounts, stating that each agency services its own loans but must cooperate when loans are cross-collateralized to protect the government’s security interest.

nodes.title: 5 Servicing Delinquent and Financially Distressed Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing procedures for USDA RHS and FSA loans, focusing on how the two agencies coordinate when managing shared or related loan accounts. It explains that both agencies have access to inquiry screens for RHS and FCP loans, but most ADPS transactions are handled separately unless they affect both accounts, such as flags, acquisitions, and name/address changes, which require coordination to avoid duplicate actions. It also notes that Labor Housing loans are generally serviced by the RHS Multi-Family Housing program, except when the same security covers both FCP and LH loans, in which case releases and liquidations follow the same rules as described for other shared-security cases. The document then begins a section on servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts, stating that each agency is responsible for its own loans, but they must work together when loans are cross-collateralized to protect the government’s security interest, especially when both loans are tied to the same property or security instruments.

nodes.title: A Debt Servicing Responsibilities
nodes.summary:
This excerpt covers servicing procedures for Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) loans, focusing on how the two agencies handle shared systems and borrower accounts. It explains that both agencies can access inquiry screens, but most ADPS transactions are processed separately unless they affect both accounts, in which case coordination is required.

It also describes handling of Labor Housing (LH) loans, noting that RHS generally services them, but special rules apply when the security is shared with FCP loans. The main section addresses delinquent and financially distressed accounts, outlining each agency’s responsibility for its own loans and the need for cooperation when loans are cross-collateralized.

The document distinguishes between cases where security is not cross-collateralized, where each agency services its loans under its own rules, and cases where security is cross-collateralized. It further separates situations where the loans are on different security instruments versus the same instrument, with the latter requiring both agencies to follow specific procedures because default on one loan affects the entire security instrument.

nodes.title: B Security Is Not Cross-Collateralized
C Security Is Cross-Collateralized and Loans Are Described on Separate Security Instruments
nodes.summary: This section explains how delinquent and financially distressed accounts are serviced when Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) loans are involved, depending on how the loan security is structured. It covers three cases: when security is not cross-collateralized, when it is cross-collateralized but the loans are on separate security instruments, and when RHS and FSA loans are cross-collateralized on the same security instrument. It states which agency procedures apply in each case, including references to specific regulations and instructions, and notes that default on one loan can create a default against the shared security instrument and result in nonmonetary default for the other agency’s loan.

nodes.title: D Security Is Cross-Collateralized and RH and FCP Loans Are Described on Same Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains servicing procedures for delinquent and financially distressed accounts involving Rural Housing (RHS), Farm Service Agency (FSA), and related loan programs, especially when security is cross-collateralized. It distinguishes between situations where loans are on separate security instruments versus the same security instrument, and sets out which agency is responsible for servicing, notifying the other agency, and handling foreclosure or conveyance actions. It also describes how defaults on one loan affect the other, how acceleration notices and appeals are handled, when foreclosure must be delayed, and how payments, flags, buyouts, and payoff information should be managed. Finally, it provides special instructions for entering and tracking RHS debt in the DALR$ system during primary loan servicing and reamortization.

nodes.title: E Special Instructions for DALR$
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts when RHS and FSA/FCP loans are tied to the same collateral or security instruments. It covers how to handle cross-collateralized accounts, including payment application, account status flags for acceleration and foreclosure, buyout requirements when an RHS loan is delinquent, and rules for releasing joint security instruments. It also gives special instructions for entering RHS debts in DALR$ calculations, including reamortization, prior-lien handling, proportional share determination, and required borrower notices. Finally, it outlines how bankruptcy cases involving joint security instruments are handled separately by each agency, with legal coordination through OGC.

nodes.title: F Bankruptcy Filings With Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts involving joint security interests between RHS and FSA. It explains how to handle DALR$ calculations when RHS has prior or junior liens, how to determine proportional shares when both agencies’ loans or security interests were made at the same time, and what statement to add if an RHS loan must be paid off during a buyout. It also states that bankruptcy filings with joint security instruments are handled separately by each agency under their own regulations, with legal coordination managed by OGC. The document then outlines how to handle foreclosures and voluntary conveyances when RHS and FSA loans are secured by separate instruments, including requirements for appraisals, title opinions, lien position review, lien payoff or release procedures, and treatment of prior versus junior liens in voluntary conveyance and foreclosure situations.

nodes.title: 6 Handling Foreclosures and Voluntary Conveyances Against Joint Security
nodes.summary: The document contains only an instruction asking to directly return a description of the partial document’s main points, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: A Loans Described on Separate Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The document describes procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security on loans held by Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA). It explains how actions differ when loans are on separate security instruments versus the same or mixed security instruments. Key points include obtaining current appraisals and title opinions, determining lien priority, requesting releases from the other agency, paying off prior or junior liens based on market value, and handling foreclosures with minimal involvement of the other agency when possible. It also outlines that third-party foreclosures are handled separately by each agency under their respective regulations, while both agencies may bid according to applicable rules.

nodes.title: B Loans Described on the Same Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security interests held by RHS and FSA. It explains how cases are handled when loans are on separate security instruments versus the same security instruments, including voluntary conveyance steps, lien evaluation, payoff or release requirements, and how proceeds or credits are applied based on lien priority. It also covers foreclosure actions initiated by either agency or a third party, assignment of notes and security interests, recording requirements, and resumption of servicing if foreclosure is canceled. In addition, the document sets rules for assigning property ID numbers by agency and outlines the ADPS 3E transaction process for property acquisitions, including required forms and submission to the St. Louis Finance Office.

nodes.title: C Assigning Property ID Numbers
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security, with a focus on two main administrative tasks:

1. **Assigning Property ID Numbers**
   - Explains that inventory property ID numbers must be unique and cannot be duplicated.
   - Requires each agency within a state to use specified number ranges:
     - **FSA:** 00001–00499
     - **RHS:** 00500–00599
   - Notes that State and county codes used are those for St. Louis Finance Office purposes.
   - Clarifies that because the State and county code is part of the ID, the same number can be reused across different counties within a state.
   - States that agency-specific numbering prevents conflicts when counties assign numbers already used by another agency.

2. **Processing ADPS 3E Transactions for Acquisitions**
   - Describes the **ADPS 3E transaction** as a combined transaction affecting both a borrower’s FCP and RHS accounts.
   - Requires the St. Louis Finance Office to process the transaction whenever property is acquired by FSA or RHS from a borrower who has both an RHS and FCP loan, even if the property is not cross-collateralized.
   - States that property cannot be acquired jointly.
   - Lists required actions for the acquiring agency:
     - Complete the **ADPS 3E Transaction Form**.
     - Fax the form, and if applicable the **Guide for Calculating the Proportionate Share of Security Value/Proceeds from Joint Mortgages**, to the St. Louis Finance Office.
   - If the security is cross-collateralized, copies must also be sent to the nonacquiring agency.

nodes.title: D Processing ADPS 3E Transaction for Acquisitions
nodes.summary: The document section covers procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security, especially when both FSA and RHS loans are involved. It explains how property ID numbers must be assigned uniquely by agency and state/county code, with separate numbering ranges for FSA and RHS to prevent duplication. It then describes the ADPS 3E acquisition transaction, which must be processed by the St. Louis Finance Office when property is acquired from a borrower with both RHS and FCP loans, including required forms and faxing instructions. Finally, it provides a calculation guide for determining the proportionate share of joint security interest, showing how to compute RHS share of market value, expenses, and the resulting debt amount to enter in DALR$ as a prior lien.

nodes.title: Guide for Calculating Proportionate Share of Joint Security Interest for DALR$
nodes.summary: This document section explains how to calculate the proportionate share of a joint security interest for DALR$, using the property’s market value minus authorized selling expenses. It provides a three-step formula to determine the RHS share of market value, the RHS share of expenses, and then the amount of debt to enter in DALR$ as RHS prior lien. It also defines the terms used, such as equal-priority debt, total unpaid RHS and FCP debt secured by the same property, and notes that debt includes unpaid principal and interest but excludes recapture.

nodes.title: Guide for Calculating the Proportionate Share of Security Value/Proceeds From Joint Mortgages
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate the proportionate share of security value or sale proceeds when FSA and RHS loans are jointly secured by the same mortgage or deed of trust. It gives step-by-step formulas for determining each agency’s share based on total unpaid debt, market value less prior liens, and liquidation or selling expenses, including notes defining what debt counts in the calculation.

It then provides instructions for completing an ADPS 3E transaction form for acquisitions involving a borrower with both FSA and RHS loans. The form section lists the required data fields such as agency name, case number, borrower name, acquisition date, loan numbers, property information, market value, amount credited, acquisition method, and related loan details, and notes that the transaction should not be processed as an ADPS 3E submission.

nodes.title: ADPS 3E Transaction
nodes.summary:
The partial document mainly covers Rural Housing Service procedures for managing escrow, taxes, insurance, losses, and payment subsidies for Section 502 loans.

Key points included:
- A form and instructions for an ADPS 3E transaction used when a borrower with both FSA and RHS loans acquires property, including detailed item-by-item coding guidance.
- Tax and insurance requirements, including tax service fees, delinquent taxes, borrower responsibilities, and when the Agency may advance funds.
- Insurance rules for security property, such as hazard, flood, and builder’s risk coverage, acceptable evidence of insurance, force-placed insurance, and handling policy changes or cancellations.
- Escrow account requirements, including who must escrow, how escrow is established for new and existing loans, disclosure statements, annual analysis, surpluses, and shortages.
- Procedures for managing insurance claims and loss proceeds, including who files claims, how proceeds are administered based on lien position and claim size, and how repairs are inspected and paid.
- Special rules for disaster situations and uninsured losses.
- Attachments listing insurance policy requirements, including approved insurers, coverage standards, mortgagee clause requirements, and a tax service fee schedule.
- The beginning of Chapter 4, introducing payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans, including an overview of subsidy types and how subsidy eligibility is determined.

nodes.title: SECTION 7: PAYMENT SUBSIDIES AND INCOME DETERMINATIONS
nodes.summary: This partial document is from Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550 and focuses on payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that payment subsidies are used to improve borrowers’ repayment ability, outlines which offices are responsible for initiating and reviewing subsidies, and introduces the two main sections of the chapter. The excerpt covers an overview of payment subsidies, noting that eligibility and subsidy amounts are based on borrower income and calculated by LoanServ. It also begins describing the first type of subsidy, interest credit, and explains that borrowers may continue receiving it if they remain eligible.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: This partial document is from Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550 and explains payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It introduces how the Agency uses payment subsidies to improve borrowers’ ability to repay loans, including who handles initial subsidy determinations and ongoing reviews. The chapter is organized into two main sections: one covering approval, renewal, and types of payment subsidies, and another covering how annual and adjusted income are calculated for subsidy purposes. The excerpt begins Section 1 by stating that payment subsidies apply only to Section 502 loans, are based on borrower income, and are calculated by LoanServ, with sample calculations intended to help servicers explain the process to borrowers. It also starts describing the first type of subsidy, interest credit, and notes that borrowers may continue receiving it if they remain eligible.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: PAYMENT SUBSIDIES [7 CFR 3550.68]
nodes.summary:
The partial document introduces Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550, which focuses on payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that payment subsidies help improve borrowers’ repayment ability and are often provided at loan origination and continued over time. The chapter outlines that the Field Office determines initial subsidy eligibility, while the Servicing and Asset Management Office handles subsidy initiation for new eligible borrowers and performs periodic reviews for those already receiving subsidies. It also previews the two main sections of the chapter: Section 1 on approval and renewal of payment subsidies, and Section 2 on calculating annual and adjusted income used to determine subsidy amounts.

The excerpt then begins Section 1, giving an overview of payment subsidies. It states that subsidies are available only for Section 502 loans and are based on borrower income. LoanServ is used to calculate the subsidy, with sample calculations included to help servicers understand and explain the process. The text also starts describing the first type of subsidy, interest credit, noting that borrowers may continue receiving it as long as they remain eligible and keep receiving it without interruption.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF PAYMENT SUBSIDIES
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces Chapter 4 on payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that the Agency uses payment subsidies to improve borrowers’ repayment ability, with field offices determining initial subsidy eligibility and the Servicing and Asset Management Office handling new subsidy initiation and periodic reviews. The chapter is divided into two main sections: one covering approval and renewal of payment subsidies, and another covering annual and adjusted income calculations used to determine subsidy amounts. It also gives an overview of payment subsidies, noting that they apply only to Section 502 loans, are based on borrower income, and are calculated by LoanServ. The excerpt begins discussing the three subsidy types, starting with interest credit and its continuation as long as the borrower remains eligible.

nodes.title: Three Types of Subsidy
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550, focusing on payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that the Agency uses payment subsidies to improve borrower repayment ability, with the Field Office determining initial subsidy eligibility and the Servicing Office handling subsidy initiation and periodic reviews.

The section outlines:
- An overview of payment subsidies and how they are based on borrower income.
- Three subsidy types: Interest Credit, Payment Assistance Method 1, and Payment Assistance Method 2.
- Rules for continuing, renewing, or converting subsidy types, including what happens if assistance lapses.
- Borrower eligibility requirements, including income limits at origination and during the loan term.
- An occupancy requirement that the borrower must personally occupy the property, with limited temporary absences allowed for acceptable reasons.

It also notes that Section 2 of the chapter will provide guidance on calculating annual and adjusted income, which are used to determine subsidy amounts.

nodes.title: Borrower Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for USDA Rural Housing payment subsidies under HB-2-3550, including how payment assistance methods are assigned and recalculated, borrower eligibility requirements, loan term requirements, and borrower reporting duties. It explains when Payment Assistance Method 1 or Method 2 applies, how eligibility depends on income and occupancy, restrictions for nonprogram or above-moderate loans, and the minimum loan term needed to qualify. It also describes annual income and household reporting obligations, notification requirements for changes in household circumstances or income, and notes special reporting/review handling for self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: Loan Requirements
nodes.summary: The document section outlines rules and conditions for payment subsidies under HB-2-3550. It explains when subsidies may continue for borrowers after loan assumption, including limitations for nonprogram, above-moderate, and pre-August 1, 1968 loans. It describes loan term requirements for subsidy eligibility, generally requiring initial or related loans to have at least a 25-year term. It also details borrower reporting responsibilities, such as annual reporting of household income, expenses, and household composition, along with notification requirements for changes in employment, household members, or income. Finally, it introduces special review timing for certain cases, beginning with self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: Borrower Reporting Requirements
nodes.summary: This section explains the rules for payment subsidies under HB-2-3550. It covers who is eligible for subsidies, including limitations for nonprogram, above-moderate, and pre-August 1, 1968 borrowers, and notes that some may instead qualify by refinancing. It also sets loan term requirements, stating that initial and certain subsequent loans must generally have at least a 25-year term to receive assistance. In addition, it outlines borrower reporting obligations: recipients must annually report household income, expenses, and composition, and notify the Agency of changes such as employment, household changes, or significant income increases. Finally, it begins discussing special review periods for certain borrowers, including self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: Self-Employed Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and procedures for USDA payment subsidies and payment assistance. It covers who is eligible, including restrictions for certain older or nonprogram loans, the minimum loan term required, and special rules for assumptions and refinance situations. It also outlines borrower reporting responsibilities, such as annual income and household updates, and exceptions for self-employed, unemployed, and annual payment borrowers. The document describes recapture requirements when a subsidized loan is paid off and the borrower leaves the property or transfers title. Finally, it introduces the calculation of payment assistance, including two methods and the basic idea behind Method 1, where assistance equals the difference between the note-rate payment and the income-based amount the borrower must pay.

nodes.title: Unemployed Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses USDA Rural Housing payment subsidy rules and how payment assistance is administered. It explains agreement periods for different borrower situations, including borrowers with annual income reviews tied to a fiscal year, unemployed borrowers whose unemployment benefits are counted as income, and borrowers with annual installment payments. It also covers the recapture requirement, stating that subsidy recapture must be repaid when a borrower no longer occupies or transfers the property, except for certain temporary absences, and notes that eligible borrowers must sign Form RD 3550-12. Finally, it introduces the calculation of payment assistance, describing two methods and beginning with Method 1, where assistance equals the difference between the note-rate payment and the borrower’s required payment based on income.

nodes.title: Annual Payment Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses USDA Rural Development payment subsidy rules and payment assistance calculations. It explains how subsidy agreements are timed for different borrower situations, including borrowers with business fiscal years, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers with annual installment payments. It also covers the recapture requirement, stating that subsidy recapture must be repaid when a property is no longer occupied or is transferred, with exceptions for temporary absences approved by the Agency. The document then introduces payment assistance calculation, describing two methods and beginning with Method 1, where assistance equals the difference between the note-rate installment and what the borrower can pay based on income.

nodes.title: Recapture Requirement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains payment subsidy rules and payment assistance procedures under HB-2-3550. It covers how subsidy review periods are aligned with a borrower’s business fiscal year, special rules for unemployed borrowers and annual payment borrowers, and limits on agreement terms. It also describes the subsidy recapture requirement, including when repayment is triggered and the need to sign Form RD 3550-12. Finally, it introduces payment assistance calculation, distinguishing between two methods and beginning the explanation of Method 1, where assistance is the difference between the note-rate installment and the amount the borrower must pay based on income.

nodes.title: CALCULATING PAYMENT ASSISTANCE
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains rules for payment subsidies and payment assistance in a housing loan program. It covers how subsidy agreement terms are set for different borrower situations, including borrowers with fiscal-year income documentation, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers with annual installment payments. It also describes the subsidy recapture requirement, which applies when a loan is fully paid and the borrower moves out or transfers title, and notes that borrowers must sign a specific form when first qualifying for subsidy. Finally, it introduces payment assistance calculation, outlining two methods and detailing Method 1, where assistance equals the difference between the scheduled note payment and what the borrower can afford based on income, subject to minimum payment rules.

nodes.title: Payment Assistance Method 1
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate payment assistance for a loan under HB-2-3550. It describes two main methods used to determine the borrower’s required payment: a payment based on the Equivalent Interest Rate (EIR) and a floor payment based on a percentage of adjusted income, minus taxes and insurance. It includes an example using the Jones family to show step-by-step calculations for payment at the note rate, floor payment, payment at the EIR, and the resulting monthly payment assistance.

The document also provides a table of equivalent interest rates tied to ranges of adjusted income as a percentage of median income, with rules that the EIR cannot be below 1% or above the note rate. In addition, it outlines the floor payment percentages for different borrower income categories, including special treatment for very low-income and low-income borrowers, and notes that leveraged loans are excluded from the floor payment rule.

nodes.title: Calculating the Payment at the Equivalent Interest Rate
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate payment assistance in two parts. First, it describes calculating the payment at the equivalent interest rate using the borrower’s loan amount, loan term, and an income-based equivalent interest rate, with a table showing the rates tied to different adjusted median income ranges and noting that the rate cannot exceed the note rate or fall below 1 percent. Second, it explains how to calculate the floor payment, requiring borrowers—except those with leveraged loans—to pay minimum PITI amounts based on income: 22% for very low-income borrowers, 24% for low-income borrowers at or below 65% of median income, and 26% for borrowers above 65% of median income.

nodes.title: Calculating the Floor Payment
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate payment assistance in two ways. First, it describes calculating the payment using an equivalent interest rate based on the borrower’s adjusted annual income and provides a table of income ranges with corresponding interest rates, noting that the rate cannot exceed the note rate or be less than 1 percent. Second, it explains how to calculate the floor payment, which is the minimum required principal, interest, taxes, and insurance (PITI) payment for most borrowers, set at 22%, 24%, or 26% of adjusted income depending on income level.

nodes.title: Payment Assistance Method 2
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate and apply USDA Rural Housing Service payment assistance and interest credit. It details Payment Assistance Method 2, including the formula for determining subsidy amounts based on the borrower’s adjusted income, loan payments, taxes, insurance, and an alternate 1% interest-rate payment. It also gives a worked example showing how to compute the borrower’s monthly subsidy, total PITI, and RHS payment. In addition, the document outlines how interest credit is calculated, describing the minimum borrower payment rules. Finally, it covers eligibility requirements and procedures for providing Payment Assistance Method 2 to borrowers who are not currently receiving a subsidy, including income limits, occupancy requirements, and restrictions on certain older or nonprogram loans.

nodes.title: CALCULATING INTEREST CREDIT
nodes.summary: The document section explains how to calculate payment assistance, focusing on interest credit and eligibility rules for borrowers who are not currently receiving a subsidy. It states that interest credit is the difference between the loan payment at the note rate and the borrower’s minimum required payment, which must be at least the greater of 20% of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance or a payment based on a 1% interest rate. It also describes “Payment Assistance Method 2,” noting that borrowers may become eligible due to reduced income and may request assistance or be identified by the servicer. To qualify, the borrower must have income at or below the low-income limit and occupy the home, and the assistance cannot be used with certain older or nonprogram loans, though refinancing may make assistance possible.

nodes.title: PROVIDING PAYMENT ASSISTANCE METHOD 2 TO BORROWERS NOT CURRENTLY RECEIVING A PAYMENT SUBSIDY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance is calculated and provided under a housing loan program. It defines how interest credit is determined for eligible borrowers, based on the difference between the note-rate payment and a minimum required payment, with the borrower required to pay the greater of 20% of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance or a payment amortized at 1% interest. It also describes “Payment Assistance Method 2” for borrowers not currently receiving a subsidy, noting that assistance may be requested or identified by the servicer when a borrower’s income decreases. To qualify, the borrower must meet low-income limits and occupy the dwelling, and assistance is not available for certain older or nonprogram loans unless they are refinanced into a new eligible loan.

nodes.title: Eligibility
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how payment assistance and interest credit are calculated and administered for eligible borrowers under the program. It covers:

- How to calculate interest credit based on the difference between the loan payment at the note rate and the minimum borrower payment requirement.
- Eligibility rules for borrowers not currently receiving a payment subsidy, including income limits, owner-occupancy, and restrictions on certain older or nonprogram loans.
- A detailed sample interest credit calculation showing how monthly payment assistance is derived.
- The process for requesting and approving payment assistance, including borrower forms, servicer review, LoanServ calculations, and required agreements.
- When payment assistance becomes effective after approval.
- Annual review requirements for subsidy agreements, including income change checks and renewal procedures to maintain continued eligibility.

nodes.title: Processing Requests for Payment Assistance
nodes.summary:
The document explains how payment assistance subsidies are processed for borrowers who are not currently receiving assistance. It covers the steps for determining eligibility, including sending Form RD 3550-21, using LoanServ to calculate the subsidy amount, and issuing Form RD 1944-14 along with Form RD 3550-12 when needed. It also explains that borrowers must repay subsidy under certain conditions such as selling, refinancing, moving out, or paying off the loan.  

The document then describes when payment assistance becomes effective, which is on the due date after the servicer receives and processes the signed documents.  

Finally, it outlines annual review procedures for subsidy agreements lasting more than 12 months. These reviews check whether the borrower still qualifies, especially if income has changed by 10 percent or more, and require verification of updated information. The review process begins before the agreement anniversary and is intended to be completed before the current subsidy expires so a new subsidy amount can take effect on time.

nodes.title: Effective Date of Payment Assistance
nodes.summary: The document explains how payment assistance subsidy is processed and reviewed for borrowers. It covers how the servicer determines eligibility by sending Form RD 3550-21, calculating assistance through LoanServ, and issuing Form RD 1944-14 along with a subsidy repayment agreement if needed. It also states when new payment assistance becomes effective. In addition, it describes annual review procedures for subsidy agreements lasting more than 12 months, including borrower notification, income-change reporting, verification of updated information, and timing requirements to renew assistance before the current agreement expires.

nodes.title: ANNUAL AND INTERIM REVIEWS OF PAYMENT SUBSIDIES
nodes.summary: The document explains how payment assistance is processed for borrowers who are not currently receiving a subsidy. It describes the servicer’s steps for determining eligibility, sending and using required forms, calculating assistance through LoanServ, and notifying eligible borrowers, including the repayment agreement requirements if one has not already been signed. It also states when the new subsidy becomes effective. In addition, the document covers annual reviews of payment subsidies, explaining that agreements can last up to 24 months, require yearly review when longer than 12 months, and are updated if the borrower reports a significant income change, with renewal completed before the current agreement expires.

nodes.title: Annual Reviews
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how payment assistance subsidies are processed and reviewed for borrowers who are not currently receiving a subsidy. It covers the servicer’s steps for determining eligibility, sending forms, computing assistance, and notifying eligible borrowers, including the requirement for a subsidy repayment agreement in certain cases. It also describes when new payment assistance becomes effective.

The document then outlines the annual review process for subsidy agreements lasting more than 12 months, including borrower notification, income verification, use of LoanServ to recalculate assistance, and adjustment of monthly payments. Finally, it explains interim reviews, which are triggered by changes in household employment, composition, or income, and describes how payment subsidy changes are handled, including notice requirements and timing for increases or decreases in monthly payments.

nodes.title: Interim Reviews
nodes.summary: The document section explains how payment subsidy reviews are handled for borrowers, including annual renewals and interim reviews. It describes the renewal process through LoanServ and Form RD 3550-21, verification of income and household information, and notification of the borrower’s required monthly payment. It also covers interim review rules when household circumstances change, including when borrowers must report changes, how subsidy adjustments are calculated, and when payment changes take effect. In addition, it outlines in-depth quality control reviews using IRS Form 4506-T for random or questionable cases, and notes special handling for borrowers with Trust loans to coordinate subsidy calculations and avoid duplication.

nodes.title: In-Depth Reviews
nodes.summary: The document section covers annual and interim reviews of payment subsidies, focusing on in-depth income verification reviews conducted by the Servicing Office. It explains that a random 1% of renewals are subject to quality control checks, and additional reviews are required when borrower information appears inaccurate or conflicts with other sources. It also notes that IRS Form 4506-T is used to independently verify tax information for all adult household members selected for review, and that cases involving unauthorized assistance may also be reviewed. Finally, it addresses Trust loan borrowers, stating that the Trust loan servicer must be notified when payment subsidies are processed so combined calculations are accurate and duplication of effort is avoided.

nodes.title: Trust Loan Borrowers
nodes.summary: The document section covers procedures for reviewing and verifying borrower income related to payment subsidies in USDA Rural Housing servicing. It explains that the Servicing Office performs random in-depth quality control reviews of at least 1% of renewals, uses IRS Form 4506-T to independently verify tax information, and may review cases with suspected inaccuracies or unauthorized assistance. It also notes special handling for borrowers with Trust loans to prevent duplication and ensure correct subsidy calculations. The next section begins an overview of evaluating borrower income, emphasizing that income information is used to determine subsidy eligibility and amount. It introduces key income concepts and definitions, especially the distinction between repayment income and adjusted income, and explains that annual income includes a broad range of household earnings and asset-related income used to assess eligibility.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: EVALUATING BORROWER INCOME [7 CFR 3550.53(a) AND (g), 7 CFR 3550.54]
nodes.summary: This section explains how the Loan Servicer evaluates borrower income to determine eligibility for payment subsidy and the amount of subsidy. It introduces the key income concepts used by the Agency, including the difference between repayment income and adjusted income, and notes that repayment income is used only for moratorium or reamortization decisions based on the note parties’ income. The section also defines annual income as the starting point for subsidy eligibility, describing it as all income received by or on behalf of household members, including amounts from outside the family and income from accessible assets, except for amounts specifically excluded by regulation.

nodes.title: Overview
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Loan Servicer evaluates borrower income to determine eligibility for payment subsidies and the amount of assistance. It focuses on the rules for verifying and calculating income, referencing USDA housing regulations and HUD guidance. The section introduces key concepts for income determinations, especially the different income definitions used by the Agency. It distinguishes between repayment income, which is used only for servicing decisions like moratorium or reamortization and includes only parties to the note, and adjusted income, which is used to determine subsidy eligibility and is based on annual income minus allowable household deductions. It also defines annual income broadly as all monetary and non-monetary income received by household members, expected income over the next 12 months, and income from assets, except amounts specifically excluded by regulation.

nodes.title: Key Concepts for Income Determinations
nodes.summary:
This section explains how the Loan Servicer evaluates borrower income to determine eligibility for payment subsidy and the amount of subsidy. It introduces key income concepts and emphasizes using the correct income definition for each purpose. The main points are:

- **Purpose of income review:** Income information is used to decide whether a borrower qualifies for payment subsidy and how much subsidy applies.
- **Different income definitions:** The Agency uses three income types, and the correct one must be applied depending on the situation.
  - **Repayment income** is used only for servicing decisions such as moratorium or reamortization eligibility, and it includes only the income of the parties to the note.
  - **Adjusted income** is used to determine subsidy eligibility and is calculated by first determining annual income and then subtracting allowable deductions.
  - **Annual income** includes all monetary and non-monetary income received by household members, with certain exclusions allowed by regulation.
- **Household member income consideration:** Income determinations must include the appropriate household members depending on the income type being evaluated.
- **Calculation framework:** Annual income is based on expected income over a 12-month period, including applicable asset-derived income, and then adjusted income is derived after deductions.

nodes.title: Income Definitions
nodes.summary: The document explains how a loan servicer should evaluate borrower income for payment subsidy eligibility and related servicing decisions. It introduces the main income definitions used by the agency—annual income, adjusted income, and repayment income—and explains the purpose of each. Annual income is the broad measure used as the starting point, adjusted income is annual income minus certain deductions to determine subsidy eligibility, and repayment income is used only during servicing to assess a borrower’s ability to make payments and eligibility for moratorium or reamortization. The section also clarifies whose income must be counted: repayment income includes only parties to the note, while adjusted income includes all household members, excluding live-in aides, foster children, and foster adults. It further addresses how to treat the income of a permanently confined household member, allowing the family to either include that person’s income with related medical deductions or exclude it entirely.

nodes.title: Whose Income To Count
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how income is determined for USDA Rural Development housing programs, especially Section 502 and 504. It distinguishes between two key income calculations: Adjusted Income, used to decide income eligibility for payment assistance, and Repayment Income, used to assess a borrower’s ability to make monthly loan payments and for certain servicing actions such as moratorium or reamortization.
