It also describes whose income must be counted. For repayment income, only household members who are parties to the note are included; for adjusted income, the income of all household members is counted. Live-in aides, foster children, and foster adults are excluded as household members for both calculations. The document notes a special rule for permanently confined family members, whose income may either be included with related medical deductions or excluded entirely, depending on the family’s choice.

Finally, the excerpt introduces income limits used to determine eligibility categories, explains that these limits are set and updated by the National Office, and includes Exhibit 4-5, a table showing which types of income are counted for different household members, including borrowers, spouses, dependents, students, foster children, foster adults, and live-in aides.

nodes.title: Income Limits
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how income is handled for USDA Section 502 and 504 housing programs, with emphasis on determining household income for eligibility and payment assistance. It covers:

- The different income limits used in the program:
  - Very low-income limit
  - Low-income limit
  - Moderate-income limit
- How adjusted income is compared to these limits to determine household category.
- Eligibility for payment assistance based on income limits, including that some borrowers already receiving subsidy may not have to meet the income threshold.

It also includes a table showing which household members’ income must be counted, including:
- Borrower, co-borrower, spouse, other adults
- Rules for dependents, full-time students, permanently confined family members
- Non-members such as foster children, foster adults, and live-in aides

The document then explains major types of income and how to judge whether income is stable and dependable:
- Wage and salary income
- Self-employment income
- Other sources of income such as public assistance, child support, alimony, and retirement
- Seasonal income
- Cases with less than two years of work history

Several examples illustrate how to classify income in real situations, such as stable job changes, irregular self-employment, temporary or sporadic work, and recent employment after a long absence.

Finally, it briefly describes the use of LoanServ and an income worksheet to calculate income, and begins section 4.8 on sources and verification of income, including what income sources count for annual income and repayment income.

nodes.title: Using LoanServ and the Income Worksheet to Compute Income
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how income is calculated and verified for loan servicing. It describes the use of LoanServ and an income worksheet to compute income, including keeping a copy in the borrower’s file if the worksheet is used. It then outlines sources of income considered for annual income and repayment income, emphasizing that annual income includes income attributable to any household member, while repayment income includes dependable income attributable to the parties to the note. The document begins listing specific income types, such as gross wages, salaries, overtime, commissions, tips, bonuses, housing allowances, and other compensation for personal services, and notes special rules such as counting only the first $480 of earned income from certain adult full-time students for annual income.

nodes.title: SOURCES & VERIFICATION OF INCOME
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how Rural Development/LoanServ calculates borrower income and how income sources are counted for annual income and repayment income determinations. It covers use of the income worksheet, required filing of the worksheet in the borrower’s file, and detailed rules for including or excluding various income types such as wages, business income, investment income, Social Security, benefits, public assistance, alimony, gifts, military pay, housing assistance, SNAP, and other exempt payments. It also lists many income sources that are never counted, such as foster care payments, temporary or nonrecurring income, certain reimbursements, reverse mortgage payments, and specific federal exclusions. Finally, it describes verification requirements, including borrower-provided tax returns and pay stubs, authorization forms, third-party written or oral verification, and acceptable documentation for wages and salaries.

nodes.title: WAGES or SALARIES
nodes.summary: This partial document describes income verification requirements for wages and salaries under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, Sources & Verification of Income. It explains preferred and acceptable alternative methods to verify employment and earnings for household members, including tax returns, pay stubs, employer verification forms, electronic records, and oral verification. It specifies what information each verification source must contain, such as employee identification, gross earnings, year-to-date income, and employer contact details.

nodes.title: SELF-EMPLOYMENT
nodes.summary:
This section of HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, explains the sources and verification requirements for different types of income used in borrower eligibility determinations. It covers self-employment income, seasonal employment, unemployment and unemployment benefits, disability and workers’ compensation, severance pay, and regular unearned income such as Social Security, SSI, retirement, pensions, annuities, and death benefits.  

For each income type, the document identifies a preferred verification source and acceptable alternatives. Common acceptable documents include recent tax returns and schedules, income and expense statements, profit and loss statements, award or benefit letters, bank statements, checks, W-2s, 1099s, and electronic verification records. It also explains how the Loan Servicer should compare submitted information, resolve discrepancies, and use IRS Form 4506-T when tax returns are unavailable.  

The main focus is ensuring income is documented accurately, using the most current evidence available, and adjusting certain verified amounts, such as adding back deductions or Medicare deductions where required.

nodes.title: SUPPLEMENTAL VERIFICATION
nodes.summary: The partial document is a section of USDA HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, covering sources and verification of income. It outlines supplemental verification requirements for different types of income, including seasonal employment, unemployment benefits, disability, workers’ compensation, severance pay, and regular unearned income such as Social Security, SSI, pensions, annuities, and death benefits. For each income type, it identifies preferred and acceptable alternative verification sources, such as tax returns, W-2s, 1098s, IRS Form 4506-T, employer contact, award or benefit letters, electronic verification, checks, bank statements, and SSA forms. It also specifies how to handle gross income calculations, deductions, COLA updates, and documentation requirements when verifying fluctuating or ongoing benefit income.

nodes.title: Seasonal Employment
nodes.summary: The document section from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, explains sources and verification requirements for different types of income, with emphasis on supplemental verification. It covers how to verify seasonal employment income using recent tax returns, W-2s, 1098s, or IRS Form 4506-T. It also describes verification for unemployment, disability, worker’s compensation, and severance pay through employer contact, agency award letters, or electronic records such as checks and bank statements. In addition, it outlines verification rules for regular unearned income like Social Security, SSI, retirement funds, pensions, annuities, and disability or death benefits, requiring recent award or benefit letters and alternative proof such as bank statements, 1099 forms, or updated benefit notices.

nodes.title: Unemployment and Unemployment Benefits, Disability & Worker’s Compensation, Severance Pay (except lump-sum additions)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines verification requirements for different types of household income under Paragraph 4.8, “Sources & Verification of Income.” It focuses on supplemental verification procedures for seasonal employment, unemployment and related benefits, and regular unearned income such as Social Security, SSI, retirement, pensions, annuities, disability, or death benefits. For each income type, it identifies preferred verification sources, acceptable alternative documents, and key rules such as using tax returns, W-2s, award letters, benefit notices, bank statements, or electronic verification. It also explains how to confirm unemployment status through a former employer, how to verify benefits when letters are outdated, and how to adjust checks or statements to reflect gross income by adding back deductions like Medicare or other authorized withholdings.

nodes.title: Regular, Unearned Income (e.g., Social Security (SS), SSI, Retirement Funds, Pensions, Annuities, Disability or Death Benefits) (except deferred periodic payments)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers requirements for verifying different types of household income under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8. It explains preferred and acceptable alternative verification sources and procedures for supplemental verification of income from seasonal employment, unemployment benefits, disability, worker’s compensation, severance pay, regular unearned income such as Social Security and pensions, alimony or child support, foster care payments, and assets/investment income. The text details what documents are needed, such as tax returns, W-2s, award letters, benefit statements, bank statements, checks, divorce decrees, and court payment histories, and notes how to handle special cases like fluctuating SSI income, Medicare deductions, and adding back deductions to show gross income.

nodes.title: Alimony or Child Support Payments
nodes.summary: The partial document covers verification of income sources and the acceptable documentation needed to calculate household income. It focuses on supplemental verification for alimony and child support, including preferred and alternative sources such as court payment histories, divorce decrees, separation agreements, cancelled checks, or borrower certifications when formal records are unavailable. It also addresses income from foster children or adults, requiring proof of payments received and the expected duration of support. In addition, it explains how to verify income from assets and investments using year-end statements, recent bank statements, or electronic tax form printouts showing interest, dividends, and capital gains.

nodes.title: Support for Foster Children or Adults
nodes.summary: The document section outlines methods for verifying supplemental income sources and the documentation required for each type. It covers alimony and child support payments, including preferred verification from a court-appointed payment entity and alternative proofs such as divorce decrees, separation agreements, or cancelled checks. It also explains how foster child or foster adult care payments may be counted as income when supported by documentation showing payment amounts and expected duration. In addition, it describes verification of income from assets and investments, noting preferred sources like year-end statements and alternatives such as recent bank statements or tax forms showing interest, dividends, and capital gains.

nodes.title: Verification of Assets and Income from Assets and Investments
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines verification requirements for supplemental sources of income under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8. It explains how to verify alimony and child support payments, including preferred evidence from a court-appointed payment history, alternative proof such as divorce decrees or separation agreements, and procedures for documenting unpaid or partially paid support. It also covers income from foster child or foster adult support, requiring documentation of the amount received and expected duration. In addition, it describes how to verify income from assets and investments, noting preferred sources like year-end statements and acceptable alternatives such as recent bank statements or tax forms showing interest, dividends, and capital gains.

nodes.title: DEDUCTIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the verification of income-related deductions under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, focusing on three types of allowable deductions: disability assistance expenses, medical expenses, and child-care expenses. It outlines the required verification sources and procedures for each.

For disability assistance expenses, the borrower must explain the expense, provide proof of the cost, and show that the expense allows a family member to work. Disability is verified using Form RD 1944-4, with Form RD 3550-21 accepted as an alternative.

For medical expenses, the document notes that these deductions apply only to elderly households. Medical expense documentation is generally not required for non-elderly households unless they have unreimbursed medical expenses above 3 percent of annual income, in which case verification is needed. Form RD 3550-21 may also be used as an alternative.

For child-care expenses, the borrower must show that the expenses are reasonable and necessary for a household member to work or attend school. The document lists specific information that must be verified, including the children receiving care, the adult enabled to work or study, the absence of another adult to provide care, and details about the provider, hours, and costs. Form RD 3550-21 is again accepted as an alternative verification form.

nodes.title: Disability Assistance Expenses
nodes.summary: The partial document covers verification of income deductions under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, focusing on three types of deductible expenses: disability assistance expenses, medical expenses, and child-care expenses. It explains the preferred and acceptable alternative verification sources for each deduction and outlines the information borrowers must provide to qualify. For disability assistance expenses, the borrower must describe the expense, document the cost, and show that it enables a household member to work, using Form RD 1944-4 or the alternative Form RD 3550-21. For medical expenses, the document states that these are generally deductible only for elderly households, but non-elderly households with unreimbursed expenses above 3 percent of annual income may need verification for an exception to asset limits; Form RD 3550-21 is the alternative form. For child-care expenses, the borrower must identify the children, the working or schooling household member, the child-care provider, hours, and costs, and provide proof of enrollment if applicable, again with Form RD 3550-21 as the alternative.

nodes.title: Medical Expenses
nodes.summary: The document section covers sources and verification requirements for income deductions under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8. It explains how to verify three types of deductible expenses: disability assistance expenses, medical expenses, and child-care expenses. For disability assistance, the borrower must describe the expense, document the cost, and show it enables a family member to work, with Form RD 1944-4 or, alternatively, a signed Form RD 3550-21 used for verification. For medical expenses, the document notes that only elderly households may deduct allowable medical expenses from annual income, while non-elderly households generally do not need to document them unless claiming an exception related to asset limits due to unreimbursed medical expenses; Form RD 3550-21 is the acceptable alternative. For child-care expenses, the borrower must show the need for care, identify the children and the adult enabled to work or attend school, confirm no other adult household member can provide care, and provide provider, schedule, and cost information, with Form RD 3550-21 again serving as the alternative verification form.

nodes.title: Child-Care Expenses
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains verification requirements for deductible expenses and how to project income for loan servicing purposes.

Main points covered:
- **Disability assistance expenses:** These can be deducted if the borrower explains the expense, provides cost documentation, and shows it helps a family member work. Disability can be verified with Form RD 1944-4, or alternatively with a signed Form RD 3550-21.
- **Medical expenses:** For elderly households, allowable medical expenses may be deducted from annual income. Non-elderly households usually do not need to verify medical expenses unless they have unreimbursed medical expenses above 3% of annual income, in which case verification is required. Form RD 3550-21 may be used as an alternative.
- **Child-care expenses:** Reasonable child-care costs may be deducted if the borrower identifies the children, the working or schooling family member benefiting from care, shows no adult household member is available to provide care, identifies the provider and costs, and provides enrollment documentation if relevant. Form RD 3550-21 is an alternative verification method.
- **Projecting expected income for the next 12 months:** After verifying income, the loan servicer must estimate future income using applicable calculation methods and compare them to determine the most representative figure.
- **Income calculation methods described:**  
  1. Straight-line annualization  
  2. Averaging recent income  
  3. Year-to-date (YTD) gross earnings calculation  
  4. Historical income from the prior year’s tax return
- **Selection of projected income:** The servicer should choose the figure most likely to represent income over the next 12 months; if results differ significantly, an average may be used. Simply choosing the lowest figure without analysis is not allowed, and additional verification may be needed.

nodes.title: F. Projecting Expected Income for the Next 12 Months
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a Loan Servicer should project a verified income source for the next 12 months under HB-2-3550, using multiple calculation methods and choosing the figure most representative of expected income. It outlines four acceptable methods: straight-based, average, year-to-date (YTD), and historical. The document emphasizes that projections should be based on analysis of all applicable methods, that conservative underestimation without justification is not acceptable, and that additional verification may be needed if figures differ significantly. It also provides examples showing how to apply the methods to a borrower’s wages and overtime, when certain methods are or are not appropriate, and how changes in income circumstances can affect the chosen projection. Finally, it notes that the calculations must be documented in the case file and mentions a web-based worksheet tool.

nodes.title: G. Income of Temporarily Absent Family Members
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to treat income from temporarily absent household members when calculating annual and repayment income, including examples for workers away from home, disabled members in rehabilitation, temporary employment, and military duty. It then covers wage matching procedures for verifying income in certain states, including who must be included and how discrepancies are handled. Finally, it introduces the rules for calculating annual and adjusted income, noting that adjusted income is derived by subtracting allowable deductions such as dependent, child care, elderly household medical, and disability assistance expenses, with eligibility varying by household type.

nodes.title: H. Wage Matching
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing income verification and calculation procedures. It covers wage matching, including when and how the Loan Originator must use it, which household members to include, and how to handle discrepancies between reported income and wage match results. It then introduces the rules for calculating annual income and adjusted income, emphasizing that all household members’ income must be counted. Finally, it outlines the purpose of adjusted income for subsidy eligibility and begins describing allowable income deductions, such as dependent deductions, child care expenses, elderly household deductions, medical expenses, and disability assistance.

nodes.title: 4.9 Calculating ANNUAL AND ADJUSTED Income [7 CFR 3550.54 (b) AND (c)]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA/Rural Housing income verification and calculation rules. It explains wage matching as a method to verify household income in states where the Agency has authority, including requirements to check all adult household members and how to handle discrepancies or limited state resources. It then introduces how annual income and adjusted income are calculated for eligibility and subsidy purposes, emphasizing that income from all household members must be considered. The excerpt outlines allowable deductions from annual income, focusing on the dependent deduction and child care expenses. It defines who can qualify as a dependent, notes special custody situations, and explains when unreimbursed child care costs can be deducted, including limits related to work, job search, schooling, and full-time students.

nodes.title: B. Dependent Deduction
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how annual income is adjusted for housing eligibility by outlining two key deductions:

- **Dependent deduction:** A $480 deduction is allowed for each qualifying household dependent. It defines who can and cannot be counted as a dependent, including age and status rules, and clarifies special situations such as shared custody disputes and required documentation.
- **Child care expenses:** Reasonable unreimbursed child care costs for children age 12 or younger may be deducted if they allow a household member to work, seek employment, or attend school, and if no other adult can provide care. It also notes limits on deductions, including restrictions when the caregiver is a household member, when expenses exceed earned income, and special rules for full-time students and minors not living in the household.

nodes.title: C. Child Care Expenses
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains how to calculate annual and adjusted income under HB-2-3550, focusing on several household deductions.

Main points covered:
- **Dependent Deduction:** A household may deduct $480 for each qualifying dependent. It defines who can and cannot be counted as a dependent, including rules for borrowers, spouses, foster children, unborn children, and live-in aides. It also explains how custody disputes are handled.
- **Child Care Expenses:** Reasonable unreimbursed child care costs for children age 12 and under may be deducted if the care allows a household member to work, seek employment, or attend school, provided no other adult is available and the deduction does not exceed earnings when tied to work. It also gives documentation and verification requirements.
- **Verification of Child Care:** The document lists what the borrower must show to claim the deduction, such as identifying the child, the household member enabled to work or attend school, the provider, costs, and proof the expense is not reimbursed.
- **Elderly Household Deduction:** A single $400 deduction is allowed for an elderly household, defined as one with a note party age 62 or older or a person with a disability. It also describes when a surviving household can continue to be treated as elderly after the death of a qualifying member.
- **Examples:** The text includes examples showing how child care deductions are calculated, including separating costs for work and school and limiting deductions based on earnings.

nodes.title: D. Elderly Household Deduction
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for calculating annual and adjusted income in the USDA HB-2-3550 handbook, focusing on two deduction types and one example. It describes the elderly household deduction, including the $400 family deduction, who qualifies as an elderly or disabled household, and when a surviving household may continue to be treated as elderly after a death. It then provides a childcare deduction example showing how costs are allocated between time worked and time spent at school, and how deductions are limited by earned income during work hours. Finally, it outlines the disability assistance expense deduction, covering eligible attendant care or auxiliary apparatus costs, documentation and verification requirements, and the conditions under which such expenses may be deducted from income.

nodes.title: E. Deduction for Disability Assistance Expense
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate annual and adjusted income by detailing two key deductions: disability assistance expenses and medical expenses.

It first describes the deduction for disability assistance expenses, which allows families to deduct unreimbursed, necessary costs for attendant care and auxiliary apparatus for a disabled family member, but only when the expenses enable the person or another adult household member to work. The document outlines eligibility rules, documentation requirements, the 3 percent of annual income threshold, and limits on the deduction. It also notes the need to verify the disability and includes examples such as motorized wheelchairs, specially equipped vans, and attendant care services.

Next, it covers the deduction for medical expenses, which applies only to elderly households. These expenses must also be unreimbursed and, when combined with disability assistance expenses, must exceed 3 percent of annual income. The document explains that all family members’ medical expenses may be counted and provides lists of typical deductible medical expenses, such as physician services, prescriptions, dental care, eyeglasses, medical equipment, and travel for treatment, as well as examples of excluded expenses like cosmetic surgery, health club dues, and nonprescription drugs unless prescribed.

nodes.title: F. Deduction for Medical Expenses (for Elderly Households Only)
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate annual and adjusted income under USDA loan rules, focusing on three main topics:

- **Medical expense deduction for elderly households**: It describes when medical expenses can be deducted, how the 3% of annual income threshold works, what household members’ expenses may be counted, and gives examples of typical medical expenses versus excluded expenses.
- **Estimating future medical expenses**: It notes that loan servicers should use realistic, historically based estimates for anticipated medical costs and only count amounts likely to be paid in the coming year.
- **Repayment income and income from assets**: It explains how repayment income is calculated for borrower parties to the note, including “grossing up” certain nontaxable income sources, and introduces how income from household assets is considered in annual income calculations.

The document also includes worked examples showing how to calculate the medical expense deduction, how to handle medical bills paid over time, and how to gross up nontaxable income.

nodes.title: 4.10 Calculating Repayment Income
nodes.summary:
The document section explains how to calculate repayment income and income from assets for loan servicing purposes. It states that repayment income should include only the income of persons who will be on the note, and that nontaxable income may need to be adjusted by “grossing up” to account for tax benefits. It gives examples of types of nontaxable income and notes that some income, such as child support, must be stable and expected to continue for at least two years.  

It also covers how income from assets is evaluated, including when borrower assets are reviewed during origination and later when borrowers request new or renewed payment subsidy. The section references forms used to verify deposits, pensions, annuities, and asset disposition, and notes that all household members’ assets are considered when computing annual income. An example is provided showing how a borrower’s repayment income is recalculated after grossing up nontaxable income.

nodes.title: 4.11 Calculating Income from Assets
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate repayment income and income from assets for loan eligibility. It states that repayment income includes only the income of people who will be on the note, and that certain nontaxable income must be “grossed up” by 120 percent to reflect tax advantages. It then describes how to determine income from assets, including what information is needed, when asset income is considered, and which household assets count or do not count in annual income. The document also gives examples illustrating the grossing-up of nontaxable income and identifying business assets that are excluded, and it lists categories of included and excluded assets such as savings, investments, retirement accounts, personal property, business assets, trusts, and special accounts.

nodes.title: Types of Assets
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate income from assets and which assets should or should not be counted in that process. It gives an example showing that assets used in an active business are excluded, and it lists common asset categories such as savings, investments, retirement accounts, trusts, lump-sum receipts, foreign assets, and personal property held as investments. It also identifies assets that are not considered, including necessary personal property, business/farm assets, irrevocable or uncontrolled trusts, restricted Indian land interests, certain health and college savings accounts, and assets intended to reduce the loan amount. The section then provides examples involving IRA and Keogh account withdrawals, illustrating when withdrawals are not treated as income and how retirement accounts are still counted as assets for income calculation purposes.

nodes.title: Example – Withdrawals from IRAs or 401K Accounts
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Housing Service/USDA loan servicing guidance, focusing on how borrower income from assets is calculated and how special servicing and liquidation are handled when borrowers fall behind or face financial hardship.

Main points include:
- Examples for counting or excluding withdrawals from retirement accounts, such as IRAs, 401(k)s, and Keogh accounts, in income calculations.
- A worksheet reference for computing income.
- An overview of special servicing tools used to help delinquent borrowers stay current, including:
  - regular counseling and follow-up,
  - delinquency workout agreements,
  - protective advances,
  - payment assistance,
  - moratoriums.
- Rules for when an account is considered past due and when liquidation can begin without further servicing.
- Guidance on identifying special servicing cases early and developing individualized servicing strategies.
- Special procedures for borrowers who have filed bankruptcy, including handling by the Office of General Counsel, proof of claim requirements, and treatment under Chapters 7 and 13.
- Use and purpose of the problem case report as a living document to track borrower status, servicing actions, and liquidation recommendations.
- Detailed rules for delinquency workout agreements, including eligibility, payment calculations, late fee treatment, cancellation, and reamortization.
- How payment assistance can be used with other servicing actions, and when refinancing may be needed to make payment assistance possible.
- Protective advances for taxes, insurance, repairs, and other actions needed to protect the Government’s interest.
- Moratoriums, including eligibility, request and review procedures, monitoring, cancellation, and reamortization after the moratorium ends.
- The decision process for recommending liquidation when servicing options are no longer effective.
- An overview of liquidation options, especially voluntary liquidation through sale of the property, including short sales, debt settlement packages, authorized selling expenses, and handling of remaining debt.

nodes.title: SECTION 8: LIQUIDATION AND DEBT SETTLEMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction asking to directly return a description of the document’s main points, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: Paragraph 6.1 Overview of Liquidation Options
nodes.summary:
This document section explains the liquidation options process when sale proceeds will not fully cover the borrower’s debt. It describes how the Servicing Office determines whether the borrower can be released from personal liability, based on a completed Debt Settlement Package reviewed and approved by the office. It notes that the package must generally be submitted at or before loan closing for sales that are less than the debt, and that sale proceeds must be sent by certified or cashier’s check.

The text also outlines exceptions when the borrower cannot or will not complete the package, allowing the Field Office to release the mortgage if it is in the Agency’s best interest, such as when the borrower has moved away or delaying closing would reduce recovery. It explains how sale proceeds and related documents are transmitted, how the Field Office records transaction details in the system, and how the Servicing Office notifies the Field Office after proceeds are posted or an assumption is processed.

Finally, the document lists the required contents of a Debt Settlement Package, including specific USDA forms, bank statements, income verification, tax returns, a net recovery worksheet if needed, estimated selling expenses, and any other relevant information. It also describes how the Field Office or Servicing Office assembles and submits this package, including fax procedures for emergency debt settlement cases.

nodes.title: Debt Settlement
nodes.summary: The partial document describes liquidation options for delinquent loans and the procedures the Agency and Servicing Office follow when a borrower cannot fully repay the debt. It explains short sales and debt settlement, including when the borrower may be released from personal liability, what forms and supporting documents are required, and how proceeds and case information are transmitted and recorded. It also covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, borrower cases in bankruptcy, and foreclosure procedures, including both Agency foreclosure and foreclosure by another lien holder. Throughout, the main focus is on determining the option that yields the greatest net recovery for the Government while following approval and reporting requirements.

nodes.title: 2. Deed in Lieu of Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The document section outlines liquidation options for delinquent loans. It explains when the Agency may accept a deed in lieu of foreclosure, emphasizing that approval depends on whether it will produce a greater net recovery than foreclosure and that borrower debt may remain unless separate debt settlement procedures are completed. It also covers cases where a borrower is in bankruptcy, noting that any conveyance of title must be approved by a supervisor and the Office of General Counsel and generally accepted only if the Agency can receive clear title and substantially recover its debt. The section then discusses foreclosure procedures, including Agency-initiated foreclosure after loan acceleration and strategies when another lien holder starts foreclosure, such as joining the action, bidding at sale, settling for assignment of the note and mortgage, or redeeming the property if it is expected to improve net recovery.

nodes.title: 3. Borrowers in Bankruptcy
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines liquidation options for a loan, focusing on how the Agency handles property when a borrower defaults. It explains that a deed in lieu of foreclosure may be accepted after acceleration only if it results in a better net recovery than foreclosure, with supervisor approval, and that the borrower remains liable for any remaining debt unless debt settlement procedures are followed. It also covers cases where a borrower is in bankruptcy, stating that acceptance of property from the Bankruptcy Court requires approval from both a supervisor and the Office of General Counsel, and is generally allowed when the Agency can obtain clear title and substantially recover its debt. The document then discusses foreclosure procedures, including agency-initiated foreclosure after acceleration, and foreclosure by another lien holder, where the Servicing Office must choose the action that maximizes government recovery, such as joining the foreclosure, bidding at sale, or settling with the other lien holder. It also notes that if the Government has redemption rights and did not protect its interest, the Supervisor decides whether to redeem based on expected net recovery.

nodes.title: B. Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options for a delinquent loan, focusing on how the Agency may recover debt through different actions. It covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, where the borrower voluntarily conveys the property and the Agency accepts only if it will recover more than through foreclosure, with supervisor approval required. It also addresses borrowers in bankruptcy, noting that the Agency may accept property conveyed by a Bankruptcy Court if it can take title free of other liens and substantially recover the debt, with approval from a supervisor and the Office of General Counsel. The section then outlines foreclosure procedures, including Agency-initiated foreclosure after loan acceleration and the possibility of voluntary liquidation before the sale. Finally, it discusses foreclosure by another lien holder, emphasizing that the Servicing Office should choose the strategy that maximizes net recovery, such as joining the foreclosure, bidding at sale, settling with the other lien holder, or redeeming the property when redemption rights exist.

nodes.title: 1. Agency Foreclosure
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines liquidation options for an accelerated loan, focusing on alternatives to foreclosure and how the Agency should decide among them. It explains:

- **Deed in lieu of foreclosure:** A borrower may voluntarily transfer the property to the Agency after acceleration, but the Agency will accept only if it yields a greater net recovery than foreclosure, and the decision requires supervisory approval. The borrower remains liable for any remaining debt unless debt settlement procedures are followed.
- **Bankruptcy-related conveyance:** If a Bankruptcy Court offers the Agency title to a property, acceptance requires approval from both a supervisor and the Office of General Counsel. The Agency generally accepts if it can obtain title free of other liens and substantially recover the debt, and it may pay court-approved fees.
- **Agency foreclosure:** Once liquidation is deemed appropriate, the loan is accelerated, and the borrower may still voluntarily liquidate before the foreclosure sale.
- **Foreclosure by another lien holder:** If another creditor initiates foreclosure, the Servicing Office must choose the strategy that maximizes government recovery, such as joining the action, bidding at the sale, or settling for an assignment. If redemption rights exist and the Government did not protect its interest, the Supervisor decides whether to redeem based on estimated net recovery.

nodes.title: 2. Foreclosure by Another Lien Holder
nodes.summary: The document explains liquidation options available to the Agency for delinquent loans and how to evaluate them. It covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, conveyance from borrowers in bankruptcy, agency foreclosure, foreclosure by another lien holder, and release of a valueless lien. It also defines estimated and actual net recovery value, describes how market value is the basis for calculating net recovery value, and notes that environmental hazards and responsibilities must be considered when estimating property value.

nodes.title: C. Release of a Valueless Lien
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options in HB-2-3550, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released and how the Agency handles that process. It defines net recovery value as the expected amount recovered from liquidating a property after costs, distinguishes estimated from actual net recovery value, and explains that market value is the basis for the calculation. It also notes that market value may be estimated, appraised, or based on sale price depending on the stage of liquidation. In addition, the document highlights the need to consider environmental hazards and responsibilities when estimating value, and summarizes the net recovery formula as market value minus costs plus income.

nodes.title: 6.2 NET RECOVERY VALUE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options for Agency-held property, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released and how the Field Office and Servicing Office coordinate that action. It then defines net recovery value, distinguishing estimated from actual net recovery value, and explains that estimated net recovery value is based on market value adjusted for costs and income. The section also describes how market value is established using estimates, appraisals, or actual sale price, and notes that environmental hazards and responsibilities must be considered when estimating value.

nodes.title: A. Establishing Market Value
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options in HB-2-3550, focusing on when an Agency lien may be released if a net recovery valuation shows the lien has no value or negative recovery potential. It describes the procedures for notifying offices and handling taxes, insurance, and debt settlement after a lien release. It then defines net recovery value as the expected amount the Agency could recover from a property after liquidation costs, and distinguishes it from actual net recovery value. The section also explains how market value is the basis for net recovery calculations, noting it may come from an estimate, appraisal, or sale price depending on the stage of the process. Finally, it highlights the need to consider environmental hazards and responsibilities when estimating market value, and shows the basic net recovery formula: market value minus costs plus income.

nodes.title: B. Environmental Considerations
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers liquidation options in foreclosure cases, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released if net recovery value shows no expected recovery. It explains that this is usually handled at the Field Office level, which must notify the Servicing Office so taxes and insurance stop being paid and a debt settlement package can be sent to the borrower.

It then defines net recovery value as the amount the Agency expects to recover from a property after subtracting all liquidation, holding, and selling costs, and contrasts this with actual net recovery value obtained after sale. The document emphasizes that market value is the basis for the calculation and may come from an estimate, appraisal, or actual sale price depending on the stage of the process.

A significant portion addresses environmental considerations in determining market value and protecting the Agency from liability. It requires prior inspection for contamination risks such as hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, petroleum products, and underground storage tanks, using the Transaction Screen Questionnaire, with concerns referred to the State Environmental Coordinator. The document also notes that the Agency must consider obligations related to environmental resources it may need to protect after acquisition.

Finally, the excerpt includes a list of environmentally sensitive land uses and extraordinary circumstances, such as historic properties, threatened or endangered species, wetlands, floodplains, violations of environmental laws, potential uncontrolled releases of contaminants, and other protected natural resources like wilderness areas, water sources, coastal zones, and coral reefs.

nodes.title: Exhibit 6-1 Environmentally Sensitive Land Uses and Resources Extraordinary Circumstances
nodes.summary:
The partial document focuses on USDA Rural Development servicing guidance for determining net recovery value before foreclosure or property acquisition. It explains that the Agency should inspect property for hazardous substances, wastes, petroleum products, and underground storage tanks, using the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and consulting the State Environmental Coordinator if concerns arise. It also notes the need to consider environmental compliance costs and references related instructions.

A major section lists environmentally sensitive land uses and extraordinary circumstances that may affect Agency actions, including historic properties, threatened or endangered species, wetlands, floodplains, violations of environmental laws, hazardous releases, and impacts on special resources such as wilderness areas, water sources, coastal zones, and coral reefs. It also mentions controversy over environmental effects as a trigger for review.

The document then addresses special considerations for projects on Tribal land or dependent Indian communities, including coordination with tribes and procedures for Section 106 historic preservation review under RD Instruction 1970-H.

Finally, it begins a section on ordering appraisals, explaining when appraisals should be obtained, such as when a lien may be valueless or when a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure, so the Servicer can decide the best interest of the Government.

nodes.title: C. Ordering An Appraisal
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses procedures related to net recovery value and appraisal decisions in servicing cases. It notes that projects on Tribal land or in dependent Indian communities may require additional coordination with the Tribe, including Section 106 review under NHPA and consultation rules involving THPO or SHPO participation. The document then explains when to order an appraisal, including immediately when a lien may be valueless and when a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure, so the servicer can determine the Government’s best interest.

nodes.title: 1. Valueless Lien
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses net recovery value procedures in the context of servicing and foreclosure actions. It notes that projects on Tribal lands or in dependent Indian communities may require additional consultation with the Tribe and careful handling of human environment, cultural resources, and Section 106 review requirements, including the role of the Tribal Historic Preservation Officer or State Historic Preservation Officer. The document also explains when to order an appraisal: if the lien may be valueless, an appraisal should be obtained immediately to decide whether to release the lien, and if a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure after acceleration, an appraisal should be obtained right away to determine whether accepting the deed is in the Government’s interest.

nodes.title: 2. Deed in Lieu of Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to determine net recovery value in foreclosure and liquidation cases, with emphasis on special coordination requirements for properties on Tribal lands or dependent Indian communities under Section 106 review and related NHPA rules. It then outlines when an appraisal should be ordered: immediately for potentially valueless liens or deed-in-lieu offers, and shortly before foreclosure sale when the property is going to foreclosure. The document also defines how to estimate the holding period based on the expected disposition method and notes that marketing/disposition time should be included if the Agency acquires the property. Finally, it lists key deductions from market value, including prior and junior liens, and explains that title searches may be needed to identify lien positions and amounts.

nodes.title: 3. Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to estimate net recovery value in foreclosure and related disposition situations. It emphasizes that appraisals for foreclosure should be done shortly before sale because property values may change during a long foreclosure process. It also defines how to estimate the holding period based on the type of disposition, such as foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, release of lien, or debt settlement, and notes that marketing and disposition time should be included if the property is acquired. Finally, it describes deductions from market value that must be considered in liquidation, especially prior liens and junior liens, and states that the servicing office should perform a title search to identify lien positions and amounts.

nodes.title: D. Holding Period
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to determine net recovery value in foreclosure-related situations. It covers when an appraisal should be obtained before foreclosure, how to estimate the holding period based on the method of disposition, and what liquidation costs must be deducted from market value. It specifically mentions prior liens and junior liens, including the need for a title search to identify liens and their amounts.

nodes.title: E. Deductions from Market Value
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to estimate net recovery value for a property in different liquidation scenarios. It covers how the holding period is determined based on the method of disposition, including foreclosure, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, release of a valueless lien, and debt settlement after acceleration. It then lists deductions from market value that must be included in the calculation, such as prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative costs, and management costs. Finally, it identifies possible additions to market value, including appreciation during the holding period and limited income from leasing REO properties under certain circumstances.

nodes.title: F. Additions to Market Value
nodes.summary: The document explains how the Agency calculates net recovery value and basic security loss for a property, including deductions such as selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative costs, and management costs, as well as possible additions like appreciation and income during the holding period. It then defines basic security loss as the difference between the property’s market value and the Agency debt, including principal, subsidy subject to recapture, and recoverable costs, and notes how this information helps guide borrower workout decisions and portfolio analysis. The final section describes the deed in lieu of foreclosure process, stating when it may be accepted, how it is processed, what documents are required from the borrower, and how liens, taxes, and assessments are handled before title conveyance.

nodes.title: 6.3 BASIC SECURITY LOSS
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers two main topics in loan servicing and liquidation. First, it explains the concept of “basic security loss,” which is calculated as the difference between the property’s market value and the outstanding Agency debt, including principal, subsidy subject to recapture, and recoverable costs. It notes that this measure helps the Servicer decide how to work with the borrower and can also support future portfolio analysis and loan origination decisions.

Second, it describes the “deed in lieu of foreclosure” process, where a borrower conveys the property title to the Agency as a method of liquidation. The document states that this option is considered only after acceleration of the account and only if it serves the Government’s best interest based on estimated net recovery value. It outlines the approval process, required documents, and the need to address liens and taxes before acceptance, while allowing the Agency to settle these obligations if doing so is advantageous.

nodes.title: 6.4 DEED IN LIEU OF FORECLOSURE
nodes.summary: The document explains how the Agency calculates and uses “basic security loss,” defines deed in lieu of foreclosure and the conditions under which it may be accepted, and outlines borrower obligations and consequences in such cases. It also begins the foreclosure section, noting that state laws and OGC guidance affect the process, and introduces the decision to accelerate an account, including situations where OGC concurrence is required.

nodes.title: 6.5 FORECLOSURE
nodes.summary:
This excerpt covers USDA/Agency loan servicing procedures for deed in lieu of foreclosure and foreclosure actions. It explains that a deed in lieu does not automatically cancel the borrower’s remaining debt, that the account is credited only with the actual net recovery value, and that any unpaid balance must be handled through debt settlement procedures. It also lists borrower advisories, including that foreclosure continues during review, conveyance costs are added to the debt, liability may remain after the deed transfer, and title insurance or a final title opinion is required.

The foreclosure section discusses how state law affects acceleration and foreclosure procedures and emphasizes consulting OGC for proper compliance. It outlines the Servicing Office’s responsibility to decide whether to accelerate the account and begin foreclosure, noting that many factors are case-specific. It also identifies situations requiring OGC concurrence before foreclosure, such as nonmonetary defaults and certain military-service-related cases under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act.

nodes.title: A. Making the Acceleration Decision
nodes.summary:
This section explains how a deed in lieu of foreclosure works and how it affects a borrower’s debt, including that it does not automatically release the borrower from remaining liability and any unpaid balance must be handled through debt settlement procedures. It also lists borrower advisements such as foreclosure continuing during consideration of the offer, conveyance costs being added to the debt, and the need for title insurance or a title opinion.

The section then begins the foreclosure process, noting that state laws affect acceleration and foreclosure procedures and that OGC should be consulted. It describes the servicing office’s role in deciding whether to accelerate a loan and start foreclosure, and introduces situations where OGC concurrence is required before foreclosure, including nonmonetary defaults and cases involving military service protections under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act.

nodes.title: 1. OGC Concurrence
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses USDA servicing procedures for dealing with loan default through deed in lieu of foreclosure and foreclosure. It explains that a deed in lieu of foreclosure does not automatically cancel the borrower’s remaining debt, how the borrower’s account is credited, and that any unpaid balance must be settled through debt settlement procedures. It also lists key borrower reminders, such as foreclosure continuing during review, conveyance costs being added to the debt, and the need for title insurance or a final title opinion.

The document then outlines the foreclosure process, emphasizing that state law affects acceleration and foreclosure procedures and that OGC consultation may be required. It covers when the Servicing Office should decide to accelerate a loan, including special cases requiring OGC concurrence, such as nonmonetary default, military service issues, and situations involving other USDA liens. It also addresses tribal land considerations and the role of other lien holders.

Finally, it describes the acceleration notice process, including what the notice must include, the borrower’s rights to discuss the decision or request an administrative appeal, the 30-day response period, and the required method of delivery to borrowers and cosigners.

nodes.title: 2. Tribal Land
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under USDA Rural Development loan servicing, focusing on when liquidation by foreclosure is appropriate and how it is carried out. It explains special situations involving other USDA loans, tribal land, and other lien holders, including how foreclosure may be affected or coordinated with those parties. The document then describes the acceleration process, starting with the acceleration notice that demands full repayment and informs the borrower of the reasons, amount due, payment options, informal discussion rights, and appeal procedures. It also specifies that the borrower and cosigners must receive the notice by regular and certified mail, with an additional copy sent to the property address if different.

nodes.title: 3. Role of Other Lien Holders
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses foreclosure procedures under USDA lending rules, focusing on special conditions and the acceleration process. It explains how foreclosure may be affected when the property also secures another USDA agency loan, when the property is on tribal allotted or trust land, and when other lien holders are involved. It also describes the acceleration notice: when it is used, what it must include, who must receive it, and the borrower’s 30-day period to pay in full or request a hearing.

nodes.title: B. Acceleration
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses foreclosure procedures under USDA servicing rules, focusing on when foreclosure may proceed and how it is handled. It covers special situations involving tribal land, where liquidation can be delayed until an offer to transfer the account to an eligible tribal member, the tribe, or the Indian Housing Authority is made. It also explains the role of other lien holders, including when they may be invited to join a foreclosure action and when assignment of promissory notes and security instruments is allowed. The document then describes the acceleration process, beginning with an acceleration notice that demands full payment and informs the borrower of the reason, amount due, payment methods, appeal rights, and the chance for an informal discussion. It also specifies that the notice must be sent to borrowers and cosigners by regular and certified mail, and to the property address if different.

nodes.title: 1. The Acceleration Notice
nodes.summary: The partial document describes USDA foreclosure and liquidation procedures under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 6.5. It explains how foreclosure can be affected by other USDA program loans, tribal land requirements, and the role of other lien holders. It then outlines the acceleration process, including the acceleration notice contents, delivery requirements, borrower appeal rights, and the 30-day response period. The text also covers how payment subsidies are treated after acceleration, restrictions on special servicing, assignment of security instruments, and conditions under which a borrower may cure the default and reinstate the loan, including possible waivers of the two-year limitation based on repayment ability and agency interest.

nodes.title: 2. Treatment of Payment Subsidy
nodes.summary:
This excerpt from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 6.5 on Foreclosure, explains how subsidized loans and servicing actions are handled after a loan is accelerated, and under what circumstances a borrower may still cure the default. It covers:  

- **Treatment of payment subsidy:** the subsidy agreement is not canceled by acceleration, but it will not be renewed after expiration unless the loan is reinstated.  
- **Special servicing:** once the account is accelerated, the borrower is no longer eligible for special servicing options described earlier in the handbook.  
- **Assignment of security instruments:** notes and security instruments may be assigned after acceleration if appeals are exhausted, foreclosure has been accepted by OGC, and the Agency is receiving at least net recovery value.  
- **Offers to pay / cure the default:** Field Offices must refer cure offers to the Servicing Office, and borrowers may cure and keep the loan only in certain cases, such as when State law or a NAD decision requires reinstatement, or when they have not cured the account within the past two years.  
- **Waiver of the two-year limit:** the Deputy Administrator may waive the two-year restriction if it benefits the government, and such requests require financial documentation showing repayment ability and agreement to establish and fund repayment terms.

nodes.title: 3. Special Servicing
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 6.5, focusing on how various servicing actions are handled after a loan is accelerated. It explains that payment subsidy agreements are not canceled by acceleration but will not be renewed unless the account is reinstated. It states that borrowers lose eligibility for special servicing actions once the account is accelerated. It also describes when promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure, including the need for exhausted appeals, OGC acceptance, and the Agency receiving at least the net recovery value. Finally, it outlines how offers to cure default are referred to the Servicing Office, the limited conditions under which borrowers may reinstate the loan, and the authority to waive the two-year limitation if doing so is in the government’s best interest, subject to financial verification and repayment ability requirements.

nodes.title: 4. Assignment of Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The excerpt from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 6.5 on Foreclosure explains several rules that apply after a borrower’s loan has been accelerated. It covers how payment subsidies are handled, noting that subsidy agreements are not canceled immediately but will not be renewed unless the account is reinstated. It states that once accelerated, the borrower is no longer eligible for special servicing options. It also describes when promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure processing. Finally, it outlines how offers to cure a default are treated, including the conditions under which a borrower may reinstate the loan, the two-year limit on curing defaults to avoid foreclosure, and the authority to waive that limit if it serves the government’s best interest.

nodes.title: 5. Offers to Pay
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key procedures related to foreclosure after a loan has been accelerated. It covers how payment subsidies are handled, the borrower’s loss of eligibility for special servicing actions, and when promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure. It also describes how offers to cure a default are processed, including conditions under which a borrower may reinstate the loan, possible waivers of the two-year limit, required financial documentation, escrow requirements, and how partial cure offers are handled. In addition, it outlines borrower remedies for challenging acceleration decisions, such as informal review, mediation, or appeal to the NAD. Finally, it discusses when foreclosure legal proceedings should begin and the rules governing the pursuit of deficiency judgments, including situations where such judgments will not be sought.

nodes.title: C. Review of the Acceleration Decision
nodes.summary: This section covers foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, including how borrowers may cure a default by making an offer and completing required financial documentation, the role of escrow and reamortization if the delinquency is not fully paid, and how borrower payments are transmitted. It also explains borrower rights to review or appeal an acceleration decision through informal review, mediation, dispute resolution, or appeal to the NAD. Finally, it describes when legal foreclosure proceedings begin, the need to consult legal counsel due to state law differences, and the limited circumstances under which the Agency will seek a deficiency judgment.

nodes.title: D. Initiating Legal Proceedings
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the foreclosure process under HB-2-3550, including how borrowers may cure a default before foreclosure, the requirements for accepting partial cure offers, and how funds must be submitted. It also covers borrower options for review or appeal of an acceleration decision, the timing and initiation of legal proceedings, and when deficiency judgments may or may not be pursued. The section then describes the foreclosure sale process, how the Agency determines its bid amount based on gross investment or net recovery value, and what happens if the Agency wins the sale or if sale proceeds do not fully cover the debt.

nodes.title: E. The Foreclosure Sale
nodes.summary: The excerpt focuses on the foreclosure process in the USDA RHS servicing handbook. It explains that while legal foreclosure proceedings are pending, the servicer should encourage the borrower to voluntarily liquidate the property and may consider debt settlement options. Once legal proceedings are complete, the property is sold at a foreclosure sale. The document then outlines how the Agency determines its bid amount: the bid is the lesser of the Agency’s gross investment or the estimated net recovery value of the security property. Gross investment includes the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received. It also notes that bidding procedures may vary locally, that a local presence may be required, and that if the Agency is the highest bidder, the property becomes REO and is managed by the Field Office. If the sale does not cover the full debt, the Servicing Office follows debt settlement procedures. The note at the end mentions RHS requirements to lease or sell inventory properties to public agencies and nonprofits for transitional and turnkey housing purposes.

nodes.title: 1. Establishing a Bid Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the foreclosure process after legal proceedings conclude, focusing on the foreclosure sale. It states that the Servicer should encourage voluntary liquidation during the legal process and may use debt settlement options. It then describes how the Agency determines its bid at the foreclosure sale: the bid is the lesser of the Agency’s gross investment or the estimated net recovery value of the security property. Gross investment includes the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received. The document also notes that bidding procedures vary by locality and may require a local presence. If the Agency wins the sale, the property becomes REO property to be managed and disposed of by the Field Office. If the sale proceeds do not cover the borrower’s debt, the Servicing Office follows debt settlement procedures. Finally, it mentions that RHS must lease or sell certain inventory properties to public agencies and nonprofits for transitional and turnkey housing purposes.

nodes.title: 2. Bidding at the Foreclosure Sale
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the foreclosure process and how the Agency determines its bid at a foreclosure sale. It states that while legal proceedings are pending, the servicer should encourage the borrower to liquidate voluntarily and may explore debt settlement options. Once foreclosure proceedings conclude, the property is sold, and Field Offices must calculate the Agency’s bid, which is based on the lesser of the Agency’s gross investment or the property’s estimated net recovery value. It defines gross investment as the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received, with differences noted between judicial and nonjudicial foreclosure states. The document also describes what happens if the Agency is the highest bidder, including conversion of the property to REO and subsequent management by the Field Office, and notes that if sale proceeds do not cover the debt, debt settlement procedures are used. 

The excerpt also includes an attachment containing a Net Recovery Value Worksheet. This worksheet outlines the information needed to calculate net recovery value, such as case details, proposed liquidation option, calculation date, and estimated holding period. It then lists the calculation steps: starting with market value, subtracting various deductions like liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative and management costs, and then adding any appreciation or income during the holding period to determine net recovery value.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 6-A NET RECOVERY VALUE WORKSHEET
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a “Net Recovery Value Worksheet” used to calculate the expected net recovery from a proposed liquidation option in a lending or foreclosure-related case. It includes sections for basic case information such as case number, borrower name/ID, proposed liquidation option, calculation date, and estimated holding period.

The main body of the worksheet focuses on computing net recovery value by starting with the market value from a current appraisal and then subtracting various deductions, including prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation during the holding period, administrative costs, and management costs. It also allows for additions to value, such as appreciation and income during the holding period.

The worksheet concludes with a formula for determining the final net recovery value and provides instructions for calculating the holding period based on the type of liquidation action, such as lien release, refinancing, sale, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, or debt settlement.

nodes.title: I. BACKGROUND
nodes.summary: The document is a Net Recovery Value Worksheet used to estimate the financial recovery from a proposed liquidation option. It collects basic case information such as case number, borrower ID, liquidation option, calculation date, and estimated holding period. The main calculation section determines net recovery value by starting with the current market value, subtracting agency-related deductions like prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative costs, and management costs, then adding any expected appreciation and income during the holding period. The worksheet also includes instructions for determining the holding period for different situations such as lien release, refinancing, sales, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, and debt settlement offers.

nodes.title: II. CALCULATION OF NET RECOVERY VALUE
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main areas of USDA Rural Development single-family housing servicing:

1. **Net Recovery Value Worksheet**
   - A form used to estimate the agency’s expected net recovery from a liquidation option.
   - It records basic case information, market value, deductions such as liens, selling expenses, holding/administrative/management costs, and possible additions like appreciation or income during holding.
   - The worksheet calculates the final **net recovery value** and explains how to determine the holding period and what costs belong in each category.

2. **Chapter 7: Special Collections**
   - Explains how the agency handles debts and collection actions beyond normal servicing.
   - Covers three major topics:
     - **Unauthorized assistance**: identifying, investigating, notifying borrowers, calculating amounts owed, and servicing consequences when assistance was received due to false or inaccurate information.
     - **Offsets**: collecting debts through administrative offsets, salary offsets, and IRS tax refund offsets, including eligibility, notification, borrower rights, appeals, and refund rules.
     - **Debt settlement**: options for resolving uncollectible debts, including compromise, adjustment, charge-off, cancellation, and Treasury debt recall.
   - The section also describes how the agency evaluates a debtor’s financial condition and uses Form RD 3550-20 and related forms to process settlements and account changes.

nodes.title: Paragraph 7.16 Negotiating A Settlement
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers procedures for negotiating a settlement on a debt, including when a debtor cannot pay and has no reasonable future prospects. It emphasizes that settlements must be handled consistently and may be arranged through a legally authorized representative if the debtor cannot act.

A major focus is assessing the debtor’s financial condition before settlement negotiations. The Servicing Office must evaluate the finances of the debtor and any cosigners, considering factors such as prior liens, current security value, equity in other assets, expected income over the next five years, possible inheritances, and other debts. The document also references Form RD 3550-20 as a tool for collecting household expense and income information, and describes the process for requesting additional financial verification within 30 days, such as proof of income.

nodes.title: 7.16 NEGOTIATING A SETTLEMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and process for negotiating a debt settlement under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 7.16. It states that settlements must be handled consistently to avoid unequal treatment and may be offered by a legally authorized party if the debtor cannot act. The section focuses on assessing the debtor’s financial condition before any settlement is negotiated, considering only the debtor, note signatories, and cosigners except in community property states. It lists key factors to evaluate, including prior liens, current security value, equity in other assets, present and future income, and other debts. The document also describes using Form RD 3550-20 to gather financial information, with the form completed by the debtor, the Servicing Office, or both, and says the debtor should be asked to provide additional verification within 30 days, such as proof of income.

nodes.title: A. Assessing the Debtor’s Financial Condition
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for negotiating a debt settlement under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 7.16. It emphasizes that settlements must be handled consistently to prevent unequal treatment and may be negotiated by an authorized representative if the debtor cannot act. The document focuses on how the Servicing Office must assess the debtor’s financial condition before making a settlement offer, including reviewing liens, security value, assets, income, other debts, and using Form RD 3550-20 to calculate available income and gather supporting financial documentation. It also outlines verification steps such as income proof, expense records, tax returns, bank statements, and other records, with costs charged to the debtor if additional information is needed. Special situations are covered for joint debtors, deceased debtors, and debtors who have disappeared, including requirements for documentation, estate considerations, and reasonable efforts to locate missing debtors before cancellation or charge-off.

nodes.title: B. Special Case Debtors
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses procedures for negotiating a settlement of debt, including what financial and property information should be verified about debtors and how verification costs are handled. It explains that credit reports are used only when there is concern that financial information may be incomplete or inaccurate. The text also covers how to handle settlements involving joint debtors, requiring documentation when some parties cannot be included, such as when they are deceased, bankrupt, or cannot be located. In addition, it outlines special rules for deceased debtors, allowing executors or administrators to settle claims based on estate value after higher-priority expenses, and for debtors who have disappeared, requiring reasonable, well-documented efforts to locate them before debt cancellation or charge-off.

nodes.title: 1. Deceased Debtors
nodes.summary: The passage explains how the Servicer should gather financial and asset information when negotiating a settlement, including using medical expense verification, tax returns, bank statements, county records, and wage data, and when credit reports may be obtained. It also states that any costs for obtaining this information are charged to the debtor’s account. The document then covers special-case debtors, especially joint debtors, deceased debtors, and debtors who have disappeared. For joint debtors, the Servicer must document why other parties are not included if only some can settle. For deceased debtors, settlements may be made by an executor or administrator and must consider estate value and priority claims. For missing debtors, the Servicer must make and document reasonable efforts to locate them before charging off or cancelling the debt.

nodes.title: 2. Debtors Who Have Disappeared
nodes.summary: The partial document describes USDA loan debt settlement procedures, focusing on how to verify a debtor’s financial information and the special rules for negotiating settlements. It lists types of documentation that may be used, such as medical expenses, tax returns, bank statements, county records, and wage information, and explains that verification costs may be charged to the debtor. It also covers situations involving joint debtors, deceased debtors, and debtors who have disappeared, including requirements to document efforts to locate missing debtors. In addition, it states that settlements should generally not be made while other USDA loans on the same property remain active unless it benefits the government. Finally, it outlines steps for processing approved settlements, including executing forms, notifying the debtor, and handling escrow, unapplied funds, and offset cancellation.

nodes.title: 3. Settlement Where Debt or Owes More Than One Type of Agency Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA loan debt settlement procedures. It explains that a property loan should generally not be settled while other USDA Agency loans secured by the same property remain active unless doing so benefits the government. It then outlines the processing of debt settlements, including how escrow and unapplied funds are handled—remaining funds are applied to the debt when settlement is completed. Finally, it lists the required actions after a settlement is approved: executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing of the approved terms, informing the debtor if a debt has been canceled without a request, and canceling any offset actions in cases of compromise, adjustment, or cancellation.

nodes.title: 7.17 PROCESSING DEBT SETTLEMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules and procedures for negotiating and processing debt settlements under USDA servicing guidelines. It explains that a property loan generally should not be settled if other USDA agency loans on the same security property are still active unless doing so benefits the government.
