It then outlines how debt settlements are handled after approval, including applying escrow and unapplied funds to the debt, executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing of the approved settlement and its terms, informing the debtor when a debt is canceled without their request, and cancelling any offset actions with paying agencies in cases of compromise, adjustment, or cancellation.

nodes.title: A. Escrow and Unapplied Funds [7 CFR 3550.252(h)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules and procedures for negotiating and processing USDA debt settlements. It states that a loan should generally not be settled if other USDA agency loans tied to the same property are still active unless doing so benefits the government. It then outlines how settlement processing works, including continuing escrow disbursements until settlement is complete, applying any remaining escrow or unapplied funds to the debt, and the required actions after a settlement is approved. These actions include executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing with the settlement terms, informing the debtor of any cancellation if applicable, and canceling any offset with paying agencies in cases of compromise, adjustment, or cancellation.

nodes.title: B. Approved Settlements
nodes.summary: This section explains how USDA Rural Development handles debt settlement, including when a loan on a property should not be settled if other agency loans remain active unless it benefits the government. It then outlines the processing steps for settlements: escrow and unapplied funds must be applied to the debt, approved settlements require execution of Form RD 3550-20 and written notice to the debtor, and rejected settlements must be documented with reasons, sent to the debtor with appeal rights, and filed appropriately. It also covers required reporting of settled debts to the IRS using Form 1099-C and notification to credit agencies. Finally, it describes how promissory notes and security instruments are handled after settlement, including generating satisfaction or release letters and whether the note is returned to the debtor or retained in the case file depending on the type of settlement.

nodes.title: D. Notify the IRS and Credit Agencies [7 CFR 3550.252(e)]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for processing debt settlements under USDA servicing rules. nodes.title: 7.18 DISPOSITION OF PROMISSORY NOTES AND RELEASE OF SECURITY INSTRUMENTS
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers procedures for handling debt settlements in USDA/RHS servicing, focusing on two main areas:

1. **Rejected settlement offers**
   - The Servicing Office must document the reasons for rejecting a debtor’s settlement offer on Form RD 3550-20.
   - The debtor must be notified in writing that the offer was rejected and informed of appeal rights.
   - The original form goes to the Field Office for the case file, and a copy is attached to the rejection letter.

2. **Reporting and post-settlement disposition**
   - Settled debts must be reported to the IRS using Form 1099-C, which covers debts cancelled, compromised, or adjusted.
   - Credit reporting agencies must also be notified.
   - Once reported to the IRS, no further collection efforts should be made.

3. **Disposition of promissory notes and release of security instruments**
   - When a debt is satisfied, the Field Office must prepare the proper satisfaction and/or release letter.
   - If the debtor requested the settlement, the note is marked as satisfied and returned to the debtor with the satisfaction/release letter and mortgage.
   - If the Agency cancels the debt without a debtor request, the note is not returned and the satisfaction letter is kept in the case file.

nodes.title: (b) Custodial property

nodes.title: (c) REO property
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains how RHS classifies and sells REO properties after taking title to a security property. It distinguishes between program property and nonprogram (NP) property based on whether the property can qualify, or be reasonably repaired to qualify, for section 502 financing. It also requires RHS to disclose when a property is not decent, safe, and sanitary or does not meet energy conservation standards, and to include deed restrictions preventing residential use until those issues are corrected; known lead-based paint hazards must also be disclosed. For REO property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land, the sale is limited to members of the tribe with jurisdiction, the tribe itself, or an Indian housing authority serving the tribe. Finally, the excerpt describes reservation rules for program REO properties, giving priority periods for eligible direct or guaranteed housing loan applicants and qualifying nonprofit or public bodies, while delaying consideration of offers during the initial 3-business-day holding period and allowing nonqualified buyers’ offers to be treated as received after the reservation period ends.

nodes.title: (1) Classification
nodes.summary:
This part of the document explains how RHS handles REO (real estate owned) property after taking title to a security property. It covers:

- **Property classification** as either program property or nonprogram (NP) property, based on whether it can qualify for section 502 financing or be reasonably repaired to do so.
- **Disclosure requirements** when an REO property is not decent, safe, sanitary, or energy compliant, including notices about defects, restrictions on residential use until corrected, and disclosure of known lead-based paint hazards.
- **Special rules for property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land**, limiting sale or disposition to tribal members, the tribe, or an Indian housing authority serving the tribe.
- **Reservation periods for program REO properties**, giving priority to eligible single-family housing loan applicants and nonprofit/public bodies providing transitional housing during the first 60 days after sale notice and 30 days after certain changes.
- **Offer handling rules**, including treatment of offers from ineligible buyers during reservation periods and a 3-business-day holding period before offers are considered.

nodes.title: (2) Disclosing decent, safe, and sanitary defects
nodes.summary: This section explains how RHS classifies and sells REO property after taking title to a security property. It defines “program” versus “nonprogram” property based on whether the property can qualify, or be reasonably repaired to qualify, for section 502 financing. It also requires RHS to disclose if a property is not decent, safe, and sanitary, if it fails energy conservation standards, and to notify buyers of known lead-based paint hazards. For property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land, disposition is restricted to eligible tribal members, the tribe, or an Indian housing authority serving the tribe. The section also sets reservation rules for program REO properties, giving priority periods for eligible single-family loan applicants and certain nonprofit or public bodies, while allowing other buyers to submit offers that are treated as received after the reservation period. It further states that no offer is considered until three business days after the property is first offered for sale.

nodes.title: (3) Property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land
nodes.summary: This section explains how RHS classifies and disposes of REO properties after taking title. It distinguishes between program properties and nonprogram properties based on whether the property can qualify for section 502 financing, and notes that properties improved beyond “modest” standards are nonprogram. It also requires disclosure of any defects affecting decent, safe, and sanitary conditions or energy efficiency, adds deed restrictions until those issues are corrected, and requires notice of known lead-based paint hazards. For properties on Indian tribal allotted or trust land, sale is restricted to eligible tribal members, the tribe, or an Indian housing authority. The section further describes reservation rules for program REO properties, including an initial period during which they are reserved for eligible housing loan applicants and certain nonprofit or public entities, how offers from ineligible buyers are treated, and the 3-business-day holding period before offers are considered.

nodes.title: (4) Reservation of program REO properties
nodes.summary: The partial document describes rules for REO property under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains how RHS classifies REO property as either program or nonprogram based on whether it can qualify for section 502 financing, and how properties that are not decent, safe, and sanitary must be disclosed with restrictions on residential use until corrected. It also addresses disposal of REO property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land, limiting sales to eligible tribal members, the tribe, or an Indian housing authority. Finally, it outlines the reservation period for program REO properties, including priority for eligible borrowers and certain nonprofit or public entities, how offers from ineligible buyers are treated during the reservation period, and the 3-business-day holding period before offers are considered.

nodes.title: (i) Program REO properties are reserved for eligible direct or guaranteed single family housing loans under this part or part 1980, subpart D of this title and nonprofit organizations or public bodies providing transitional housing during the first 60 days after the date of the first notice of sale, and during the first 30 days following any reduction in price or any other change in credit terms or other sale terms. After the expiration of a reservation period, program REO properties can be bought by any buyer.
nodes.summary: This section explains how RHS classifies and disposes of Real Estate Owned (REO) property. It defines the difference between program and nonprogram (NP) property based on whether the property can qualify for section 502 financing or be reasonably repaired to qualify. It also requires disclosure of any defects affecting decent, safe, and sanitary condition or energy conservation standards, includes restrictions and notices related to lead-based paint hazards, and sets special rules for property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land. Finally, it describes reservation rules for program REO properties, including who may buy them during the initial reservation periods, how offers from non-qualifying buyers are handled, and the 3-business-day holding period before offers are considered.

nodes.title: (ii) An offer on a program REO property from a buyer who does not qualify for a direct or guaranteed single family housing loan may be submitted during a reservation period, but is considered to have been received on the day after the reservation period ends.
nodes.summary: This section explains how RHS classifies and sells REO properties. It defines when a property is considered program or nonprogram based on its eligibility for section 502 financing and whether it can be reasonably repaired to qualify. It also requires disclosure of any decent, safe, and sanitary deficiencies, energy conservation issues, and known lead-based paint hazards, with restrictions on residential use until the property is brought into compliance. The section further limits disposal of REO property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land to eligible tribal members, the tribe, or an Indian housing authority. Finally, it describes reservation periods for program REO properties, giving priority to eligible single-family housing applicants and nonprofit or public bodies providing transitional housing, and sets rules for when offers are considered and evaluated.

nodes.title: (iii) No offer is considered until 3 business days after the date the property is offered for sale. An offer received during the 3-day holding period is not considered until the 4th day, and is evaluated with any other offers actually received on the 4th day.
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for Rural Housing Service (RHS) REO property sales and disposal. It covers how REO properties are classified as program or nonprogram based on eligibility for section 502 financing, and how defects, energy standards, and lead-based paint hazards must be disclosed. It also states special disposal rules for property on Indian tribal allotted or trust land. The document describes reservation periods for program REO properties, giving priority to eligible direct or guaranteed loan applicants and certain nonprofit or public bodies, along with timing rules for when offers are considered. It outlines how offers are prioritized when received on the same day, including order of preference and selection by lot for equal offers. Finally, it explains that RHS may sell REO properties by sealed bid or auction when appropriate, and introduces special-purpose sales such as conversion to multiple family housing.

nodes.title: (5) Priority of offers received the same day
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for selecting offers on RHS real estate-owned (REO) properties, especially when multiple offers arrive on the same day, and describes special sale procedures. It prioritizes offers in this order: eligible single-family housing applicants seeking program financing, nonprofits or public bodies proposing special-purpose uses such as transitional housing, cash offers, and nonprogram credit offers; ties among equal-priority offers are decided by lot. It also notes that REO properties are not held off the market during financing appeal requests. The document then covers sale by sealed bid or auction, allowing RHS to use these methods when beneficial to the government, including rules for bid deposits, negotiation if bids are too low or absent, and in the special purposes section, it mentions that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multifamily housing.

nodes.title: (i) Offers received on the same business day are selected in the following order:
nodes.summary: The document section outlines the rules for handling and prioritizing offers on REO properties under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains the order of priority for same-day offers, giving first priority to eligible single-family housing loan applicants using program terms, then nonprofits or public bodies seeking transitional or special-use housing, followed by cash offers and then NP credit offers. It also states that equal-priority offers are chosen by lot and that properties are not held off the market while financing appeals are pending. The section further describes how RHS may sell REO properties through sealed bid or public auction when advantageous, including public solicitation, bid deposit retention if a winning bidder fails to close, and the possibility of negotiated sale if bids are too low or absent. Finally, it begins a section on special purposes, noting that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multifamily housing.

nodes.title: (A) Offers from eligible direct or guaranteed single family housing loan applicants, with a request for credit on program terms. All offers are evaluated as if they were submitted at the listed price, regardless of the offering price.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes rules for selling USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS) real estate owned (REO) properties. It explains how offers received on the same business day are prioritized: first eligible program applicants, then nonprofits or public bodies for special-use conversions, then cash offers, and then non-program credit offers, with ties broken by lottery. It also states that REO properties are not held off the market while financing appeals are pending. In addition, it covers sale by sealed bid or public auction, including when RHS may use those methods, how bids are solicited, what happens if a bidder fails to settle, and when RHS may negotiate a sale if bids are too low or absent. Finally, it begins a section on special purposes, noting that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multifamily housing.

nodes.title: (B) Offers from nonprofits or public bodies for conversion to use as transitional housing or for other special purposes as specified in paragraph (d)(4) of this section.
nodes.summary: This section of 7 CFR Part 3550 explains how RHS handles the sale of real estate owned (REO) properties when multiple offers are received, including the priority order for same-day offers, tie-breaking by lottery, and the rule that properties are not held off the market during financing appeals. It also covers the use of sealed bids or public auctions when in the government’s best interest, including public notice requirements, bid deposit retention if a bidder fails to close, and negotiation options if bids are too low or no acceptable bids are received. nodes.title: (C) Cash offers, from highest to lowest.

It also states that REO properties are not withheld from the market during appeals of financing rejections. In addition, the document describes when RHS may use sealed bids or public auctions, how unsuccessful bidders may lose deposits, and when RHS may negotiate a sale if bids are too low or none are acceptable. Finally, it begins a section on special purposes, noting that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multifamily housing.

nodes.title: (D) NP credit offers, from highest to lowest.
nodes.summary: The document excerpt describes rules for selling Real Estate Owned (REO) properties under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains how offers received on the same business day are prioritized, with order given to eligible program applicants, nonprofits or public bodies for special uses, then cash offers, and finally NP credit offers. If offers have equal priority, selection is by lot. It also states that REO properties are not held off the market during appeals of financing rejections. The excerpt further covers RHS authority to sell properties by sealed bid or public auction when beneficial, including public notice requirements, handling of unsuccessful bidders, and the ability to negotiate privately if bids are too low or absent. Finally, it introduces special-purpose sales, including conversion of REO property to multiple family housing.

nodes.title: (ii) Acceptable offers of equal priority received on the same business day are selected by lot.
nodes.summary: The document describes USDA RHS rules for disposing of REO properties, focusing on how offers are prioritized when received on the same day and how sales may be conducted by sealed bid or auction. It explains the order of preference among eligible program applicants, nonprofits/public bodies, cash offers, and NP credit offers, and notes that equal-priority offers are chosen by lot. nodes.title: (iii) REO properties are not held off the market pending the outcome of an appeal of RHS rejection of a request for financing.
nodes.summary: The passage describes rules for prioritizing offers on RHS real estate-owned (REO) properties when multiple offers are received on the same business day. It sets an order of selection: eligible program applicants requesting credit on program terms first, then nonprofits or public bodies seeking conversion to transitional or special-purpose housing, followed by cash offers from highest to lowest, and then NP credit offers from highest to lowest. If offers have equal priority, they are chosen by lot. It also states that REO properties are not held off the market while an appeal of a financing rejection is pending. The section then explains that RHS may sell REO property by sealed bid or public auction if that serves the government’s interest, may keep bid deposits if a winning bidder fails to close, and may negotiate a sale without further public notice if bids are too low or none are acceptable. Finally, it begins a new subsection on special purposes, noting that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multiple family housing.

nodes.title: (1) REO property may be purchased for conversion to multiple family housing.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for the sale of Rural Housing Service (RHS) real estate owned (REO) properties. It explains how offers received on the same day are prioritized, with direct or guaranteed housing loan applicants given first consideration, followed by nonprofit/public body offers for special uses, then cash offers, and finally non-program credit offers. Finally, it begins a section on special purposes, stating that REO property may be purchased for conversion to multifamily housing.

nodes.title: (2) When a nonprofit organization or public body notifies RHS in writing of its intent to buy an REO property to provide transitional housing for the homeless, RHS may withdraw the property from the market for up to 30 days to give the entity an opportunity to execute a purchase contract. The listed price may be discounted for offers on a nonprogram REO property at any time, and on a program REO property after the 60-day reservation period. No down payment is required, and the loan term will be for a maximum of 30 years. Until RHS executes a sales agreement, an offer from a program-eligible applicant will receive priority, regardless of a nonprofit’s interest in purchasing the REO property for use as transitional housing.
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for handling real estate owned (REO) properties and debt settlement. It explains special sale or lease options for nonprofit organizations and public bodies to use REO or NP properties for transitional housing for the homeless, including temporary withdrawal from the market, discounted pricing, no down payment, up to 30-year loan terms, and leasing at $1 per year with RHS making initial repairs. It also notes special sale provisions for affordable housing. The second major topic is debt settlement policy, outlining when RHS may pursue collection actions for amounts due, seek judgments, refuse to settle one loan while others remain active on the same property, collect from cosigners or estates, and report settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies.

nodes.title: (3) NP properties may be leased to a nonprofit organization or public body to provide transitional housing for the homeless at an annual cost of one dollar. When an REO property is to be leased as transitional housing, RHS will make repairs needed to put the property in decent, safe, and sanitary condition. The lessee is responsible for all future repairs and maintenance.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules on selling or leasing real estate owned (REO) properties for special housing purposes and on collecting debts owed to RHS. It explains that nonprofit organizations or public bodies can notify RHS of interest in buying REO property for transitional housing for the homeless, with possible temporary withdrawal from the market, pricing discounts under certain conditions, no down payment, and loan terms up to 30 years. It also states that REO properties may be leased for transitional housing at a nominal annual cost, with RHS handling initial repairs and the lessee responsible for future maintenance. Another provision allows REO property to be sold under special terms to nonprofit or public bodies to provide affordable housing for very low- and low-income families. The document then outlines debt settlement policies, including when RHS may pursue debts, seek judgments, handle multiple loans, collect from cosigners or estates, and report settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies.

nodes.title: (4) REO property may be sold under special provisions to nonprofit organizations or public bodies for the purpose of providing affordable housing to very low- and low-income families.
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for handling real estate owned (REO) properties and debt collection. It explains special provisions allowing nonprofits or public bodies to buy or lease REO properties for transitional housing for the homeless, including possible withdrawal from the market, discounted pricing, no down payment, and lease terms with RHS handling initial repairs. It also notes that REO property may be sold to nonprofit or public bodies to provide affordable housing for very low- and low-income families. The second major topic is debt settlement policy, stating when RHS may pursue collection of amounts owed, seek judgments, avoid settling one loan while others on the same property remain active, collect from cosigners or estates, and report settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies.

nodes.title: § 3550.252 Debt settlement policies
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for handling real estate owned (REO) properties and debt settlement policies. It explains special procedures for selling or leasing REO properties to nonprofits or public bodies for transitional housing for the homeless, including temporary market withdrawal, possible price discounts, no down payment, long loan terms, and priority for eligible applicants. It also describes leasing properties at a nominal annual cost and RHS responsibilities for necessary repairs before leasing. In addition, the document outlines how REO property may be sold for affordable housing to very low- and low-income families.

The second major section addresses debt settlement policies, including when RHS may pursue collection actions for amounts owed, such as unpaid loan balances, subsidy recapture, grant amounts, and unauthorized assistance. It notes that RHS may seek judgments to improve collection, will not settle one loan while other loans on the same property remain active, may pursue cosigners and deceased borrowers’ estates, and will report settled debts to the IRS and credit reporting agencies.

nodes.title: (a) Applicability
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for disposing of real estate owned (REO) properties and for settling debts owed to RHS. It explains special arrangements for nonprofit organizations and public bodies to buy or lease REO properties for transitional housing for the homeless, including temporary market withdrawal, discounted pricing, no down payment, and low-cost leases with repair responsibilities divided between RHS and the lessee. It also covers special sale provisions for affordable housing for very low- and low-income families. The second major topic is debt settlement policy, describing when RHS may pursue settlement actions, seek judgments, handle multiple loans, collect from cosigners or estates, and report settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies.

nodes.title: (b) Judgment
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for handling and disposing of real estate owned (REO) properties and for settling debts. It also notes that REO properties may be sold under special terms to create affordable housing for very low- and low-income families. The second major section outlines RHS debt settlement policies, including when settlement procedures may be used, when RHS may seek judgments, restrictions on settling debts while related loans remain active, collection from cosigners and estates, and reporting settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies.

This excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for disposing of real estate owned (REO) properties and for settling debts owed to RHS. It also describes how RHS may temporarily withdraw a property from the market, set pricing and loan terms, require no down payment in certain cases, and give priority to eligible program applicants until a sales agreement is executed.

The second major topic is debt settlement policy. It explains when RHS may initiate debt collection or settlement actions, including recovery of unpaid loan balances, subsidy recapture, grant amounts, and unauthorized assistance. It also outlines RHS’s authority to seek judgments, its policy on not settling one loan while other loans on the same property remain active, its ability to collect from cosigners and estates, and its reporting obligations to the IRS and credit bureaus when debts are canceled, compromised, or adjusted.

nodes.title: (d) Cosigners and claims against estates
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules on disposing of real estate owned (REO) properties and on debt settlement policies.

Main points include:

- RHS responsibilities to repair leased properties to a decent, safe, and sanitary condition, while the lessee handles future repairs and maintenance.
- Special sale provisions for nonprofit or public bodies to provide affordable housing for very low- and low-income families.
- Debt settlement rules, including when RHS may pursue collection of unpaid balances, subsidy recapture, grants, or unauthorized assistance.
- RHS authority to seek judgments, limits on settling debts when other loans on the same security property remain active, and collection actions against cosigners or deceased borrowers’ estates.
- Reporting requirements for debts settled through cancellation, compromise, or adjustment to the IRS and credit reporting agencies.

nodes.title: (e) Reporting
nodes.summary: The partial document describes USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for handling real estate owned (REO) properties and debt settlement. It then outlines debt settlement policies, explaining when RHS may pursue collection actions, judgments, offsets, and application of escrow funds, as well as how debts involving cosigners or estates are handled and reported. Finally, it explains settlement by compromise or adjustment, defining those terms, when settlement offers may be considered, and conditions under which a debtor may retain the security property.

nodes.title: (f) Settlement during legal or investigative action
nodes.summary:
This section explains how RHS handles debt settlement and recovery actions, including when cases under investigation cannot be settled until the investigation or prosecution is complete. It also describes the use of offsets and the application of escrow or unapplied funds to reduce debt at liquidation.

The main focus is on settling debts by compromise or adjustment. A compromise allows a debtor to pay a lump sum less than the total owed to be released from liability, while an adjustment allows release through an initial payment plus additional payments over up to five years. RHS may accept these settlements only when they are in the Government’s best interest.

The section also sets timing rules for settlement offers, including when secured and unsecured debts may be considered, and explains that a debtor may keep the security property if the compromise payment meets the net recovery value and retaining the property benefits the Government.

nodes.title: (g) Offsets
nodes.summary: This section explains how debts may be settled by the Rural Housing Service (RHS) through compromise or adjustment, including when such settlements are allowed, how escrow and offset funds are applied, and restrictions on cases under investigation. It defines compromise and adjustment arrangements, states that offers may be made by either the debtor or RHS only when in the government’s best interest, outlines timing rules for secured and unsecured debts, and notes that a debtor may keep the security property if the compromise payment meets the net recovery value and retention is favorable to the government.

nodes.title: (h) Escrow funds
nodes.summary: This section explains when and how RHS may settle debts, including rules that delay settlement if a case is under investigation or referred for legal action, the use of offsets, and applying escrow or unapplied funds to the debt at liquidation. It then describes debt settlement by compromise or adjustment, defining each method, stating that offers may come from the debtor or RHS but must be in the government’s best interest, and outlining when settlement offers can be considered for secured and unsecured debts. It also covers when a debtor may keep the security property if the compromise payment meets the net recovery value and is favorable to the government.

nodes.title: § 3550.253 Settlement of a debt by compromise or adjustment

It explains that compromises involve accepting a lump sum less than the total owed, while adjustments allow an initial payment plus periodic payments over up to five years. The section also describes when settlement offers may be made for secured and unsecured debts and when a debtor may retain the security property if doing so is in the government’s best interest.

nodes.title: (a) Compromise
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains rules for settling debts under 7 CFR Part 3550, including when debt settlement is postponed because of investigations or legal actions, how offsets and escrow or unapplied funds are used to reduce debt, and how debt can be settled through compromise or adjustment. It defines a compromise as accepting a lump-sum payment for less than the total owed, and an adjustment as accepting an initial payment plus possible periodic payments over up to five years. It also describes when settlement offers may be made for secured and unsecured debts and when a debtor may retain the security property if doing so is in the government’s best interest and the payment meets the net recovery value.

nodes.title: (b) Adjustments
nodes.summary: The partial document describes rules for settling debts under 7 CFR Part 3550, including when settlement is postponed during legal or investigative actions, the use of offsets and escrow funds to collect debt, and the process for settling a debt by compromise or adjustment. It explains that compromises and adjustments may be initiated by either the debtor or RHS, are approved only when in the Government’s best interest, defines the difference between a compromise and an adjustment, sets timing requirements for when settlement offers may be considered, and allows the debtor to retain the security property in certain cases if the payment meets the net recovery value and government interests are protected.

nodes.title: (c) Timing of offers
nodes.summary: This section explains how debts under 7 CFR Part 3550 may be settled when collection is being pursued. It says debts under investigation or referred for legal/prosecutorial action cannot be settled until that action is finished, that RHS may use offsets to collect amounts owed, and that escrow or unapplied funds at liquidation must be applied to the debt. It then describes debt settlement by compromise or adjustment: a compromise is a lump-sum payment for less than the full debt, while an adjustment allows an initial payment plus periodic payments for up to five years, if doing so is in the Government’s best interest. The section also sets timing rules for when settlement offers may be made and allows a debtor to keep the security property if the compromise payment is at least the net recovery value and retention benefits the Government.

nodes.title: (1) For a settlement offer to be considered, secured debts must be fully matured under the terms of the debt instrument or must have been accelerated by RHS.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes rules for settling debts under 7 CFR Part 3550. It also outlines debt settlement by compromise or adjustment, including definitions of each, who may initiate offers, when settlement offers can be considered, and conditions under which a debtor may keep the security property if the settlement amount meets required recovery thresholds and serves the government’s best interest.

nodes.title: (2) Unsecured debts owed after the sale of the security property may be proposed for compromise or adjustment at any time. Debts that were never secured may be proposed for compromise or adjustment when they are due and payable.
nodes.summary: The document section explains how debt settlement works under 7 CFR Part 3550. It states that debts under legal or investigative review cannot be settled until the investigation or prosecution is finished, that RHS may use offsets to collect what is owed, and that escrow or unapplied funds are applied to the debt at liquidation. It then describes settlement by compromise or adjustment: both may be proposed by the debtor or RHS, but only if in the Government’s best interest. A compromise allows release of the debtor after a lump-sum payment less than the full amount owed, while an adjustment allows release after an initial payment and possibly additional payments over up to five years. The section also explains when settlement offers may be made for secured and unsecured debts, and when a debtor may keep the security property if the compromise payment is at least equal to the net recovery value and retention benefits the Government.

nodes.title: THE LOSS OF ASSISTANCE

- **Regular servicing**: Payment handling, fees, inspections, borrower obligations, payment subsidies, military duty assistance, approval requirements for borrower actions, refinancing with private credit, final payoff procedures, recapture rules, loan assumptions, and unauthorized assistance.
- **Special servicing**: Past-due account actions, payment assistance, delinquency workouts, protective advances, moratoriums, reamortization, offsets, and liquidation procedures.
- **Post-servicing actions**: Management and disposal of property after servicing, debt settlement policies, and compromise or adjustment of debts.

Overall, the document outlines the rules for administering, servicing, modifying, and resolving Rural Housing Service home loan and grant assistance.

nodes.title: § 3550.3 Civil rights

In addition, the document describes procedural protections such as written notice and appeal rights for adverse decisions, environmental review obligations, and compliance with related federal environmental regulations. It notes that state, local, and tribal laws may affect how the rules are applied, allows for limited demonstration programs to test new approaches, and gives RHS authority to grant exceptions when consistent with statute and in the government’s interest.

nodes.title: § 3550.4 Reviews and appeals

It notes that state, local, and tribal laws may affect how RHS implements certain provisions and that supplemental guidance may be issued when conflicts arise. The text also authorizes RHS to run limited demonstration programs to test new housing approaches, even if those programs differ from some normal regulatory requirements, as long as statutory requirements still apply. Finally, it describes exception authority, allowing RHS officials to request and obtain exceptions to certain requirements when consistent with law and when necessary to protect the Government’s interest.

nodes.title: § 3550.6 State law or state supplement
